title: Instagram Caption Generator for Products
promptText: Write 5 Instagram captions for [YOUR PRODUCT] targeting [TARGET AUDIENCE]. The captions should be [TONE] in tone and include a call-to-action to [DESIRED ACTION]. Include relevant hashtags for each caption. Vary the length — include at least one short punchy caption under 15 words and one longer storytelling caption.
description: Generate five on-brand Instagram captions with CTAs and hashtags for any product.
useCase: Use this when you need a batch of Instagram captions for a product launch, promotion, or ongoing content calendar. It works especially well when you have a clear audience and know the action you want followers to take. Run it weekly to keep your content queue full.

title: Re-Engagement Email Newsletter
promptText: Write a re-engagement email newsletter for [BUSINESS NAME] targeting subscribers who haven't opened an email in [TIME PERIOD]. The email should acknowledge the gap without being self-deprecating, offer something genuinely valuable like [INCENTIVE OR VALUE], and include a subject line, preview text, and full body copy. Brand tone: [BRAND TONE]. End with a soft CTA — either stay subscribed or unsubscribe — so only engaged readers remain.
description: Craft a compelling re-engagement email to win back inactive newsletter subscribers.
useCase: Use this when your email list has gone cold or when you're cleaning your list and want to give inactive subscribers one last chance before removing them. Sending this 2–3 times per year protects your deliverability and improves open rates across your entire list.

title: Facebook Ad Copy for Lead Generation
promptText: Write 3 variations of Facebook ad copy for [PRODUCT OR SERVICE] targeting [TARGET AUDIENCE]. Each variation should include a headline (under 40 characters), primary text (under 125 characters), and a description. The campaign goal is [CAMPAIGN GOAL] and the single most important benefit to highlight is [KEY BENEFIT]. Variation 1: problem-aware angle. Variation 2: desire/aspiration angle. Variation 3: social proof or urgency angle.
description: Generate three Facebook ad copy variations optimized for lead generation, each using a different psychological angle.
useCase: Use this when launching a paid social campaign and you need multiple ad variations to A/B test from day one. The three angles are designed to reach prospects at different stages of awareness. Start all three, pause the lowest CTR after 3 days, and double budget on the winner.

title: Personalized Cold Outreach Email
promptText: Write a cold outreach email to [PROSPECT NAME] at [COMPANY NAME]. They are a [JOB TITLE] in the [INDUSTRY] industry. I'm reaching out to introduce [YOUR PRODUCT OR SERVICE], which helps [IDEAL CUSTOMER PROFILE] achieve [DESIRED OUTCOME]. Include this specific detail about their company or work to personalize the opening: [PERSONALIZATION DETAIL]. Keep the entire email under 150 words. End with one low-friction CTA (e.g., a question or 15-minute call ask, not a demo request).
description: Write a short, highly personalized cold email built around a specific prospect detail that prompts a genuine reply.
useCase: Use this when prospecting new clients and you have done at least minimal research on the prospect. The personalization hook is the most important field — the more specific and relevant [PERSONALIZATION DETAIL] is, the higher your reply rate. Avoid generic compliments; reference something that shows you actually looked at their work.

title: Objection Handling Response Script
promptText: I sell [PRODUCT OR SERVICE] priced at [PRICE POINT]. A prospect just said: '[OBJECTION]'. Write 3 distinct responses to this objection. Each response should: (1) acknowledge the concern without being dismissive, (2) reframe the value or address the real underlying fear, and (3) move the conversation forward with a question or next step. Keep each response conversational, under 100 words, and avoid sounding scripted.
description: Generate three distinct, conversational responses to any specific sales objection.
useCase: Use this when preparing for a sales call, coaching your team, or when a recurring objection keeps killing deals. Run your 5 most common objections through this prompt to build a comprehensive objection handling playbook. The three variations give you options for different prospect personalities.

title: Discovery Call Question Bank
promptText: Create a discovery call question bank for a [TYPE OF BUSINESS] selling [PRODUCT OR SERVICE] to [IDEAL CLIENT PROFILE]. Generate 5 questions for each of these stages: (1) Understanding their current situation, (2) Identifying pain points and consequences, (3) Uncovering desired outcomes, (4) Understanding the decision-making process and stakeholders, (5) Budget, timeline, and urgency. All questions must be open-ended. Flag which 3 questions are the most critical if the call runs short.
description: Build a complete 25-question discovery call framework tailored to your product and buyer, with priority flags for short calls.
useCase: Use this before your next discovery call when you want to run a more structured, consultative conversation. Customize [IDEAL CLIENT PROFILE] as specifically as possible — the more defined your buyer, the sharper the questions. Print it out or paste it into your CRM as a call template.

title: SEO-Optimized Blog Post Outline
promptText: Create a detailed blog post outline for an article titled '[BLOG POST TITLE]' targeting the primary keyword '[TARGET KEYWORD]'. The target audience is [TARGET AUDIENCE] and the goal of the post is [CONTENT GOAL — e.g., drive signups, rank for keyword, build authority]. Structure the outline with: an attention-grabbing intro hook, 5–7 H2 sections with 2–3 H3 sub-points each, a key takeaway or stat to include per section, and a conclusion with a clear CTA. Also suggest 3 internal linking opportunities relevant to a blog in the [NICHE] space.
description: Generate a complete, SEO-structured blog outline with H2s, H3s, internal linking suggestions, and a content goal-aligned CTA.
useCase: Use this before writing any long-form blog content. A strong outline cuts writing time significantly and ensures the post covers the angles that both search engines and readers expect. Pair with the SEO Title and Meta Description prompt to complete your on-page optimization before publishing.

title: SEO Title and Meta Description Generator
promptText: Generate 5 SEO title tags and meta descriptions for a page about [PAGE TOPIC] targeting the keyword '[PRIMARY KEYWORD]'. The page is for [BUSINESS OR WEBSITE TYPE] and the target audience is [TARGET AUDIENCE]. Rules: each title must be under 60 characters and include the primary keyword; each meta description must be under 155 characters, include the keyword naturally, and give a clear reason to click. Number each pair and vary the angle (curiosity, benefit, urgency, question, how-to).
description: Create five click-worthy, character-compliant SEO title and meta description pairs for any webpage.
useCase: Use this when publishing new pages or auditing existing content for SEO improvements. The five variations make it easy to A/B test headlines. Validate your top choice against Google Search Console impressions data after 30 days to find what drives the highest CTR.

title: Podcast Episode Show Notes Writer
promptText: Write show notes for a podcast episode titled '[EPISODE TITLE]' from [PODCAST NAME]. The episode features [GUEST NAME], who is [GUEST BIO — ONE SENTENCE]. Key topics discussed: [LIST OF KEY TOPICS]. Episode length: [EPISODE LENGTH]. Produce: (1) a 2–3 sentence SEO-optimized episode summary, (2) 5 key takeaways in bullet form, (3) approximate timestamps for each topic, (4) 2–3 notable quotes from the episode, (5) a resources and links section. Write for both listeners and search engines.
description: Produce complete, SEO-optimized show notes from basic episode details and talking points.
useCase: Use this immediately after recording an episode while details are fresh. Provide accurate topics and rough timestamps for the most useful output. Consistent, detailed show notes improve podcast SEO, make episodes more shareable, and give audio content a longer shelf life through search discovery.

title: New Offer Brainstorm for Service Businesses
promptText: I run a [TYPE OF BUSINESS] that currently offers [CURRENT SERVICES]. My ideal client is [IDEAL CLIENT PROFILE] and their top 3 pain points are: [TOP 3 PAIN POINTS]. Brainstorm 10 new offer ideas spanning a price range from [LOW PRICE RANGE] to [HIGH PRICE RANGE]. For each idea include: offer name, format (course, coaching, done-for-you, productized service, etc.), delivery method, estimated time to build, and a one-sentence value proposition. Flag the 2 ideas with the fastest path to first revenue.
description: Generate 10 diverse offer ideas across multiple price points, each with format, delivery method, build time, and a value prop.
useCase: Use this during a quarterly business review or when revenue has plateaued and you're searching for new income streams. The more specific you are about your audience's pain points, the more targeted and actionable the output. Use the 'flag' instruction to help prioritize which idea to build first.

title: Business Idea Validation Framework
promptText: I have a business idea: [BUSINESS IDEA DESCRIPTION]. The target market is [TARGET MARKET] and the proposed price point is [PRICE POINT]. Act as a direct, critical business advisor — not a cheerleader. Evaluate this idea across 5 dimensions: (1) Market demand signals and how to verify them, (2) Competitive landscape and what's already winning, (3) Monetization viability and unit economics, (4) Key risks and red flags I might be ignoring, (5) The fastest possible path to generating the first $1,000. Give me your honest take, not a balanced pros-and-cons list.
description: Get a structured, critical evaluation of any business idea across five key dimensions from a direct-feedback perspective.
useCase: Use this before investing significant time or money into a new idea. The prompt is intentionally designed to surface problems and blind spots — run it when you're excited about an idea and want someone to stress-test it. Use the output as a go/no-go checklist before committing resources.

title: Professional Review Response Writer
promptText: Write a professional response to this [STAR RATING]-star review left for [BUSINESS NAME]: '[CUSTOMER REVIEW TEXT]'. If it's a negative review: acknowledge the specific issue, apologize sincerely, and offer a concrete resolution or next step. If it's a positive review: express genuine gratitude, reinforce one key differentiator, and invite them back. Keep the response under 100 words, match a [BRAND TONE] tone, and avoid generic filler phrases like 'We appreciate your feedback' or 'We're sorry you feel that way.'
description: Write a genuine, on-brand review response that handles both praise and complaints without sounding robotic.
useCase: Use this when managing your online reputation across Google, Yelp, TripAdvisor, or any review platform. Responding to every review — especially negative ones — signals to potential customers that you're attentive and trustworthy. Batch-process a week of reviews in one session to stay consistent without it eating your time.

title: Refund and Complaint Email Handler
promptText: A customer sent this complaint or refund request to [BUSINESS NAME]: '[CUSTOMER MESSAGE]'. My refund policy is: [REFUND POLICY]. Write a response email that: (1) opens with genuine empathy for their experience, (2) clearly states what I can and cannot do within my policy without hiding behind jargon, (3) offers [GOODWILL GESTURE — e.g., store credit, extension, or 'none'] as a goodwill gesture if appropriate, and (4) closes on a warm, forward-looking note. Keep it under 200 words, professional but human, and never defensive.
description: Draft a warm, policy-compliant response to any customer complaint or refund request that preserves the relationship.
useCase: Use this whenever you receive a difficult customer email you're unsure how to handle — especially when the request falls outside your policy and you need to say no gracefully. A well-handled complaint can turn a dissatisfied customer into a loyal one. Save your best outputs as templates for your team.

title: Standard Operating Procedure (SOP) Writer
promptText: Write a Standard Operating Procedure (SOP) for [TASK OR PROCESS NAME] at [COMPANY OR TEAM NAME]. The person following this SOP holds the role of [ROLE OR JOB TITLE] and this process is triggered when [TRIGGER CONDITION]. Required tools and access: [TOOLS NEEDED]. The SOP must include: (1) a one-paragraph purpose statement, (2) scope — what's in and out, (3) numbered step-by-step instructions written simply enough for a new hire, (4) quality checkpoints at key stages, and (5) a troubleshooting section for the 3 most common failure points. Use plain language throughout.
description: Create a complete, ready-to-delegate SOP for any repeatable business task — with quality checkpoints and a troubleshooting section.
useCase: Use this when onboarding new team members, systematizing a growing business, or preparing to delegate a task you currently do yourself. The best time to write an SOP is immediately after completing the task, while every step is still fresh. Build your operations playbook one SOP at a time.

title: Logo Concept Prompt for Midjourney
promptText: Minimalist logo for [BRAND NAME], a [BUSINESS TYPE] brand in the [INDUSTRY] space, [STYLE DIRECTION — e.g. geometric, hand-drawn, wordmark, lettermark], [COLOR PALETTE — e.g. deep navy and gold, black and white], conveying [CORE BRAND VALUE — e.g. trust, innovation, warmth], clean white background, professional, scalable vector style, no gradients, no shadows --style raw --v 6 --ar 1:1
description: Generate a Midjourney prompt for a professional logo concept tailored to your brand identity and style direction.
useCase: Use this when starting a brand identity project or exploring logo directions before working with a designer. Generate 4–8 variations by adjusting [STYLE DIRECTION] and [COLOR PALETTE] to find the visual language that best represents your brand. The best outputs can be used as direct creative briefs for a professional designer.

title: New Customer Onboarding Email
promptText: Write a new customer onboarding email for [BUSINESS NAME] sent immediately after someone purchases [PRODUCT OR SERVICE]. The customer's name will be personalized via merge tag as [FIRST NAME]. The email should: (1) make them feel confident they made the right decision, (2) tell them exactly what happens next in [NEXT STEPS — 2 to 3 actions], (3) introduce one quick win they can get right away, (4) share how to get support: [SUPPORT CHANNEL], and (5) end with a warm, human sign-off from [SENDER NAME AND TITLE]. Keep it under 250 words and avoid corporate-sounding language.
description: Write a warm, confidence-building onboarding email that sets new customers up for early success.
useCase: Use this when setting up your post-purchase email automation. A strong onboarding email reduces buyer's remorse, cuts support requests, and increases the chance of a customer getting their first result — which is the single biggest driver of retention and referrals. Set it to send within 5 minutes of purchase.

title: Product Launch Caption Series
promptText: Write a 5 post caption series announcing the launch of [YOUR PRODUCT] for [PLATFORM]. Post 1 teases the launch without revealing details, post 2 reveals the product and its main benefit, post 3 addresses the biggest objection [MAIN OBJECTION], post 4 shares social proof or an early result, post 5 creates urgency with [LAUNCH OFFER]. Each caption should sound like a solo founder speaking directly to [TARGET AUDIENCE], not a corporate brand.
description: Generate a complete 5 post launch sequence that builds anticipation and converts followers into buyers.
useCase: Use this when launching any new product, service, or offer. Instead of one launch post that disappears in the feed, you get a week long sequence that builds momentum. Works for Instagram, LinkedIn, Facebook, or X.

title: Batch a Full Week of Captions
promptText: Create 7 captions, one for each day of the week, for [BUSINESS TYPE] on [PLATFORM]. Mix the content types: 2 educational tips about [YOUR TOPIC], 2 personal or behind the scenes moments, 1 client result or testimonial style post, 1 engagement question, and 1 soft promotion of [YOUR OFFER]. Keep my voice [TONE] and each caption under 120 words. Label each with the day and content type.
description: Produce a full week of varied, ready to post captions in one batch session.
useCase: Use this every Sunday or Monday to batch your entire week of content in ten minutes. The built in content mix stops your feed from becoming all promotion or all tips, which keeps engagement steady.

title: Write Engagement Question Captions
promptText: Write 10 engagement question captions for [PLATFORM] that a [BUSINESS TYPE] could post to spark comments from [TARGET AUDIENCE]. Each question should relate to [YOUR TOPIC], be answerable in one short comment, and avoid yes or no phrasing. For each, add one line of context I can post above the question so it does not feel random.
description: Generate comment sparking questions that boost engagement and feed the algorithm.
useCase: Use this when your engagement is dipping or you need low effort posts between bigger content pieces. Question posts train your audience to comment, which raises the reach of everything else you post.

title: Turn Behind the Scenes Moments into Captions
promptText: I run [BUSINESS TYPE] and want to share behind the scenes content. Here is what happened recently: [DESCRIBE YOUR WEEK OR PROCESS]. Write 5 captions that turn these ordinary moments into engaging behind the scenes posts for [PLATFORM]. Each should feel authentic and unpolished, include one small lesson or insight, and end with a light question or invitation to respond. Keep my tone [TONE].
description: Convert ordinary work moments into authentic behind the scenes captions people connect with.
useCase: Use this when you feel like nothing interesting happened this week. Behind the scenes content consistently outperforms polished promotion for solo businesses because people buy from people. Feed it your mundane reality and get relatable posts back.

title: Write Your Founder Story Caption
promptText: Write a founder story caption for [PLATFORM] based on these facts: I started [YOUR BUSINESS] because [YOUR REASON]. Before this I was [YOUR BACKGROUND]. The hardest moment was [YOUR STRUGGLE]. Today [WHERE YOU ARE NOW]. Structure it with a scroll stopping first line, a short vulnerable middle, and a closing line that invites people to follow the journey. Under 200 words, tone [TONE], no corporate language.
description: Craft the personal origin story post that anchors your entire personal brand.
useCase: Use this for your pinned post, a reintroduction after gaining new followers, or your LinkedIn featured section. Origin stories are the highest converting personal brand content because they explain why anyone should care about you specifically.

title: Write a Reintroduction Post
promptText: Write a reintroduction caption for [PLATFORM]. I am [YOUR NAME], I help [TARGET AUDIENCE] achieve [MAIN OUTCOME] through [YOUR OFFER OR METHOD]. Include 3 quick facts about me: [FACT 1], [FACT 2], [FACT 3]. Open with a hook that stops both new and old followers, and close by telling people exactly what to expect if they follow me. Tone [TONE], under 150 words.
description: Reintroduce yourself to new followers with a clear positioning post.
useCase: Use this every 4 to 6 weeks or after any follower growth spike. Most of your audience at any moment has no idea who you are or what you sell, and a regular reintroduction quietly fixes that without feeling salesy.

title: Turn a Testimonial into a Caption
promptText: Here is a testimonial from my client: [PASTE TESTIMONIAL]. Turn it into a caption for [PLATFORM] that tells the client's before and after story without sounding boastful. Open with the client's starting problem, show the turning point, land on the result, then add one line about what made the difference. Close with a soft call to action for [YOUR OFFER]. Do not use the phrase 'I'm so proud' or exclamation marks.
description: Convert raw client testimonials into story driven social proof posts.
useCase: Use this every time you receive a good testimonial, review, or thank you message. A screenshot alone gets scrolled past, but a story built around the same words makes the result feel achievable to your audience.

title: Write Social Proof Captions Without Bragging
promptText: Write 5 caption options for [PLATFORM] sharing this win: [DESCRIBE YOUR WIN OR RESULT]. Each caption should share the result in a way that teaches the audience something useful rather than bragging. For each, use a different angle: the mistake I avoided, the unexpected lesson, what I would do differently, the boring habit behind it, and what this means for [TARGET AUDIENCE]. Tone [TONE].
description: Share wins and results in a way that builds authority instead of resentment.
useCase: Use this when something good happens in your business and you want to post about it without the humble brag cringe. Framing wins as lessons keeps the audience on your side while still establishing credibility.

title: Write a Before and After Caption
promptText: Write a before and after caption for [PLATFORM]. The before: [DESCRIBE BEFORE STATE]. The after: [DESCRIBE AFTER STATE]. The transformation took [TIMEFRAME] and the key change was [KEY CHANGE]. Structure the caption to make the before feel familiar to [TARGET AUDIENCE], make the after feel achievable, and position [YOUR OFFER] as the bridge without a hard sell. Under 150 words.
description: Frame any transformation, yours or a client's, as a compelling before and after story.
useCase: Use this for client results, your own business milestones, or even process improvements. Before and after framing is the most persuasive content structure on social media because the audience inserts themselves into the before.

title: Compress a Case Study into One Caption
promptText: Compress this client project into a single caption for [PLATFORM]: the client was [CLIENT DESCRIPTION], their problem was [PROBLEM], I did [WHAT YOU DID], the result was [RESULT WITH NUMBER IF POSSIBLE]. Write it as a mini case study with a hook line, 3 short beats of story, one concrete number, and a closing line inviting similar businesses to reach out. No jargon, tone [TONE].
description: Turn full client projects into scannable one caption case studies.
useCase: Use this after finishing any client project. Long case studies live on your website, but a compressed caption version reaches people who will never click a link, and it gives you a steady stream of proof content.

title: Write Educational Tip Captions
promptText: Write 5 educational captions for [PLATFORM] teaching [TARGET AUDIENCE] about [YOUR TOPIC]. Each caption should teach exactly one specific, actionable tip they can use today, open with a hook that names the problem the tip solves, and end with a one line summary. No fluff, no 'in today's fast paced world' openers. Tone [TONE], each under 130 words.
description: Create single tip teaching posts that position you as the expert worth following.
useCase: Use this to fill the educational slots in your content calendar. One tip per post beats listicles for saves and shares because the audience can actually remember and apply it. Batch 5 at a time and you have educational content for two weeks.

title: Write a Myth Busting Caption
promptText: Write 3 myth busting captions for [PLATFORM]. The myths in [YOUR INDUSTRY] that hurt [TARGET AUDIENCE] are: [MYTH 1], [MYTH 2], [MYTH 3]. For each caption, state the myth plainly, explain why people believe it, reveal what is actually true, and give one action the reader should take instead. Confident tone but not arrogant, no strawman arguments.
description: Challenge common industry myths to spark discussion and establish contrarian authority.
useCase: Use this when you keep seeing the same bad advice circulating in your industry. Myth busting posts get shared and debated in comments, and taking a clear position separates you from competitors who post safe generic tips.

title: Write a Common Mistakes Caption
promptText: Write a caption for [PLATFORM] about the top mistakes [TARGET AUDIENCE] make with [YOUR TOPIC]. Use these mistakes: [MISTAKE 1], [MISTAKE 2], [MISTAKE 3]. For each, give one line on the mistake, one line on the cost of making it, and one line on the fix. Open with a hook implying the reader is probably making at least one. Close by asking which one they recognize.
description: Turn common client mistakes into a high engagement warning post.
useCase: Use this monthly with fresh mistakes you notice in client work or industry conversations. Mistake posts perform because readers self diagnose as they scroll, and the comment prompt at the end reliably drives replies.

title: Write a Hot Take Caption
promptText: I have an unpopular opinion in my industry: [YOUR OPINION]. Write a hot take caption for [PLATFORM] that states this position clearly in the first line, backs it with my reasoning: [YOUR REASONING], acknowledges the strongest counterargument fairly, and ends by inviting disagreement in the comments. Keep it punchy and confident, under 120 words, no rage bait phrasing.
description: Package your contrarian opinions into discussion driving posts without crossing into rage bait.
useCase: Use this when you genuinely disagree with common industry practice. Honest hot takes are the fastest organic growth lever for personal brands because the comment debates push the post to new audiences. Only use opinions you can defend.

title: Create Poll and This or That Captions
promptText: Create 8 poll or this or that post ideas with captions for [PLATFORM] for a [BUSINESS TYPE]. Each should present two options related to [YOUR TOPIC] that [TARGET AUDIENCE] will have a genuine opinion about, with a short caption setting up the choice. Mix fun low stakes choices with ones that reveal something useful about my audience's preferences that I can use in my marketing.
description: Generate easy vote posts that boost engagement while quietly researching your audience.
useCase: Use this for low effort posting days and story content. The hidden value is audience research: which option wins tells you what your audience actually wants, which sharpens your offers and content without running a survey.

title: Write Urgency Captions That Do Not Feel Sleazy
promptText: Write 4 captions for [PLATFORM] promoting [YOUR OFFER] which ends or changes on [DEADLINE]. The urgency is real: [REASON FOR DEADLINE]. Write one caption for a week before, one for 3 days before, one for the final day, and one for the last hours. Each must state the deadline honestly, remind the reader of the core benefit, and address the hesitation [MAIN HESITATION]. No fake scarcity, no all caps, no countdown clock energy.
description: Create honest deadline driven promotion captions for the final days of any offer.
useCase: Use this for the closing window of any launch, discount, or enrollment period. Most sales happen at the deadline, but most solopreneurs go quiet in the final days because they fear being annoying. This gives you the exact posts for that window.

title: Write Soft Sell Captions
promptText: Write 5 soft sell captions for [PLATFORM] that mention [YOUR OFFER] without the post being about the offer. Each caption should lead with genuine value or story about [YOUR TOPIC], and reference the offer naturally in one line near the end, the way you would mention it to a friend. Vary the approaches: a client story, a lesson learned, a tip that relates to the offer, a day in the life moment, and answering a question I get asked a lot.
description: Weave your offer into value posts so you can sell daily without feeling salesy.
useCase: Use this for the 80 percent of your content that should not be direct promotion. Soft sells keep your offer visible in every post without triggering the audience's ad blindness, which is how solo brands sell consistently between launches.

title: Write Captions That Handle Objections
promptText: The most common objections to buying [YOUR OFFER] are: [OBJECTION 1], [OBJECTION 2], [OBJECTION 3]. Write one caption per objection for [PLATFORM]. Each should raise the objection in the reader's own words as the hook, validate why it is a reasonable concern, then reframe it with evidence, a story, or logic. End each with a low pressure next step. Never dismiss the objection or call it an excuse.
description: Address the real reasons people are not buying, one objection per post.
useCase: Use this in the middle of any launch or whenever sales conversations keep stalling on the same concern. Handling objections publicly means prospects arrive at your DMs already reassured, which shortens every sales conversation that follows.

title: Write a Milestone Celebration Caption
promptText: Write a milestone caption for [PLATFORM]. The milestone: [YOUR MILESTONE]. The journey context: [HOW LONG IT TOOK AND ONE STRUGGLE ALONG THE WAY]. Structure it to open with the milestone, rewind to the struggle so the audience feels the distance traveled, thank the audience for their part in it, and close with what is next. Grateful tone without being saccharine, under 150 words.
description: Celebrate business milestones in a way that includes your audience in the win.
useCase: Use this for follower milestones, business anniversaries, revenue goals, or client number milestones. Milestone posts humanize your brand and reliably pull high engagement because audiences love being part of a journey.
variables: HOW LONG IT TOOK AND ONE STRUGGLE ALONG THE WAY

title: Rewrite a Caption in Your Voice
promptText: Here are 3 captions I wrote myself that sound like me: [PASTE 3 OF YOUR OWN CAPTIONS]. Now rewrite this draft caption to match my voice exactly: [PASTE DRAFT CAPTION]. Match my sentence length, punctuation habits, level of formality, and the way I open and close posts. Do not make it more polished than my examples, match the roughness too.
description: Make any AI drafted caption sound authentically like you wrote it.
useCase: Use this as the final step on any AI generated caption before posting. Feeding it your real past captions as voice samples removes the generic AI polish that audiences increasingly recognize and scroll past.

title: Generate 10 Hooks for One Caption
promptText: Here is my caption for [PLATFORM]: [PASTE CAPTION]. Write 10 alternative first lines for it. Include: 2 that open with a bold claim, 2 that open with a specific number or result, 2 that open with a question, 2 that call out [TARGET AUDIENCE] directly, and 2 that start mid story. Each must be under 12 words and work as the visible line before the 'see more' cut.
description: Generate ten scroll stopping first lines for any caption you have already written.
useCase: Use this on every important post. The first line decides whether anyone reads the rest, and testing different hook styles over time teaches you exactly what stops your specific audience mid scroll.

title: Cut a Caption in Half Without Losing Its Punch
promptText: Cut this caption to roughly half its length while keeping the hook, the core message, and the call to action intact: [PASTE CAPTION]. Remove filler phrases, redundant sentences, and throat clearing. Keep my voice and any specific numbers or names. Show me the shortened version, then list what you cut and why in 2 or 3 bullet points so I learn the pattern.
description: Trim bloated captions down to their strongest half, with an explanation of the cuts.
useCase: Use this when a caption feels important but long. Shorter captions get read to the end, and the explanation of what got cut trains you over time to write tighter first drafts yourself.

title: Write 10 Call to Action Variations
promptText: Write 10 call to action closing lines for [PLATFORM] posts, all driving toward [DESIRED ACTION]. Include: 3 direct asks, 3 curiosity based versions, 2 that frame the action as helping the reader avoid a specific cost, and 2 ultra casual one liners. Each under 15 words. My audience is [TARGET AUDIENCE] and my tone is [TONE].
description: Build a reusable bank of CTA lines so your posts stop all ending the same way.
useCase: Use this once per quarter to refresh your CTA bank. Rotating your closing lines prevents audience blindness to your asks, and having them pre written means every batched caption gets a deliberate CTA instead of a default one.

title: Write Seasonal and Holiday Captions That Fit Your Brand
promptText: Write 5 captions for [HOLIDAY OR SEASON] for a [BUSINESS TYPE] on [PLATFORM]. Avoid generic holiday greetings. Instead, connect the occasion to [YOUR TOPIC] in a way that is genuinely useful or entertaining for [TARGET AUDIENCE]: a seasonal tip, a reflection prompt, a light tradition or confession from my business, a themed offer mention for [YOUR OFFER], and one purely fun post. Tone [TONE].
description: Create holiday content that stays on brand instead of defaulting to generic greetings.
useCase: Use this ahead of any holiday, season change, or awareness day relevant to your audience. Generic happy holidays posts get ignored, but occasion content tied to your actual expertise keeps you useful even on days when nobody is buying.

title: Write a 5 Email Welcome Sequence
promptText: Write a 5 email welcome sequence for new subscribers who joined my list via [LEAD MAGNET OR SIGNUP SOURCE]. My business: [BUSINESS TYPE]. My audience: [TARGET AUDIENCE]. Email 1 delivers the promised item and sets expectations, email 2 tells my origin story, email 3 shares my most useful piece of advice on [YOUR TOPIC], email 4 shares a client result or proof, email 5 introduces [YOUR OFFER] with a soft ask. Give each a subject line and preview text. Tone [TONE], each email under 300 words.
description: Build the automated welcome sequence that turns new subscribers into warm prospects.
useCase: Use this once when setting up your email list, then refine quarterly. The welcome sequence is the highest open rate email real estate you will ever have, and most solopreneurs waste it with a single confirmation email.

title: Turn One Idea into a Full Newsletter
promptText: Turn this one idea into a complete newsletter issue: [YOUR IDEA OR OBSERVATION]. My audience: [TARGET AUDIENCE]. Structure: a subject line and preview text, a short personal opening that connects the idea to something real from my week: [OPTIONAL PERSONAL CONTEXT], the main lesson expanded with one concrete example, one actionable takeaway the reader can apply today, and a closing line with a question to invite replies. Length 350 to 500 words, tone [TONE].
description: Expand a single thought or observation into a complete, ready to send newsletter issue.
useCase: Use this every week when you have a spark of an idea but no time to draft. One genuine observation from your work is enough raw material, and the reply question at the end steadily builds the sender reputation that keeps you out of spam.

title: Generate 15 Subject Lines for One Email
promptText: My email is about: [EMAIL TOPIC OR PASTE DRAFT]. Write 15 subject lines: 3 curiosity based, 3 with a specific number or result, 3 that name the reader's problem directly, 3 short punchy ones under 5 words, and 3 that sound like a personal note from a friend. For each, add matching preview text under 8 words. Audience: [TARGET AUDIENCE]. Avoid clickbait that the email body cannot pay off.
description: Generate a full spread of subject line styles with matching preview text for any email.
useCase: Use this on every send. Subject lines decide your open rate more than anything else, and generating 15 across different styles lets you A/B test and learn which style your specific list responds to over time.

title: Write a Story Driven Newsletter
promptText: Write a newsletter issue built around this story from my business or life: [DESCRIBE WHAT HAPPENED]. The lesson I want readers to take: [THE LESSON]. My audience: [TARGET AUDIENCE]. Open in the middle of the action with no preamble, tell the story in short paragraphs with sensory detail, pivot to the lesson naturally, then bridge to how it applies to the reader's situation. Subject line and preview text included. 400 to 550 words, tone [TONE].
description: Transform personal anecdotes into newsletters that readers actually finish.
useCase: Use this whenever something notable happens: a client interaction, a mistake, a small win, an awkward moment. Story emails get the highest read through and reply rates of any newsletter format, and they make your list feel like a personal letter, not a broadcast.

title: Write a Win Back Email for Cold Subscribers
promptText: Write 3 versions of a re engagement email for subscribers who have not opened my emails in [TIME PERIOD]. My business: [BUSINESS TYPE]. Version 1 is honest and direct about noticing their absence, version 2 leads with my single best resource on [YOUR TOPIC] as a gift, version 3 is a short breakup style email asking if they want to stay or go, with a one click way to remain. Each needs a subject line built to stop a cold subscriber. No guilt tripping.
description: Wake up cold subscribers or cleanly remove them, protecting your deliverability.
useCase: Use this quarterly on segments that stopped opening. Reviving even a fraction of cold subscribers is far cheaper than acquiring new ones, and removing the truly gone improves deliverability for everyone who remains.

title: Write a List Cleaning Goodbye Email
promptText: Write a final email to subscribers who ignored my re engagement attempts, telling them I am removing them from my list of [BUSINESS TYPE] in [NUMBER OF DAYS] days unless they click to stay. Keep it warm and respectful, one short paragraph, make the stay button action completely clear, and wish them well either way. Subject line should be honest about what this email is. No passive aggression, no last chance pressure tactics.
description: Send a respectful final notice before removing inactive subscribers from your list.
useCase: Use this as the last step of any re engagement campaign, a week or two after the win back attempts. A clean list opens better, costs less on most email platforms, and this email occasionally revives people the win back emails missed.

title: Write a Promotional Email That Does Not Feel Pushy
promptText: Write a promotional email for [YOUR OFFER] priced at [PRICE] going to my list of [TARGET AUDIENCE]. Lead with the specific problem it solves: [PROBLEM IT SOLVES], not the product. Include one line of proof: [PROOF POINT], address the main hesitation: [MAIN HESITATION], and make one clear ask. The reader should feel informed of an option, not cornered. Subject line and preview text included. Under 300 words, tone [TONE].
description: Promote your offer to your list in a way that informs rather than pressures.
useCase: Use this for any direct promotion outside a launch window. The problem first structure keeps promo emails useful to people who will not buy, which protects your open rates for future sends.

title: Write a 6 Email Launch Sequence
promptText: Write a 6 email launch sequence for [YOUR OFFER] launching to [TARGET AUDIENCE] with cart open from [OPEN DATE] to [CLOSE DATE]. Email 1 announces with the core promise, email 2 teaches something useful that reveals the gap the offer fills, email 3 shares proof: [PROOF OR TESTIMONIAL], email 4 handles the top objection: [MAIN OBJECTION], email 5 is a straight FAQ, email 6 is the honest final call. Subject lines and preview text for each. My tone: [TONE].
description: Generate the complete email sequence for an open and close cart launch.
useCase: Use this two weeks before any launch so you can edit and schedule everything in advance. Having the full arc written upfront prevents the mid launch panic writing that produces the desperate sounding emails subscribers unsubscribe over.

title: Write a Value First Sales Email
promptText: Write an email that teaches [SPECIFIC TIP OR MINI LESSON] to [TARGET AUDIENCE] and transitions naturally into mentioning [YOUR OFFER] as the deeper version of the same help. The teaching must be genuinely complete on its own, someone who never buys should still gain something usable. The pivot to the offer takes no more than 2 sentences. Subject line focused on the lesson, not the offer. 300 to 400 words, tone [TONE].
description: Teach something real, then bridge to your offer in two sentences or less.
useCase: Use this as your default sales email between launches. It keeps every send valuable, which trains subscribers to open your emails even when they know you sell things, the exact opposite of the list fatigue most solopreneurs create.

title: Plan 12 Weeks of Newsletter Topics
promptText: Plan 12 weekly newsletter topics for a [BUSINESS TYPE] writing to [TARGET AUDIENCE] about [YOUR TOPIC]. For each week give: the topic, the angle that makes it fresh, the email format (story, teaching, list, Q and A, behind the scenes, or curation), and a working subject line. Balance the mix: no more than 2 of the same format in a row, and weave in 3 soft mentions of [YOUR OFFER] spread across the quarter.
description: Map a full quarter of newsletter topics with formats and subject lines in one session.
useCase: Use this quarterly. The single biggest reason solopreneur newsletters die is the weekly blank page, and a pre planned topic list turns each week's email from an invention problem into a simple writing task.

title: Build a Curated Links Newsletter
promptText: Turn these links and resources into a curated newsletter issue: [PASTE LINKS OR RESOURCES WITH ONE LINE ON EACH]. My audience: [TARGET AUDIENCE]. For each item write 2 to 3 sentences: what it is, the single most useful insight from it, and who specifically should read it. Add a short personal intro connecting the theme of this batch, and a subject line teasing the best item. My commentary should add value beyond the links themselves. Tone [TONE].
description: Turn a handful of links into a curated issue where your commentary is the product.
useCase: Use this on weeks you have no original content. Curation done with real commentary positions you as the filter for your industry, and it is the fastest legitimate newsletter format, 20 minutes from links to scheduled send.
variables: PASTE LINKS OR RESOURCES WITH ONE LINE ON EACH

title: Write a Q and A Mailbag Newsletter
promptText: Write a Q and A newsletter issue answering these 3 questions I received from clients or followers: [QUESTION 1], [QUESTION 2], [QUESTION 3]. My business: [BUSINESS TYPE]. Answer each in 100 to 150 words with one concrete example or action step. Open with a line inviting readers to send their own questions for future issues, and close by picking the question you want more replies about. Subject line built around the most intriguing question.
description: Turn audience questions into a newsletter issue that generates more questions.
useCase: Use this monthly. Answering real questions guarantees relevance, the invitation loop generates content for future issues, and the replies it produces boost your deliverability more than any technical fix.

title: Write About a Failure Without Losing Authority
promptText: Write a newsletter about this failure or hard moment in my business: [DESCRIBE THE FAILURE]. What I learned: [THE LESSON]. What changed after: [WHAT CHANGED]. Write it honestly without fishing for sympathy, spending most of the words on the lesson and the change rather than the pain. The reader should finish trusting me more, not worrying about me. Subject line that is intriguing without being dramatic. 350 to 450 words.
description: Share business failures in a way that deepens trust instead of undermining it.
useCase: Use this sparingly, a few times a year, when something genuinely went wrong and you have real distance from it. Failure emails done well get more replies than any other format because they give readers permission to be honest back.

title: Turn Existing Content into a Newsletter
promptText: Turn this existing content into a newsletter issue: [PASTE BLOG POST, VIDEO SCRIPT, OR SOCIAL POST]. Do not summarize it. Instead, pull the single strongest idea, rewrite it for email with a more personal and direct voice, add one new example or angle not in the original, and end by linking to the full piece for readers who want more: [LINK]. Subject line and preview text included. Under 350 words, tone [TONE].
description: Repurpose blogs, videos, or posts into fresh feeling email issues, not lazy summaries.
useCase: Use this to get a second life from every piece of content you make. The added angle keeps it valuable for people who already saw the original, and the link drives traffic back to your site, which matters for an ad supported or SEO driven business.
variables: PASTE BLOG POST, VIDEO SCRIPT, OR SOCIAL POST

title: Nurture Subscribers Who Do Not Know You Yet
promptText: Write 3 nurture emails for subscribers who joined from [COLD SOURCE, E.G. A GIVEAWAY OR PAID AD] and barely know me. My business: [BUSINESS TYPE], my audience: [TARGET AUDIENCE]. Email 1 establishes what I stand for and against in [YOUR INDUSTRY], email 2 delivers my single most useful quick win on [YOUR TOPIC], email 3 shows who I have helped and how: [PROOF POINT]. No selling in any of them. Subject lines that work for people who do not recognize my name.
description: Warm up subscribers from cold sources before they ever see a pitch.
useCase: Use this whenever a giveaway, collaboration, or ad campaign adds people who never chose you specifically. Pitching cold subscribers burns them, but three warming emails first roughly doubles how many survive to become real audience.
variables: COLD SOURCE, E.G. A GIVEAWAY OR PAID AD

title: Write a Survey Email People Actually Answer
promptText: Write an email asking my list of [TARGET AUDIENCE] to answer a short survey about [WHAT YOU WANT TO LEARN]. Keep the email under 120 words, explain in one line why their answer shapes what I make next, state exactly how long it takes: [TIME TO COMPLETE], and offer [INCENTIVE, OR WRITE NONE] as thanks. Alternatively include a version where they just hit reply to answer one single question instead of clicking a survey link. Subject line for each version.
description: Get real survey responses from your list with a short, honest ask.
useCase: Use this before creating any new offer or planning next quarter's content. The reply based version consistently outperforms survey links for small lists, and the responses double as testimonials and copy research.

title: Write 10 PS Lines for Your Emails
promptText: Write 10 PS lines I can rotate at the bottom of my newsletters. My business: [BUSINESS TYPE], my current offer: [YOUR OFFER], my audience: [TARGET AUDIENCE]. Include: 3 soft offer mentions, 2 that point to my best free resource: [FREE RESOURCE], 2 that ask for a reply or referral, 2 personality one liners that match my tone: [TONE], and 1 that teases next week's issue. Each under 25 words.
description: Build a rotating bank of PS lines, the second most read line in every email.
useCase: Use this once and reuse the bank for months. The PS is prime real estate because skimmers jump straight to it, making it the ideal home for low pressure offer mentions that never interrupt the main content.

title: Announce a Change Without Losing Subscribers
promptText: Write an announcement email about this change in my business: [DESCRIBE THE CHANGE, E.G. PRICE INCREASE, REBRAND, NEW DIRECTION, PAUSED SERVICE]. My audience: [TARGET AUDIENCE]. Lead with what stays the same for them, explain the change and the honest reason: [REASON], spell out exactly what it means for existing subscribers or clients, and end with where to ask questions. No corporate spin, no burying the point. Subject line that says what the email is about.
description: Communicate business changes clearly while keeping subscriber trust intact.
useCase: Use this for price increases, pivots, rebrands, schedule changes, or discontinued offers. Changes announced plainly retain far more goodwill than ones subscribers discover on their own, and the structure prevents the defensive rambling these emails usually contain.
variables: DESCRIBE THE CHANGE, E.G. PRICE INCREASE, REBRAND, NEW DIRECTION, PAUSED SERVICE

title: Write a Monthly Roundup Email
promptText: Write a monthly roundup email covering: my best content this month: [LIST CONTENT PIECES], one business update: [UPDATE], one thing I learned: [LESSON], and one recommendation for [TARGET AUDIENCE]: [RECOMMENDATION]. Keep each section 2 to 4 sentences with a clear mini heading. Open with one sentence on the month's theme, close with what is coming next month. Subject line format: a hook, not the word roundup. Total under 400 words.
description: Compile the month into a scannable digest that keeps quiet subscribers connected.
useCase: Use this on the last week of every month, especially if you cannot sustain weekly sends. A reliable monthly digest is the minimum viable newsletter, enough to keep your list warm for the moments you have something to sell.

title: Write a Two Part Email with an Open Loop
promptText: Write a two part email series about [YOUR TOPIC OR STORY] for [TARGET AUDIENCE]. Part 1 tells the setup and stops at a genuine turning point, promising the resolution in tomorrow's email. Part 2 opens with a one line recap, delivers the resolution and the lesson, then connects it to [YOUR OFFER OR CALL TO ACTION]. The cliffhanger must be honest, no manufactured drama that part 2 cannot pay off. Subject lines for both parts, each email under 300 words.
description: Split a story across two emails to double opens with an honest cliffhanger.
useCase: Use this a few times per quarter for your strongest stories or before an offer drop. Open loops train subscribers to watch for your next email, but only work when part 2 genuinely delivers, so save it for material that earns it.
variables: YOUR OFFER OR CALL TO ACTION

title: Rewrite an Email in Your Voice
promptText: Here are 2 emails I wrote myself that sound like me: [PASTE 2 OF YOUR OWN EMAILS]. Rewrite this draft to match my voice exactly: [PASTE DRAFT EMAIL]. Match my greeting and sign off style, sentence rhythm, level of formality, and how directly I make asks. Keep all facts and links intact. Do not smooth out quirks that appear in both of my samples, those are my voice.
description: Strip the AI polish from any drafted email so it reads as unmistakably yours.
useCase: Use this as the last pass on any AI assisted email before sending. Subscribers joined for you, and voice consistency between your emails and everything else you write is what keeps the relationship feeling personal at scale.

title: Edit an Email Draft for Clarity and Punch
promptText: Edit this email draft: [PASTE DRAFT]. Cut it by at least 25 percent. Remove hedging words, redundant sentences, and any paragraph that delays the point. Make the first line earn the open, break any paragraph over 3 lines, and ensure there is exactly one call to action. Show the edited version first, then a 3 bullet summary of the biggest changes so I learn the pattern. Keep my voice, do not formalize it.
description: Cut a quarter of your draft and sharpen what remains, with the reasoning shown.
useCase: Use this on every email that matters before sending. Emails are skimmed on phones, and the built in explanation of the cuts gradually trains you to write tighter drafts, making this prompt slowly obsolete, which is the point.

title: Ask Subscribers to Share Your Newsletter
promptText: Write a short email asking my subscribers to forward my newsletter to one person who would benefit. My newsletter helps [TARGET AUDIENCE] with [YOUR TOPIC]. Make the ask specific: forward to one person, not share everywhere. Include a one line description they can send with it, and a line for new arrivals explaining where to subscribe: [SIGNUP LINK]. Under 120 words, warm tone, no begging. Subject line included.
description: Turn happy subscribers into a quiet referral engine with one specific ask.
useCase: Use this twice a year, ideally right after an issue that got strong replies. Forwarded newsletters convert to subscribers better than any social promotion because they arrive with a personal endorsement built in.

title: Write a Seasonal Email That Is Not Generic
promptText: Write a [HOLIDAY OR SEASON] email for my list of [TARGET AUDIENCE]. Skip the generic greetings. Instead connect the occasion to [YOUR TOPIC] with either: a reflection that is actually useful, a seasonal tip they can apply now, or a short story from my business this time of year: [OPTIONAL STORY OR CONTEXT]. If I have a seasonal offer, mention [YOUR OFFER, OR WRITE NONE] in one line only. Under 250 words, tone [TONE].
description: Send holiday emails with substance instead of interchangeable seasonal greetings.
useCase: Use this for any holiday or season change when you feel obligated to send something. An email with one real insight beats a happy holidays graphic, and inboxes are so full of generic greetings that substance stands out most on exactly these days.

title: Write Facebook Ad Primary Text Variations
promptText: Write 5 Facebook ad primary text variations for [YOUR OFFER] targeting [TARGET AUDIENCE]. The main benefit: [MAIN BENEFIT]. The offer or hook: [OFFER OR DISCOUNT]. Include: 1 short version under 50 words, 1 story led version, 1 problem agitation version, 1 social proof led version using [PROOF POINT], and 1 question led version. Each ends with a clear call to action to [DESIRED ACTION]. No clickbait, no fake urgency.
description: Generate five distinct Facebook ad angles from one offer so you can test what converts.
useCase: Use this when launching any Facebook or Instagram ad campaign. Testing 5 genuinely different angles, not 5 rewordings, is how you find the message that converts before scaling spend.

title: Write Google Search Ads Within Character Limits
promptText: Write a Google Search ad for [YOUR OFFER] targeting the keyword [TARGET KEYWORD]. Give me 8 headlines of 30 characters or fewer and 4 descriptions of 90 characters or fewer, showing character counts for each. Headlines should mix: the keyword itself, the main benefit [MAIN BENEFIT], a credibility point [CREDIBILITY POINT], and a call to action. Descriptions should each stand alone since Google mixes them. Match search intent, not brand fluff.
description: Produce Google Ads headlines and descriptions that fit the character limits exactly.
useCase: Use this when setting up Google Search campaigns. Responsive search ads need many asset variations, and having character counts verified upfront saves the tedious trimming that eats setup time.

title: Generate 20 Ad Headlines from One Benefit
promptText: Generate 20 ad headlines for [YOUR OFFER] whose core benefit is [MAIN BENEFIT] for [TARGET AUDIENCE]. Give me: 4 that lead with the outcome, 4 that lead with the pain point, 4 with a specific number or timeframe, 4 that challenge a common belief in [YOUR INDUSTRY], and 4 curiosity driven ones that still tell the truth. Keep each under 10 words. Mark your top 3 picks and say why.
description: Produce a wide spread of headline angles and get the strongest three flagged.
useCase: Use this at the start of any campaign, for any platform. The headline carries most of the ad's weight, and generating across 5 distinct styles surfaces angles you would not have thought of manually.

title: Find 10 Ad Angles for One Product
promptText: Find 10 distinct advertising angles for [YOUR OFFER] sold to [TARGET AUDIENCE]. For each angle give: the angle name, the core emotional driver (fear, aspiration, frustration, belonging, pride, relief), the opening line an ad using it would start with, and which audience segment it hits hardest. Base the angles on these product truths: [3 TO 5 FACTS ABOUT YOUR OFFER]. No angle should overlap more than 20 percent with another.
description: Map every distinct selling angle for your offer before writing a single ad.
useCase: Use this before any campaign or when a winning ad fatigues and performance drops. Most solopreneurs run one angle until it dies. Having 10 mapped means you rotate angles instead of scrambling when costs rise.

title: Write Retargeting Ads for Warm Audiences
promptText: Write 4 retargeting ads for people who [WHAT THEY DID, E.G. VISITED THE SALES PAGE OR ABANDONED CART] for [YOUR OFFER]. Ad 1 addresses the most likely hesitation: [MAIN HESITATION]. Ad 2 adds new proof they have not seen: [PROOF POINT]. Ad 3 answers the top question: [COMMON QUESTION]. Ad 4 is a straightforward reminder with [INCENTIVE, OR WRITE NONE]. Keep each under 80 words. Acknowledge familiarity without being creepy about it.
description: Re-engage people who almost bought with ads that address why they paused.
useCase: Use this for any retargeting campaign. Warm audiences do not need introduction, they need their specific hesitation resolved, and each ad here targets a different reason people stall before buying.
variables: WHAT THEY DID, E.G. VISITED THE SALES PAGE OR ABANDONED CART

title: Write a UGC Style Video Ad Script
promptText: Write a 30 to 45 second UGC style video ad script for [YOUR OFFER]. The speaker is a customer type: [CUSTOMER DESCRIPTION]. Structure: hook in the first 3 seconds naming the problem [PROBLEM], a quick personal context line, discovering the product, the specific result [RESULT], and a casual recommendation with [DESIRED ACTION]. Write it in spoken language with natural filler, not ad copy language. Include timestamp markers and one line of visual direction per beat.
description: Script an authentic sounding user generated content ad, beat by beat with visuals.
useCase: Use this for TikTok, Reels, or Facebook video ads, whether you film it yourself, hire a creator, or brief a customer. UGC style ads consistently outperform polished brand ads for small businesses because they read as recommendation, not advertising.

title: Write 15 Video Ad Opening Hooks
promptText: Write 15 opening hook lines for video ads about [YOUR OFFER] aimed at [TARGET AUDIENCE]. Each must work in the first 3 seconds before anyone decides to skip. Mix: direct callouts of the audience, surprising statements about [YOUR TOPIC], mid story openings, blunt questions, and pattern interrupts. Each under 12 words. Mark which 3 would work best as text overlay on screen versus spoken aloud.
description: Generate skip proof opening lines for video ads on any platform.
useCase: Use this whenever making video ads. The first 3 seconds decide everything on TikTok, Reels, and YouTube, and testing multiple hooks on the same body footage is the cheapest performance lever available.

title: Turn a Testimonial into Ad Copy
promptText: Turn this real testimonial into 3 ad variations: [PASTE TESTIMONIAL]. My offer: [YOUR OFFER], my audience: [TARGET AUDIENCE]. Version 1 leads with the strongest quote line as the hook. Version 2 tells the customer's before and after as a mini story. Version 3 stacks the key result against the common objection [MAIN OBJECTION]. Keep the customer's actual words where they are strong, paraphrase only where needed for flow. Each under 100 words with a call to action.
description: Convert customer testimonials into three ready to run ad variations.
useCase: Use this whenever you collect a strong testimonial. Customer language converts better than marketer language because it contains the exact phrases prospects use in their own heads.

title: Write Ads That Preempt the Top Objection
promptText: The top 3 objections to buying [YOUR OFFER] are: [OBJECTION 1], [OBJECTION 2], [OBJECTION 3]. Write one ad per objection for [PLATFORM]. Each ad raises the objection openly in the first line, in the prospect's own words, then resolves it with evidence, logic, or a guarantee: [GUARANTEE OR PROOF AVAILABLE]. Under 90 words each, ending with [DESIRED ACTION]. The tone should feel like honest conversation, not defensiveness.
description: Neutralize your biggest sales objections directly inside the ad itself.
useCase: Use this when ads get clicks but sales stall, a sign the objection lives between the click and the purchase. Naming the objection in the ad filters for readier buyers and raises conversion quality even if click rate dips.

title: Match Landing Page Copy to Your Ad
promptText: Here is my ad: [PASTE AD COPY]. Here is my current landing page headline and first section: [PASTE LANDING PAGE OPENING]. Rewrite the landing page opening so it continues the exact promise, language, and emotional register of the ad with zero disconnect. Keep the same key phrases the ad used. Flag any claim in the ad that the landing page fails to support, because that gap is where conversions die.
description: Eliminate the ad to landing page disconnect that silently kills conversion rates.
useCase: Use this every time you launch a new ad to an existing page. Message match between ad and landing page is one of the largest conversion factors in paid traffic, and it degrades quietly every time ads change but the page does not.

title: Check Ad Copy for Platform Policy Risks
promptText: Review this ad copy for policy risk before I submit it to [PLATFORM]: [PASTE AD COPY]. My industry: [YOUR INDUSTRY]. Flag: personal attributes callouts (you are struggling with X), unrealistic or guaranteed results claims, before and after implications, prohibited words for my industry, and anything likely to trigger review delays. For each flag, rewrite the line in a compliant way that keeps the persuasive intent. Note this is general guidance, not a guarantee of approval.
description: Catch the policy violations that get ads rejected before you submit them.
useCase: Use this before submitting ads in any sensitive category like health, finance, or coaching. Rejected ads delay campaigns and repeated rejections weaken account standing, so a two minute pre check is cheap insurance.

title: Write Local Ads for a Neighborhood Business
promptText: Write 4 local ads for [BUSINESS TYPE] in [CITY OR NEIGHBORHOOD] targeting people within [RADIUS OR AREA]. Use local anchors: nearby landmarks, neighborhood names, or local situations residents recognize. Ad 1 introduces the business to new locals, ad 2 promotes [CURRENT OFFER], ad 3 highlights what makes us different from the chains: [DIFFERENTIATOR], ad 4 targets a seasonal or timely local moment: [SEASONAL CONTEXT]. Under 80 words each.
description: Create geo targeted ads that feel like they come from a neighbor, not a corporation.
useCase: Use this for any local service business running Facebook or Google local campaigns. Local specificity in ad copy signals real community presence, which is the one advantage a small business has over every national competitor.

title: Write Ads for a Limited Time Offer
promptText: Write 4 ads promoting [YOUR OFFER] with this deal: [THE DEAL] ending [DEADLINE]. The reason for the deadline: [HONEST REASON]. Ad 1 for announcement, ad 2 for midway with proof: [PROOF POINT], ad 3 for the final 48 hours, ad 4 for the last day. Urgency must come from the real deadline, not manufactured panic. Each under 75 words with the deal and deadline stated plainly. Platform: [PLATFORM].
description: Build the full ad sequence for a time limited promotion, launch to last call.
useCase: Use this for seasonal sales, launch discounts, or enrollment windows. Staging the urgency across four phases captures both early planners and deadline buyers without running the same tired ad for two weeks.

title: Write Ads Positioning Against the Big Competitor
promptText: Write 3 ads positioning [YOUR OFFER] as the alternative for people frustrated with the typical solution in [YOUR INDUSTRY]. The common frustrations: [FRUSTRATION 1], [FRUSTRATION 2]. My genuine advantages: [YOUR ADVANTAGES]. Do not name any competitor. Frame it as 'if you are tired of X, this is built differently' rather than attacking. Each ad under 90 words targeting [TARGET AUDIENCE] with a call to action to [DESIRED ACTION].
description: Capture competitor switchers by naming their frustrations, not the competitor.
useCase: Use this when your best customers keep arriving frustrated with a bigger alternative. Switcher targeting converts well because the prospect already knows they have the problem, you just have to show you are built differently.

title: Write Ads for a Free Lead Magnet
promptText: Write 4 ads promoting my free [LEAD MAGNET TYPE]: [LEAD MAGNET NAME] which helps [TARGET AUDIENCE] achieve [SPECIFIC OUTCOME]. Ad 1 leads with the outcome, ad 2 with the frustration it removes, ad 3 with what is inside (list 3 specifics: [3 CONTENTS]), ad 4 with a bold claim the freebie backs up. Emphasize instant access and no cost without saying free so often it feels cheap. Under 70 words each.
description: Drive lead magnet downloads with ads that sell the outcome, not the format.
useCase: Use this for any list building campaign. Nobody wants another PDF, they want the result it promises, and these ads sell the transformation while the format stays a footnote.

title: Cut Ad Copy to Half Length
promptText: Cut this ad copy to half its length while keeping the hook, the core promise, and the call to action fully intact: [PASTE AD COPY]. Remove qualifiers, redundant benefits, and any sentence that repeats an earlier point in different words. Keep specific numbers and proof. Show the short version, then a version at one quarter length for placements with tight limits. Note anything important that had to be sacrificed in the quarter version.
description: Compress ad copy for tight placements without losing what makes it work.
useCase: Use this when adapting one master ad across placements with different length tolerances. Shorter ads usually outperform anyway, and cutting forced discipline reveals which lines were doing no work.

title: Refresh a Fatigued Winning Ad
promptText: This ad performed well but is fatiguing after [TIME RUNNING]: [PASTE WINNING AD]. Create 4 refreshes that preserve what worked: same core promise and audience, but change the surface. Version 1 swaps the hook style, version 2 changes the format (story to list, or statement to question), version 3 rotates in different proof: [ALTERNATIVE PROOF POINT], version 4 flips the perspective from the customer's view to mine or vice versa. Explain in one line what each version preserves from the original.
description: Extend a winning ad's life with variations that keep its core intact.
useCase: Use this when a profitable ad's costs start climbing from audience fatigue. The audience is tired of the surface, not the message, so refreshing the wrapper while keeping the promise routinely buys another month of performance.

title: Write LinkedIn Ads for a B2B Offer
promptText: Write 3 LinkedIn ads for [YOUR B2B OFFER] targeting [JOB TITLE OR ROLE] at [COMPANY TYPE]. The business problem it solves: [BUSINESS PROBLEM], the measurable outcome: [OUTCOME]. Ad 1 opens with the cost of the status quo, ad 2 with a contrarian take on how [YOUR INDUSTRY] handles this, ad 3 with a customer result: [PROOF POINT]. Professional but human, no corporate jargon, no 'in today's fast paced business environment'. Under 120 words each.
description: Create LinkedIn ads that speak to business buyers without drowning in jargon.
useCase: Use this for any B2B service, consulting, or software offer on LinkedIn. The platform's ads mostly sound identical, so plain human language aimed at a specific role stands out more there than anywhere else.

title: Write a Carousel Ad Card by Card
promptText: Write a [NUMBER OF CARDS] card carousel ad for [YOUR OFFER] on [PLATFORM]. Card 1 must hook with [HOOK ANGLE] and promise what the carousel delivers. Middle cards each carry exactly one idea: steps, benefits, myths, or results from this list: [YOUR CONTENT POINTS]. Final card is the call to action to [DESIRED ACTION]. For each card give the headline (under 8 words), the supporting line (under 15 words), and a one line visual suggestion.
description: Structure a swipe worthy carousel ad with one idea per card and a strong close.
useCase: Use this for Instagram and Facebook carousel campaigns. Carousels earn cheap engagement when each card pays off the swipe, and this structure prevents the common mistake of cramming three ideas onto card two.

title: Mine Customer Language for Ad Copy
promptText: Here is raw customer language from reviews, emails, or calls: [PASTE CUSTOMER QUOTES OR FEEDBACK]. Extract: the exact phrases they use to describe the problem before buying, the words they use for the result after, any vivid metaphors or emotional language worth stealing, and the objections hiding in their phrasing. Then draft 3 ad hooks built directly from their strongest phrases for [YOUR OFFER] targeting [TARGET AUDIENCE].
description: Turn raw customer feedback into ads written in your buyers' own words.
useCase: Use this quarterly on accumulated reviews, testimonials, and support emails. Ads built from real customer phrasing outperform marketer language because prospects recognize their own thoughts in the copy.

title: Write 12 Ad Call to Action Variations
promptText: Write 12 call to action lines for ads promoting [YOUR OFFER] where the next step is [NEXT STEP, E.G. BOOK A CALL OR START A TRIAL]. Include: 3 direct commands, 3 that state what happens immediately after clicking, 3 that reduce perceived risk using [RISK REDUCER, E.G. FREE TRIAL OR GUARANTEE], and 3 low pressure soft asks. Each under 10 words. Rank the top 3 for a cold audience and top 3 for a warm audience.
description: Build a tested bank of ad CTAs ranked for cold versus warm audiences.
useCase: Use this once per offer and reuse across campaigns. The CTA is the most copy paste neglected line in advertising, and matching CTA pressure to audience temperature measurably changes click quality.
variables: NEXT STEP, E.G. BOOK A CALL OR START A TRIAL
RISK REDUCER, E.G. FREE TRIAL OR GUARANTEE

title: Write Ads for a Seasonal Campaign
promptText: Write 3 ads tying [YOUR OFFER] to [SEASON, HOLIDAY, OR EVENT] for [TARGET AUDIENCE]. The seasonal connection must be genuine: [WHY THE SEASON MATTERS FOR YOUR OFFER]. Ad 1 uses the season's deadline energy, ad 2 the emotional register of the season, ad 3 a practical seasonal use case. Avoid cliches like 'tis the season and new year new you. Under 80 words each with [DESIRED ACTION] as the close.
description: Ride seasonal moments with ads that connect genuinely instead of pasting on holly.
useCase: Use this ahead of any relevant season, holiday, or industry event. Seasonal ads get cheaper attention because the moment does half the targeting, but only when the connection to your offer is real rather than decorative.
variables: WHY THE SEASON MATTERS FOR YOUR OFFER

title: Plan an Ad A/B Test That Actually Teaches You Something
promptText: Design an A/B test plan for my ads promoting [YOUR OFFER] with a budget of [BUDGET] over [TIMEFRAME]. My current best guess ad: [PASTE CURRENT AD]. Define: the single variable to test first and why it is the highest leverage one, the two versions to run, what stays identical, the metric that decides the winner, and the minimum results needed before calling it. Then list the next 3 tests in priority order after this one resolves.
description: Structure ad testing so each round produces a clear answer, not noise.
useCase: Use this before spending on any campaign. Most solopreneur ad testing changes five things at once and learns nothing, and a disciplined one variable sequence turns the same budget into compounding knowledge.

title: Write Display Banner Ad Copy
promptText: Write copy for display banner ads promoting [YOUR OFFER] to [TARGET AUDIENCE]. Banners get glanced at, not read, so give me 6 options each containing: a headline under 6 words, a supporting line under 10 words, and a button label under 4 words. Mix outcome led, problem led, and offer led versions using [MAIN BENEFIT] and [OFFER OR HOOK]. Every word must survive being seen for one second.
description: Write banner copy tight enough to land in a one second glance.
useCase: Use this for Google Display campaigns, sponsorship banners, or any placement where you get a glance, not attention. Banner copy fails by trying to say too much, and the strict word limits here force the discipline the format demands.

title: Define Your Brand Voice from Scratch
promptText: Help me define the brand voice for [YOUR BUSINESS], which helps [TARGET AUDIENCE] with [WHAT YOU DO]. Ask me nothing, work from this: the personality I want to project is [3 PERSONALITY TRAITS], and brands I admire are [BRANDS OR PEOPLE YOU ADMIRE]. Produce: 4 to 5 voice attributes each phrased as 'we are X but not Y', a short paragraph describing the overall voice, the 5 words we use often, and the 5 words we never use. Make it specific enough that a stranger could write in my voice from it.
description: Turn a vague sense of your brand personality into a concrete, usable voice definition.
useCase: Use this as the foundation before writing any other brand content. A defined voice with clear boundaries is what stops your marketing sounding like ten different people wrote it, and it makes every future AI prompt produce more consistent output.

title: Reverse Engineer Your Voice from Your Writing
promptText: Here are 3 to 5 samples of writing that sound like my brand at its best: [PASTE YOUR BEST WRITING SAMPLES]. Analyze them and extract a reusable voice guide: my typical sentence length and rhythm, my punctuation habits, my level of formality, the kinds of words and phrases I reach for, how I open and close, my humor style if any, and what I consistently avoid. Present it as a guide someone else could follow to write as me, with 3 example sentences you generated to prove the guide works.
description: Extract a documented voice guide from writing that already sounds like you.
useCase: Use this when you already write well but have never documented how. The output becomes a reference you paste into other prompts so every AI draft matches the voice you have proven works, instead of inventing one from adjectives.

title: Build Your Core Messaging Pillars
promptText: Help me build 3 to 4 messaging pillars for [YOUR BUSINESS] serving [TARGET AUDIENCE]. What I do: [WHAT YOU DO], what I believe about [YOUR INDUSTRY]: [YOUR BELIEFS], the main outcomes I deliver: [KEY OUTCOMES]. For each pillar give: a short name, the core message in one sentence, why it matters to my audience, and 2 example talking points that express it. These pillars should cover everything I ever need to communicate, so I never run out of on brand things to say.
description: Define the handful of core messages that everything you publish should reinforce.
useCase: Use this once to anchor your entire content strategy. Messaging pillars keep your marketing from drifting into random topics by giving you a fixed set of themes, which also makes content planning far faster.

title: Write Your Elevator Pitch Three Ways
promptText: Write my elevator pitch in 3 lengths. I help [TARGET AUDIENCE] achieve [MAIN OUTCOME] by [HOW YOU DO IT], and what makes me different is [DIFFERENTIATOR]. Give me: a one sentence version for introductions, a 30 second version for networking that includes a hook and a concrete result, and a 3 sentence version for my website or bio. All three must sound natural spoken aloud, avoid jargon, and lead with the outcome for my audience rather than my job title.
description: Produce three lengths of a clear, natural elevator pitch from one set of facts.
useCase: Use this before any networking event, podcast appearance, or bio update. Having all three lengths ready means you never fumble the what do you do question, and leading with the outcome makes people actually remember you.

title: Sharpen Your Value Proposition
promptText: Sharpen my value proposition. Current attempt: [YOUR CURRENT VALUE PROP OR WRITE NONE]. My offer: [YOUR OFFER], my audience: [TARGET AUDIENCE], the specific outcome I deliver: [OUTCOME], the alternatives they currently use: [ALTERNATIVES], and what I do better: [YOUR EDGE]. Give me 5 value proposition statements ranging from safe to bold. Each must name the audience, the outcome, and the differentiator in under 20 words. Flag which one is clearest and which is most memorable, and explain the tradeoff.
description: Refine your value proposition into a handful of clear, differentiated statements.
useCase: Use this for your homepage headline, sales page opener, or anywhere you have seconds to explain why you. A weak value prop makes everything downstream work harder, so this is worth getting right before writing more copy.
variables: YOUR CURRENT VALUE PROP OR WRITE NONE

title: Adapt Your Voice Across Different Contexts
promptText: My core brand voice is: [PASTE YOUR VOICE GUIDE OR DESCRIBE IT]. Show me how to flex this same voice across 5 contexts without losing its identity: a sales page, a support reply to a frustrated customer, a casual social post, a professional email to a potential partner, and an apology for a mistake. For each, write 2 to 3 example sentences and note what shifts (formality, warmth, directness) and what stays constant as the recognizable core.
description: Learn to flex one brand voice across contexts while keeping it recognizable.
useCase: Use this when your voice feels great on social but wrong in emails, or vice versa. A voice is not one rigid setting, it flexes with context, and seeing the same voice across five situations teaches you the constant that makes it yours.
variables: PASTE YOUR VOICE GUIDE OR DESCRIBE IT

title: Check Content Against Your Brand Voice
promptText: Here is my brand voice guide: [PASTE VOICE GUIDE OR DESCRIBE VOICE]. Here is a piece of content I drafted: [PASTE CONTENT]. Check it against my voice. Flag every line that sounds off brand and explain why, then rewrite those lines to match. Point out any word from my never use list, any tonal drift, and anywhere it sounds generic rather than like me. End with a one line verdict on how on brand the piece is overall.
description: Audit any draft against your voice guide and fix the off brand lines.
useCase: Use this on important content before publishing, especially anything drafted with AI or written when tired. Voice drift is subtle and cumulative, and a consistency check catches the lines that quietly make your brand sound like everyone else.
variables: PASTE VOICE GUIDE OR DESCRIBE VOICE

title: Reframe Your Message for Different Audience Segments
promptText: My core message is: [YOUR CORE MESSAGE OR VALUE PROP]. My offer: [YOUR OFFER]. I serve these distinct audience segments: [SEGMENT 1], [SEGMENT 2], [SEGMENT 3]. For each segment, reframe the same core message to lead with what that specific group cares about most, using the words and priorities native to them. Keep the underlying promise identical, only the emphasis and language change. Note the key priority difference driving each version.
description: Tailor one message to different audience segments without diluting the core promise.
