useCase: Use this when you serve several types of buyer with one offer. The same product solves different felt problems for different people, and leading with each segment's real priority converts far better than one generic message aimed at everyone.
variables: YOUR CORE MESSAGE OR VALUE PROP

title: Generate Taglines That Fit Your Brand
promptText: Generate 20 tagline options for [YOUR BUSINESS], which helps [TARGET AUDIENCE] with [WHAT YOU DO]. My brand personality: [PERSONALITY TRAITS], my key differentiator: [DIFFERENTIATOR]. Give me: 5 that state the benefit plainly, 5 that are clever or play on words, 5 that are bold or provocative, and 5 that are short and punchy under 4 words. Avoid empty words like innovative, quality, and solutions. Mark your 3 favorites and explain what makes each work.
description: Produce a range of tagline styles from plain to provocative, with picks explained.
useCase: Use this for a website header, logo lockup, or social bio. Twenty options across four styles gives you real range to react to, and reactions to specific taglines often clarify your positioning better than abstract strategy talk.

title: Write Your Brand Story
promptText: Write my brand story from these facts: I started [YOUR BUSINESS] because [ORIGIN REASON]. The problem I saw in [YOUR INDUSTRY] was [THE PROBLEM]. What I believe should be different: [YOUR BELIEF]. Who I help now: [TARGET AUDIENCE] and how: [HOW YOU HELP]. Write it in 3 versions: a 2 sentence version for a bio, a paragraph for an About page, and a fuller 200 word version for a story driven page. Make the audience the hero and me the guide, not the other way around.
description: Craft your brand story in three lengths with the customer as the hero.
useCase: Use this for your About page, which is often the most visited and least optimized page on a small business site. The three lengths cover bios, pages, and pitches, and the hero framing keeps it from becoming a self indulgent autobiography.

title: Write Your Bio in Every Length You Need
promptText: Write my professional bio in 4 lengths from these facts: I am [YOUR NAME], I help [TARGET AUDIENCE] with [WHAT YOU DO], my credibility markers are [CREDENTIALS OR RESULTS], and one human detail is [PERSONAL DETAIL]. Give me: a one line version for social bios, a 50 word version for guest posts, a 100 word version for speaker intros written in third person, and a first person version for my own website. Keep them consistent but suited to each context.
description: Generate consistent bios in every length from social to speaker intro.
useCase: Use this once and reuse for months. Being asked for a bio always happens under time pressure, and having four polished lengths ready, in both first and third person, removes a recurring small friction from your business.

title: Write an About Page That Converts
promptText: Write an About page for [YOUR BUSINESS]. My audience: [TARGET AUDIENCE], their problem: [THEIR PROBLEM], what I do: [WHAT YOU DO], why I do it: [YOUR WHY], my credibility: [CREDENTIALS OR RESULTS], and what I want readers to do next: [DESIRED ACTION]. Structure it to open with the reader's problem not my history, establish that I understand them, show why I am equipped to help, then guide them to the next step. Include a human moment so I do not read like a robot. Around 350 words.
description: Write an About page that serves the reader and drives a next step, not a resume.
useCase: Use this to fix the most common small business mistake: an About page that is all about you. Opening with the reader's problem and ending with a clear action turns a dead end page into one that actually moves people toward buying.

title: Pressure Test Your Brand Adjectives
promptText: I describe my brand as [YOUR 3 TO 5 BRAND ADJECTIVES]. Pressure test these. For each adjective, tell me: whether it actually differentiates me or whether every competitor claims it too, what it would look like in practice if I truly lived it, and a sharper or more specific word if the original is generic. Then show me the before and after of one piece of copy: [PASTE SHORT COPY SAMPLE] written to embody the weak adjectives versus the sharpened ones.
description: Find out which of your brand adjectives are generic filler and sharpen them.
useCase: Use this when your brand words feel safe but forgettable. Most small businesses pick the same adjectives (professional, trustworthy, innovative), and replacing generic claims with specific ones is one of the cheapest ways to sound distinct.

title: Build a Messaging Hierarchy for Your Page
promptText: Build a messaging hierarchy for my [PAGE TYPE, E.G. HOMEPAGE OR SALES PAGE]. My offer: [YOUR OFFER], audience: [TARGET AUDIENCE], main outcome: [MAIN OUTCOME], supporting benefits: [SUPPORTING BENEFITS], proof available: [PROOF POINTS], objections to handle: [OBJECTIONS]. Order everything by what the reader needs to hear first to keep scrolling: the primary message, secondary messages, supporting points, and where proof and objection handling should sit. Explain the logic of the order so I understand why each element earns its position.
description: Organize everything you need to say into the order that keeps readers scrolling.
useCase: Use this before writing or restructuring any key page. Knowing what to say is easy, ordering it so each section earns the next scroll is the hard part, and a deliberate hierarchy is what separates pages that convert from ones that lose people halfway.
variables: PAGE TYPE, E.G. HOMEPAGE OR SALES PAGE

title: Create a Brand Do and Do Not Guide
promptText: Create a one page brand do and do not guide for [YOUR BUSINESS] so anyone helping me stays on brand. My voice: [DESCRIBE VOICE OR PASTE GUIDE], my values: [YOUR VALUES], my audience: [TARGET AUDIENCE]. Produce paired do and do not rules across: word choice, tone, how we handle complaints, how we talk about competitors, formatting and emoji use, and claims we make. Make each rule concrete with a short example of the right and wrong way, so it is usable by a contractor or a tool, not just me.
description: Build a practical do and do not reference so anyone can create on brand content.
useCase: Use this when you start delegating content to a VA, freelancer, or AI tool. Clear paired rules with examples prevent the off brand output that happens when someone else guesses at your voice, and it makes onboarding help far faster.

title: Reposition Your Messaging Without Losing Existing Customers
promptText: I am shifting my brand from [OLD POSITIONING] to [NEW POSITIONING] because [REASON FOR SHIFT]. My existing audience: [CURRENT AUDIENCE], my target audience going forward: [NEW AUDIENCE]. Help me message this transition. Give me: the bridge narrative that connects old to new so existing customers feel brought along not abandoned, the 3 things to keep saying, the 3 things to stop saying, and a short announcement for my existing audience explaining the evolution without apologizing for growth.
description: Navigate a positioning shift while keeping the customers you already have.
useCase: Use this when your business is evolving beyond how you first positioned it. Repositioning done abruptly confuses loyal customers, and a deliberate bridge narrative lets you grow into a new market without alienating the base that got you here.

title: Create Signature Phrases for Your Brand
promptText: Help me create signature phrases I can repeat until they become associated with my brand. My philosophy on [YOUR TOPIC]: [YOUR CORE BELIEFS], my audience: [TARGET AUDIENCE], what I want to be known for: [WHAT YOU WANT TO BE KNOWN FOR]. Generate 10 short, repeatable phrases: some that frame my methodology, some that name a problem or enemy, and some that capture my belief in a memorable line. Each under 8 words. Mark which lend themselves to repetition across content.
description: Coin repeatable phrases that become shorthand for your ideas over time.
useCase: Use this to build the verbal signatures that make a personal brand sticky. Repeated phrases become mental shortcuts your audience uses to think about your niche, and owning a phrase is a durable positioning advantage competitors cannot copy easily.
variables: WHAT YOU WANT TO BE KNOWN FOR

title: Name and Frame Your Signature Method
promptText: Help me name and frame my signature method. My process for helping [TARGET AUDIENCE] get [OUTCOME] works like this: [DESCRIBE YOUR STEPS OR APPROACH]. Give me: 5 memorable names for the overall method, a way to structure it as a named framework with clear steps or stages, an acronym option if one fits naturally without forcing it, and a one sentence description I can use everywhere. The name should feel ownable and specific to me, not a generic industry term.
description: Turn your way of working into a named, ownable framework you can market.
useCase: Use this when you have a repeatable process but describe it differently every time. A named framework raises your perceived expertise, makes your approach easier to refer, and gives you an asset that justifies premium pricing.

title: Find the Gap in Competitor Messaging
promptText: Here is how my main competitors talk about themselves: [PASTE COMPETITOR TAGLINES, HEADLINES, OR ABOUT COPY]. My business: [YOUR BUSINESS] serving [TARGET AUDIENCE]. Analyze what they all emphasize and where they sound identical. Then identify: the messaging angle none of them own, the audience frustration none of them address, and the positioning space open for me to claim. Give me 3 possible positioning statements that plant me in that gap, each phrased so a customer would immediately feel the difference.
description: Spot the positioning space competitors leave open and claim it.
useCase: Use this when everyone in your market sounds the same, which is most markets. Competitors converging on identical messaging leaves gaps, and occupying the space they all ignore is easier than out shouting them on the ground they crowd.
variables: PASTE COMPETITOR TAGLINES, HEADLINES, OR ABOUT COPY

title: Simplify Messaging a Twelve Year Old Could Understand
promptText: Here is how I currently describe what I do: [PASTE YOUR CURRENT DESCRIPTION]. It is too complicated. Rewrite it so a smart twelve year old would understand it, removing all industry jargon, acronyms, and insider terms. Give me 3 versions at increasing levels of simplicity. Then tell me which jargon words I rely on that my audience may not actually understand, and what to say instead. The goal is clarity, not dumbing down my expertise.
description: Strip jargon from your messaging until anyone can instantly understand it.
useCase: Use this when prospects nod politely but do not seem to get what you do. Confused people do not buy, and the curse of knowledge makes experts systematically overestimate how clear they are, so a deliberate simplification pass reliably lifts conversion.

title: Add Emotional Resonance to Rational Messaging
promptText: My current messaging is accurate but flat and rational: [PASTE YOUR MESSAGING]. My audience: [TARGET AUDIENCE], the deeper emotional stakes of their problem: [WHAT IT REALLY MEANS TO THEM]. Rewrite it to keep every factual claim but add emotional resonance: name the frustration they feel, the fear behind the problem, or the pride behind the goal. Show me the emotional version alongside the rational one, and explain which specific words carry the feeling so I learn to do this myself.
description: Layer genuine emotion onto messaging that is correct but forgettable.
useCase: Use this when your copy is clear but nobody feels anything reading it. People decide emotionally and justify rationally, and adding the emotional layer to accurate messaging is often what turns a fine page into one that moves people to act.
variables: WHAT IT REALLY MEANS TO THEM

title: Articulate Brand Values You Will Actually Live
promptText: Help me articulate 3 to 5 real brand values for [YOUR BUSINESS]. What genuinely matters to me in how I run this: [WHAT MATTERS TO YOU], decisions I have made that reveal my priorities: [EXAMPLE DECISIONS], and what I refuse to do even for money: [YOUR LINES]. For each value give: a specific name (not generic words like integrity), what it means in one sentence, and one concrete way it shows up in how I operate. These must be values I can defend and demonstrate, not marketing decoration.
description: Define brand values grounded in real decisions, not generic wall art words.
useCase: Use this when you want stated values that hold up rather than the empty integrity and excellence every company claims. Values tied to real decisions and refusals attract aligned customers and make hard choices easier because you have already decided what you stand for.

title: Write Messaging That Filters the Wrong Clients Out
promptText: Help me write messaging that attracts my ideal clients and repels the wrong ones. My ideal client: [IDEAL CLIENT DESCRIPTION], the clients who drain me or are a bad fit: [BAD FIT DESCRIPTION], my offer: [YOUR OFFER]. Give me: messaging that speaks directly to the ideal client's mindset, honest statements about who I am not for, and how to phrase this so it feels like confident clarity rather than arrogance. Include one example of a this is for you versus this is not for you section.
description: Craft messaging that draws ideal clients in and screens bad fits out.
useCase: Use this when you keep attracting price shoppers or mismatched clients. Deliberately naming who you are not for filters your inbound so more of the people who reach out are ready to buy and pleasant to work with, which raises income and lowers stress at once.

title: Align Your Message Across Every Channel
promptText: Here is my messaging on different channels: website says [WEBSITE MESSAGE], social bio says [SOCIAL MESSAGE], how I describe myself in person is [SPOKEN MESSAGE]. My audience: [TARGET AUDIENCE], my true core offer: [CORE OFFER]. Find the inconsistencies and gaps where these send different signals. Then give me one aligned core message plus channel appropriate versions of it that all clearly point to the same business, so someone who finds me in three places sees one coherent brand.
description: Unify a message that currently says different things on different channels.
useCase: Use this when your website, socials, and pitch have drifted apart over time, which happens to every solo business. Inconsistent messaging across channels quietly erodes trust because prospects cannot tell what you actually do, and alignment makes every channel reinforce the others.

title: Build a Full Month Content Calendar
promptText: Build a 30 day content calendar for [BUSINESS TYPE] posting on [PLATFORM] to reach [TARGET AUDIENCE]. I can realistically post [POSTS PER WEEK] times a week. My topics: [YOUR TOPICS], my current goal: [GOAL, E.G. GROW AUDIENCE OR PROMOTE OFFER]. For each post give: the date, the content pillar, the specific topic, the format (story, tip, question, promo, behind the scenes), and a one line hook. Balance the mix so no more than one in five posts promotes [YOUR OFFER]. Present it as a table.
description: Generate a complete 30 day posting plan with topics, formats, and hooks.
useCase: Use this at the start of each month to end the daily what do I post scramble. A full calendar turns content from a recurring decision into a simple execution task, which is the single biggest factor in whether solopreneurs post consistently.
variables: GOAL, E.G. GROW AUDIENCE OR PROMOTE OFFER

title: Plan Your Content Week
promptText: Plan my content for one week. I run [BUSINESS TYPE], post on [PLATFORMS], and this week I want to focus on [WEEKLY FOCUS OR THEME]. My audience: [TARGET AUDIENCE]. Give me a day by day plan for [NUMBER OF POSTS] posts including: the platform, the format, the topic, the hook, and the call to action. Include one repurposing note per piece showing how to adapt it for a second platform so I get more from each idea. Keep it realistic for a solo operator.
description: Map a single week of posts across platforms with built in repurposing.
useCase: Use this every Sunday or Monday for tighter planning than a monthly view allows. The weekly cadence lets you respond to what happened recently, and the repurposing notes double your output without doubling the work.

title: Design Your Content Pillars
promptText: Design 4 to 5 content pillars for [BUSINESS TYPE] serving [TARGET AUDIENCE]. What I sell: [YOUR OFFER], my expertise areas: [EXPERTISE AREAS], what I want to be known for: [DESIRED REPUTATION]. For each pillar give: a name, what it covers, why my audience cares, the ratio it should take in my calendar, and 5 example post topics. Ensure the pillars together build authority, show personality, and support sales without every post being a pitch. Explain how they work as a system.
description: Define the recurring content themes that structure everything you post.
useCase: Use this once as the backbone of your content strategy. Pillars stop your feed from being random by giving every post a home, and they make monthly planning far faster because you are filling defined buckets instead of inventing from nothing.

title: Generate 50 Content Ideas at Once
promptText: Generate 50 content ideas for [BUSINESS TYPE] targeting [TARGET AUDIENCE] about [YOUR TOPIC]. Organize them into: 10 educational how to ideas, 10 that address common questions or objections, 10 personal or behind the scenes angles, 10 opinion or contrarian takes, and 10 that showcase results or proof. Each idea should be a specific post concept with a working title, not a vague theme. Mark the 5 with the highest engagement potential and the 5 most likely to drive sales.
description: Produce 50 specific, categorized content ideas in a single session.
useCase: Use this whenever the idea well runs dry, then bank the list. Fifty categorized ideas is weeks of content, and having them pre generated means your planning sessions become picking and scheduling rather than brainstorming under pressure.

title: Plan a Monthly Content Theme
promptText: Help me plan a themed content month for [BUSINESS TYPE]. The theme: [MONTHLY THEME, E.G. A PROBLEM YOU SOLVE OR A LAUNCH RUNUP]. My audience: [TARGET AUDIENCE], my goal for the month: [GOAL]. Design how the theme unfolds across 4 weeks so it builds rather than repeats: week 1 opens the theme, weeks 2 and 3 develop it from different angles, week 4 converts toward [DESIRED ACTION]. For each week give the sub focus and 3 post concepts. Show how the arc leads naturally to the goal.
description: Build a month long content arc around one theme that builds to a goal.
useCase: Use this when you have a launch coming or one big idea to establish. A themed month makes your content cumulative instead of scattered, so by the time you make an offer the audience has been primed by weeks of related content.
variables: MONTHLY THEME, E.G. A PROBLEM YOU SOLVE OR A LAUNCH RUNUP

title: Turn One Piece into a Week of Content
promptText: Here is one substantial piece of content: [PASTE BLOG POST, VIDEO TRANSCRIPT, OR LONG POST]. Break it into a week of smaller content across [PLATFORMS]. Extract: the core thesis for one anchor post, 3 to 5 individual points as standalone posts, one quotable line as a graphic, one question it raises for engagement, and one behind the scenes angle on why I made it. For each, give the format, platform, and hook. Show me how one idea becomes seven posts without feeling repetitive.
description: Atomize one long piece of content into a full week of varied posts.
useCase: Use this after publishing anything substantial: a blog post, podcast episode, or long video. Repurposing is how solo creators sustain output, and one good piece genuinely contains a week of content if you break it apart deliberately.
variables: PASTE BLOG POST, VIDEO TRANSCRIPT, OR LONG POST

title: Plan a Content Batching Day
promptText: Design a content batching session for me. I have [HOURS AVAILABLE] hours and need to produce [NUMBER OF POSTS] posts for [PLATFORMS] covering these topics: [YOUR TOPICS]. Give me: an order of operations that groups similar tasks together (all writing, then all editing, then all scheduling), realistic time blocks for each stage, which pieces to make first while energy is high, and where to place breaks. The goal is to produce a batch efficiently in one focused session rather than context switching all day.
description: Structure a single batching session to produce a batch of content efficiently.
useCase: Use this when you want to stop making content daily and switch to batching. Grouping similar tasks and sequencing by energy turns a scattered week of posting into one productive session, which is how solo operators reclaim their time.

title: Build an Evergreen Content Bank
promptText: Help me build a bank of evergreen content for [BUSINESS TYPE] that stays relevant year round for [TARGET AUDIENCE]. Based on my topics: [YOUR TOPICS] and offer: [YOUR OFFER], generate 20 evergreen post concepts that never expire: foundational how tos, timeless principles, recurring questions, and core beliefs. For each give a title and a note on when to re post it in rotation. Mark which ones can be lightly refreshed and reposted every few months without anyone minding.
description: Create a reusable bank of timeless posts you can rotate all year.
useCase: Use this to build a safety net of content that never goes stale. On weeks you have no capacity to create, an evergreen bank keeps you posting, and rotating proven pieces often outperforms scrambling for something new.

title: Plan Content Leading Up to a Launch
promptText: Plan the content runway for my launch of [YOUR OFFER] opening on [LAUNCH DATE]. My audience: [TARGET AUDIENCE], the problem it solves: [PROBLEM], the transformation it delivers: [OUTCOME]. Work backward from the launch date across [WEEKS BEFORE] weeks. Map what to seed each week: problem awareness first, then solution awareness, then proof and desire, then the offer. Give weekly focuses and 3 post concepts per week so that by launch day my audience is primed and warm, not surprised.
description: Build the pre launch content sequence that warms your audience before you sell.
useCase: Use this weeks before any launch. Launches fail when the offer arrives cold, and a deliberate runway that moves the audience from problem aware to ready to buy is the difference between a launch that converts and one that echoes.

title: Map a Quarterly Content Strategy
promptText: Help me map a content strategy for the next quarter for [BUSINESS TYPE]. My business goals this quarter: [QUARTERLY GOALS], my audience: [TARGET AUDIENCE], key dates or launches: [IMPORTANT DATES]. Give me: the overarching content focus for the quarter, how each month should build toward the goals, which months lean audience growth versus sales, and the content pillars to emphasize. Keep it high level enough to guide monthly planning without dictating every post. Explain how this quarter connects to sustainable long term growth.
description: Set a quarter long content direction that ties posting to business goals.
useCase: Use this at the start of each quarter to align content with what the business actually needs. Without a quarterly view, monthly plans drift and content stops serving revenue, and this keeps your posting connected to goals rather than just filling a calendar.

title: Plan Seasonal and Holiday Content
promptText: Map out seasonal content opportunities for [BUSINESS TYPE] serving [TARGET AUDIENCE] over the next [NUMBER OF MONTHS] months. Include: major holidays relevant to my audience, industry specific dates or seasons for [YOUR INDUSTRY], and natural annual moments (new year planning, back to school, year end review). For each, give the date, why it matters to my audience, and one content angle that connects it to [YOUR TOPIC] without being a generic holiday greeting. Flag the 3 highest value moments to prepare for early.
description: Identify the seasonal moments worth planning content around, months ahead.
useCase: Use this at the start of a season or year to plan ahead of the calendar. Seasonal content performs when prepared early rather than thrown together the day of, and this surfaces the industry specific dates generic holiday lists miss.

title: Find the Gaps in Your Content
promptText: Here is what I have been posting lately: [LIST YOUR RECENT CONTENT TOPICS AND FORMATS]. My business: [BUSINESS TYPE], my audience: [TARGET AUDIENCE], my offer: [YOUR OFFER]. Analyze the gaps: which stages of the buyer journey am I neglecting, which content pillars am I overusing or ignoring, which formats I never try, and which audience questions I have not addressed. Then give me 10 specific post ideas that fill the most important gaps. Prioritize gaps that are costing me sales or growth.
description: Audit your recent content to find the neglected topics and formats costing you.
useCase: Use this quarterly or when growth stalls. Most solo creators unconsciously post the same few things, and a gap analysis reveals the buyer journey stages and formats they are missing, which is often where the easiest wins hide.
variables: LIST YOUR RECENT CONTENT TOPICS AND FORMATS

title: Audit Old Content Worth Repurposing
promptText: Here is a list of my past content and how each performed: [LIST PAST CONTENT WITH ROUGH PERFORMANCE NOTES]. Help me find the pieces worth repurposing. Identify: my top performers worth remaking in new formats, evergreen pieces that could run again, older pieces that are outdated but salvageable with an update, and any topic that clearly resonates and deserves a follow up. For each, suggest the specific repurpose move. My platforms: [PLATFORMS], my current focus: [CURRENT FOCUS].
description: Mine your content archive for proven pieces worth reusing or updating.
useCase: Use this when you have a back catalog but keep making everything new. Your past hits already proved they resonate, and repurposing proven winners is lower risk and less effort than betting on untested fresh ideas.
variables: LIST PAST CONTENT WITH ROUGH PERFORMANCE NOTES

title: Adapt One Calendar Across Platforms
promptText: Here is my content plan built for [PRIMARY PLATFORM]: [PASTE YOUR PLAN OR TOPIC LIST]. Adapt it for these other platforms I also use: [OTHER PLATFORMS]. For each piece, show how it changes per platform in format, length, tone, and hook style, since the same idea should not be posted identically everywhere. Note which pieces are worth adapting and which are better skipped for a given platform. My audience differs slightly by platform: [ANY AUDIENCE DIFFERENCES].
description: Reshape one content plan to fit the native style of each platform.
useCase: Use this when you post to multiple platforms but do not have time to plan each separately. Cross posting identical content underperforms because each platform has its own norms, and adapting rather than duplicating gets far more from the same ideas.
variables: PASTE YOUR PLAN OR TOPIC LIST

title: Pre Write Hooks for Your Whole Calendar
promptText: Here are the post topics on my calendar this month: [LIST YOUR PLANNED TOPICS]. For each topic, write 3 hook options in different styles: one curiosity based, one that names the pain, and one bold statement. My audience: [TARGET AUDIENCE], my platform: [PLATFORM]. Keep each hook under 12 words and scroll stopping. Present them grouped by topic so I can pick the strongest hook for each post when I sit down to write. Mark your single favorite per topic.
description: Generate multiple hook options for every planned post in one batch.
useCase: Use this after building your calendar but before writing. Pre writing hooks removes the hardest part of each post in advance, so when you sit down to create, the scroll stopping opener is already chosen and you just fill in the rest.

title: Schedule Engagement Posts Into Your Calendar
promptText: My content calendar is heavy on educational and promotional posts. Help me weave in engagement posts to keep my audience active. My business: [BUSINESS TYPE], audience: [TARGET AUDIENCE], topic: [YOUR TOPIC]. Give me 15 engagement post ideas (questions, polls, fill in the blanks, hot takes, this or that, ask me anything prompts) and recommend how often to space them into a calendar between heavier content. Explain why engagement posts between value posts lift the reach of everything else.
description: Add reach boosting engagement posts to a calendar that is all teaching and selling.
useCase: Use this when your content is valuable but engagement is flat. Educational posts inform but do not always spark interaction, and spacing engagement posts between them trains the algorithm to show your heavier content to more people.

title: Build a Minimum Viable Content Calendar
promptText: I am overwhelmed and can only commit to the bare minimum content that still moves my business. I run [BUSINESS TYPE] for [TARGET AUDIENCE] and can realistically manage [REALISTIC TIME PER WEEK] per week. Design the smallest sustainable content plan that still builds audience and supports [YOUR OFFER]. Tell me: exactly how many posts and where, which one format to master first, and what to ignore for now. Prioritize consistency over volume. The plan must survive a busy week without collapsing.
description: Design the smallest content plan that still works when you are stretched thin.
useCase: Use this when a full calendar feels impossible and you are close to quitting content entirely. A sustainable minimum beats an ambitious plan you abandon, and consistency at low volume outperforms sporadic bursts for long term growth.

title: Design a Recurring Content Series
promptText: Help me design a recurring content series for [BUSINESS TYPE] that my audience will look forward to. My topic: [YOUR TOPIC], audience: [TARGET AUDIENCE], the cadence I can sustain: [CADENCE, E.G. WEEKLY OR MONTHLY]. Give me 3 series concepts, each with: a memorable series name, the repeatable format, what makes each installment different, and why people would return for it. Pick the strongest and outline the first 6 installments. A good series should be easy for me to produce and easy for my audience to anticipate.
description: Create a named recurring series your audience anticipates and you can sustain.
useCase: Use this to build appointment content that trains your audience to expect you. Recurring series lower your creative load because the format is fixed, and they build the habit loop that turns passive followers into a returning audience.

title: Fit Trending Topics Into Your Calendar
promptText: A topic is trending in my space: [THE TRENDING TOPIC]. My business: [BUSINESS TYPE], my audience: [TARGET AUDIENCE], my expertise: [YOUR EXPERTISE]. Help me decide if and how to jump on it. Give me: an honest take on whether this trend fits my brand or is a distraction, 3 angles to cover it that connect to my actual expertise, how to add a perspective rather than just repeating the trend, and how to slot it into my existing calendar without derailing my planned content. Skip it if the fit is weak.
description: Decide whether to ride a trend and how to add real perspective if you do.
useCase: Use this when something blows up in your niche and you feel pressure to react. Chasing every trend dilutes a brand, and this helps you judge fit honestly and, when it does fit, contribute a real angle instead of adding to the noise.

title: Reverse Engineer a Calendar from Your Offer
promptText: Build a content calendar that sells [YOUR OFFER] without being pushy. The offer solves [PROBLEM] for [TARGET AUDIENCE] and delivers [OUTCOME]. Work backward from the sale: map the beliefs my audience must hold before they will buy, then design content that builds each belief in sequence over [TIMEFRAME]. For each week give the belief being built and 2 to 3 post concepts that build it. The audience should feel educated and understood, then find the offer an obvious fit rather than a hard pitch.
description: Design content that installs the beliefs a buyer needs, then presents your offer.
useCase: Use this when your content gets engagement but does not lead to sales. The gap is usually missing beliefs, and reverse engineering the calendar from the sale ensures every post moves the audience closer to seeing your offer as the obvious next step.

title: Plan a Calendar Around One Format Engine
promptText: I want a content system built around one core format I create each week that feeds everything else. My strongest format is [YOUR CORE FORMAT, E.G. A NEWSLETTER, VIDEO, OR PODCAST]. My platforms: [PLATFORMS], audience: [TARGET AUDIENCE]. Design a weekly system where I create that one anchor piece, then a clear repurposing chain turns it into posts for every other platform. Show the exact chain: anchor to what, then to what. The goal is one real creative act per week that generates a full week of presence everywhere.
description: Build a content system where one weekly anchor piece feeds every platform.
useCase: Use this to escape the trap of creating separately for every platform. A single anchor format with a repurposing chain is how solo creators maintain a presence everywhere from one weekly effort, which is the only sustainable model for one person.
variables: YOUR CORE FORMAT, E.G. A NEWSLETTER, VIDEO, OR PODCAST

title: Run a Monthly Content Review
promptText: Help me run a monthly content review. Here is what I posted and rough results: [PASTE YOUR POSTS AND PERFORMANCE NOTES]. My goal was [MONTHLY GOAL]. Analyze: what performed best and the likely why, what flopped and the lesson, which content pillar or format is pulling weight, and whether my mix served my goal. Then give me 3 specific changes to make next month based on the evidence, not guesses. Keep it honest, I want to learn what works, not be reassured.
description: Turn last month's content results into concrete changes for next month.
useCase: Use this on the last day of each month before planning the next. Posting without reviewing means repeating mistakes, and a monthly evidence based review is how your content compounds in effectiveness instead of just accumulating.
variables: PASTE YOUR POSTS AND PERFORMANCE NOTES

title: Set Up a Content Idea Capture System
promptText: Help me set up a simple system to capture content ideas as they occur so I never face a blank calendar. I run [BUSINESS TYPE] and the moments ideas hit me are [WHEN IDEAS OCCUR, E.G. CLIENT CALLS OR SHOWER THOUGHTS]. Give me: a lightweight capture method that fits my workflow, prompts and triggers that reliably surface ideas from my daily work, a simple way to organize captured ideas by pillar or format, and a weekly ritual to turn raw captures into scheduled posts. Keep it low friction, complex systems get abandoned.
description: Build a low friction system that captures content ideas from your daily work.
useCase: Use this once to solve the root cause of content droughts. The best ideas come from real client work and daily moments, then vanish, and a simple capture habit turns your everyday business into an endless content pipeline.
variables: WHEN IDEAS OCCUR, E.G. CLIENT CALLS OR SHOWER THOUGHTS

title: Plan Your First 30 Days of Content
promptText: I am starting content for [BUSINESS TYPE] from near zero on [PLATFORM]. My audience: [TARGET AUDIENCE], what I offer: [YOUR OFFER], my expertise: [YOUR EXPERTISE]. Design my first 30 days with the goals of establishing who I am, what I stand for, and what I help with. Include: the essential foundational posts to publish first (intro, origin story, what I do, who I help), a realistic cadence for a beginner, and how to build early momentum without an existing audience. Prioritize clarity and consistency over cleverness.
description: Map the first month of content for a brand new account starting from zero.
useCase: Use this when launching a new brand or finally getting serious about a dormant account. The first month should establish your identity and positioning clearly, and starting with foundational posts beats jumping into tactics before anyone knows who you are.

title: Write a Reel Script with a Scroll Stopping Hook
promptText: Write a 30 to 45 second Reel script for [BUSINESS TYPE] about [TOPIC] aimed at [TARGET AUDIENCE]. Open with a hook in the first 2 seconds that stops the scroll, deliver 3 quick value points on the topic, and close with a call to action to [DESIRED ACTION]. Format it with timestamps, spoken lines, and one line of on screen text per beat. Write it in natural spoken language, not written prose. My tone is [TONE].
description: Generate a full Reel script with hook, value beats, on screen text, and a CTA.
useCase: Use this whenever you sit down to make short form video and freeze at the blank page. The timestamped structure means you can film straight from the script, and the front loaded hook is what determines whether anyone watches past the first second.

title: Write 15 Hooks for a Short Video
promptText: My short video is about [VIDEO TOPIC] for [TARGET AUDIENCE]. Write 15 opening hooks that work in the first 2 seconds. Mix: direct callouts of the audience, surprising claims about [TOPIC], mid action openings, blunt questions, and pattern interrupts. Each under 10 words. For each, note whether it works better spoken aloud or as on screen text. Mark the 3 most likely to stop the scroll and explain why they work.
description: Generate fifteen tested opening hooks so you can pick the strongest before filming.
useCase: Use this before filming any short form video. The hook carries most of the performance, and testing multiple hooks on the same footage is the cheapest way to learn what stops your specific audience mid scroll.

title: Write a Talking Head Value Video
promptText: Write a talking head video script teaching [TARGET AUDIENCE] one specific thing about [TOPIC]. Length around [TARGET LENGTH]. Structure: a hook naming the problem the tip solves, a promise of what they will learn, the teaching delivered in clear steps, one example, and a close that invites [DESIRED ACTION]. Write it conversational as if talking to one person. Add brief delivery notes where a pause or emphasis matters. My tone is [TONE].
description: Script a single tip talking head video that teaches one thing clearly.
useCase: Use this for the educational backbone of your video content. One clear tip per video beats cramming many because the viewer can actually remember and apply it, and the conversational script keeps you from sounding like you are reading.

title: Write a Numbered List Reel Script
promptText: Write a fast paced list style Reel script: [NUMBER] tips, tools, or mistakes about [TOPIC] for [TARGET AUDIENCE]. Open with a hook that promises the full list and a reason to watch to the end. Deliver each item in one or two punchy lines with on screen text for the number and keyword. Keep the pace quick so retention stays high. Close with a CTA to [DESIRED ACTION]. Include timestamps and on screen text cues.
description: Script a rapid numbered list Reel built for high retention.
useCase: Use this for saveable, shareable short form content. List videos perform because the promise of a full list pulls viewers to the end, and the number in the hook sets a clear expectation that drives completion, which lifts reach.

title: Write a Myth Busting Video Script
promptText: Write a short video script busting a common myth in [YOUR INDUSTRY] that hurts [TARGET AUDIENCE]. The myth: [THE MYTH]. The truth: [THE TRUTH]. Open by stating the myth plainly so believers feel called out, explain why people believe it, reveal the truth with reasoning or evidence, and give one action to take instead. Confident but not arrogant. Around [TARGET LENGTH]. Include the hook, spoken lines, and on screen text for the key turn.
description: Script a myth versus truth video that sparks debate and builds authority.
useCase: Use this when you keep seeing the same bad advice in your niche. Myth busting videos get shared and argued in comments, and taking a clear contrarian position separates you from creators who only post safe generic tips.

title: Write a Hot Take Video Script
promptText: I have an unpopular opinion in my field: [YOUR OPINION]. Write a short video script that states it clearly in the first line, backs it with my reasoning: [YOUR REASONING], fairly acknowledges the strongest counterpoint, and invites disagreement in the comments. Keep it punchy and confident, around [TARGET LENGTH], no rage bait. Write it for [TARGET AUDIENCE] in spoken language. Include the hook and any on screen text that reinforces the position.
description: Script a confident hot take video that drives discussion without rage baiting.
useCase: Use this when you genuinely disagree with common practice in your space. Honest hot takes are one of the fastest organic growth levers because the comment debate pushes the video to new audiences, but only use opinions you can defend.

title: Write a Story Time Video Script
promptText: Turn this experience into a story time video script: [DESCRIBE WHAT HAPPENED]. The lesson for [TARGET AUDIENCE]: [THE LESSON]. Open in the middle of the action with no setup, tell the story in short spoken beats that keep tension, land the lesson naturally, and connect it to the viewer's situation. Around [TARGET LENGTH]. Write in natural spoken voice with delivery notes for the key emotional beats. My tone is [TONE].
description: Turn a real experience into a story time video that holds attention to the lesson.
useCase: Use this whenever something notable happens in your work or life that carries a lesson. Story videos get the highest completion and share rates in short form because narrative tension holds attention in a way tips cannot.

title: Adapt a Trend to Your Niche
promptText: A trend or format is popular right now: [DESCRIBE THE TREND OR FORMAT]. My business: [BUSINESS TYPE], my audience: [TARGET AUDIENCE], my topic: [YOUR TOPIC]. Adapt the trend to my niche in a way that fits my brand and delivers real value, not a forced copy. Give me 3 script concepts using the trend's structure but my expertise, each with a hook and the beats. Flag if the trend is a poor fit for my brand and I should skip it.
description: Adapt a trending format to your niche with substance instead of copying it.
useCase: Use this when a format is blowing up and you want in without looking like a bandwagon jumper. Adapting the structure to your expertise rides the trend's reach while still saying something only you could say.

title: Write a Product Demo Video Script
promptText: Write a short demo video script for [YOUR PRODUCT OR SERVICE] showing [TARGET AUDIENCE] how it solves [PROBLEM]. Open with the problem or the end result to earn attention, show the product in action across the key moments, highlight the one feature that matters most: [KEY FEATURE], and close with [DESIRED ACTION]. Keep it benefit led, not feature dump. Around [TARGET LENGTH]. Include spoken lines, on screen text, and a note on what to show visually at each beat.
description: Script a demo video that leads with the result and shows the product solving it.
useCase: Use this for showing an offer in action on social or a sales page. Demos fail when they list features, and this structure keeps the focus on the problem being solved so the viewer sees themselves in the result.

title: Script a Customer Testimonial Video
promptText: Help me script a customer testimonial video for [YOUR OFFER]. My customer's situation before: [BEFORE STATE], the result they got: [RESULT], and what made the difference: [KEY DIFFERENCE]. Write a set of interview questions that will draw out a natural, story driven testimonial covering their before, the turning point, and the after. Then give a suggested edit structure for the final video. The goal is authentic and specific, not a scripted endorsement the customer reads.
description: Script the interview questions and edit structure for an authentic testimonial video.
useCase: Use this before filming any client testimonial. Handing a customer a script produces stiff results, but the right questions draw out a natural before and after story, and the edit structure turns raw answers into a persuasive video.

title: Write a Founder Journey Video Script
promptText: Write a founder journey video script from these facts: I started [YOUR BUSINESS] because [YOUR REASON]. Before this I was [YOUR BACKGROUND]. The hardest moment was [YOUR STRUGGLE]. Where I am now: [WHERE YOU ARE NOW]. Open with a scroll stopping first line, tell the arc in short spoken beats, and close by inviting [TARGET AUDIENCE] to follow the journey. Around [TARGET LENGTH]. Keep it honest and human, no corporate language. Include delivery notes for the emotional beats.
description: Script your origin story as a video that anchors your personal brand.
useCase: Use this for a pinned video or a reintroduction after gaining followers. Founder stories convert because they explain why anyone should care about you specifically, and video carries the emotion of the story better than text.

title: Turn a Common Question into a Video
promptText: A question I get asked a lot by [TARGET AUDIENCE] is: [THE QUESTION]. Write a short video script answering it clearly and completely. Open by restating the question as the hook so anyone with it stops scrolling, give the direct answer fast, add the nuance or the one thing most people get wrong, and close with a next step toward [DESIRED ACTION]. Around [TARGET LENGTH]. Conversational spoken language with on screen text for the question and the key answer.
description: Turn a frequently asked question into a searchable, shareable answer video.
useCase: Use this for every question clients and prospects keep asking. Question led videos rank in search and get shared to others with the same question, and answering common questions publicly shortens your future sales conversations.

title: Script a Before and After Transformation Video
promptText: Script a before and after video for [TARGET AUDIENCE]. The before: [BEFORE STATE]. The after: [AFTER STATE]. The change took [TIMEFRAME] and the key move was [KEY CHANGE]. Structure it to make the before feel familiar to the viewer, show the turning point, reveal the after as achievable, and position [YOUR OFFER] as the bridge without a hard sell. Around [TARGET LENGTH]. Include the hook, spoken lines, and visual cues for showing the contrast on screen.
description: Script a transformation video that makes the before relatable and the after attainable.
useCase: Use this for client results or your own progress. Before and after is the most persuasive short form structure because viewers insert themselves into the before, and video lets you show the contrast visually for extra impact.

title: Write a YouTube Shorts Script
promptText: Write a YouTube Shorts script under 60 seconds about [TOPIC] for [TARGET AUDIENCE]. Open with a hook in the first 3 seconds that also works as the title, deliver the value fast with no filler, and end on a loop or a line that encourages a rewatch. Add a soft CTA to [DESIRED ACTION]. Since Shorts favor watch time and loops, note where the ending could tie back to the opening. Include timestamps and on screen text cues.
description: Script a YouTube Short optimized for watch time and looping.
useCase: Use this for the Shorts feed specifically, where loops and watch time drive the algorithm differently than Reels or TikTok. A script built to loop back on itself squeezes extra retention from the same content.

title: Turn a Blog Post into a Video Script
promptText: Turn this blog post into a short video script: [PASTE BLOG POST OR KEY POINTS]. Do not narrate it word for word. Pull the single strongest idea, rewrite it for spoken delivery with a scroll stopping hook, and adapt the structure for [PLATFORM]. My audience: [TARGET AUDIENCE]. Around [TARGET LENGTH]. End with a CTA to [DESIRED ACTION, E.G. READ THE FULL POST]. Include the hook, spoken beats, and on screen text. Flag which supporting points are worth their own separate videos.
description: Adapt written content into a spoken video script instead of reading it aloud.
useCase: Use this to get video content from writing you have already done. Repurposing a blog into video reaches people who never read, and pulling one strong idea rather than narrating the whole thing produces a video that actually works as video.
variables: PASTE BLOG POST OR KEY POINTS
DESIRED ACTION, E.G. READ THE FULL POST

title: Write the Caption and Description for a Video
promptText: My video is about [VIDEO TOPIC] for [TARGET AUDIENCE] on [PLATFORM]. Write the supporting text: a caption with a hook line that complements the video without repeating it, a short description that adds context or a bonus point, a call to action to [DESIRED ACTION], and relevant hashtags or keywords for discovery. Match my tone: [TONE]. The caption should give people who watch on mute a reason to engage and give the algorithm keywords to categorize the video.
description: Write the caption, description, and hashtags that support a video's reach.
useCase: Use this after scripting or filming any video. The caption and description are what mute viewers read and what the algorithm uses to categorize your content, so treating them as an afterthought quietly caps your reach.

title: Batch Five Reel Scripts from One Topic
promptText: Generate 5 distinct short video scripts around the theme of [THEME OR TOPIC] for [TARGET AUDIENCE]. Vary the formats so they do not feel repetitive: one talking head tip, one list, one myth bust, one story, and one question answer. Each needs a hook, the spoken beats, and on screen text cues, around [TARGET LENGTH] each. My business: [BUSINESS TYPE], my tone: [TONE]. The goal is a filming session that produces five ready to post videos from one theme.
description: Produce five varied Reel scripts on one theme for a single filming session.
useCase: Use this to batch film efficiently. Preparing five scripts on one theme in mixed formats means one filming session yields a week or more of content, and the format variety keeps the batch from feeling like the same video five times.

title: Improve a Script's Retention
promptText: Here is my video script: [PASTE SCRIPT]. Analyze it for retention and tell me where viewers are most likely to drop off and why. Then rewrite the weak points: strengthen the hook if the first 2 seconds are soft, cut any slow section, add a pattern interrupt or open loop where attention would sag, and tighten the pacing. Show the improved script and explain each change so I learn the retention principles. Platform: [PLATFORM], target length: [TARGET LENGTH].
description: Diagnose and fix the drop off points in a video script before you film.
useCase: Use this on any script for a video that matters before filming. Retention is the single biggest factor in short form reach, and catching the drop off points on paper is far cheaper than discovering them after the video underperforms.

title: Design a Video Series
promptText: Help me design a recurring video series for [BUSINESS TYPE] that [TARGET AUDIENCE] will follow. My topic area: [YOUR TOPIC], my sustainable cadence: [CADENCE]. Give me a memorable series name, a repeatable format that is easy to produce, and the scripts or outlines for the first 3 episodes. Each episode should stand alone but reward following the series. Include a consistent intro and outro line that signals the series. Explain what makes people come back for the next one.
description: Create a named, repeatable video series with the first three episodes outlined.
useCase: Use this to build appointment video content. A named series lowers your creative load because the format is fixed, and it trains your audience to anticipate and return, turning passive viewers into followers of a show.

title: Write a Launch Announcement Video
promptText: Write a short video script announcing the launch of [YOUR OFFER] to [TARGET AUDIENCE]. The problem it solves: [PROBLEM], the main benefit: [MAIN BENEFIT], the offer or deadline: [OFFER OR DEADLINE]. Open by naming the problem or teasing the result, reveal the offer, give one reason to act now, and close with a clear CTA to [DESIRED ACTION]. Keep the energy genuine, not hype. Around [TARGET LENGTH]. Include the hook, spoken lines, and on screen text for the offer and deadline.
description: Script a launch announcement video that reveals your offer without hype.
useCase: Use this to announce a launch, sale, or new offer on video. Video announcements carry more energy and trust than a graphic, and leading with the problem rather than the product keeps it from feeling like a pure ad.

title: Script a Day in the Life Video
promptText: Help me script a day in the life video for [BUSINESS TYPE] that is engaging, not boring. My real day includes: [DESCRIBE YOUR TYPICAL DAY OR KEY MOMENTS]. My audience: [TARGET AUDIENCE]. Give it a narrative thread rather than just clips, weave in one insight about running my kind of business, and include a hook that promises a reason to watch. Around [TARGET LENGTH]. Provide the voiceover or on screen text beats and note which moments to actually film. Keep it real, not staged perfection.
description: Turn an ordinary work day into a day in the life video with a narrative thread.
useCase: Use this for relatable behind the scenes content. Day in the life videos build parasocial connection that makes people buy from you, but only work with a narrative thread and an insight, otherwise they are just boring clips.
variables: DESCRIBE YOUR TYPICAL DAY OR KEY MOMENTS

title: Write a Reaction or Commentary Script
promptText: I want to react to or comment on this in my niche: [THE ARTICLE, POST, STAT, OR TREND]. My business: [BUSINESS TYPE], my audience: [TARGET AUDIENCE], my expertise angle: [YOUR ANGLE]. Write a short commentary script where I add real insight, not just repeat the thing. Open by showing what I am reacting to, give my expert take, add the context or nuance others miss, and close with what my audience should take from it. Around [TARGET LENGTH]. Include the hook and key spoken beats.
description: Script a commentary video that adds expert insight to something in your niche.
useCase: Use this to react to news, stats, or posts in your field. Commentary content is fast to produce because the source does half the work, and adding your expert angle positions you as a voice in your space rather than just a curator.
variables: THE ARTICLE, POST, STAT, OR TREND

title: Turn a Carousel or List into a Video
promptText: Here is a carousel or text list I already made: [PASTE CAROUSEL OR LIST CONTENT]. Adapt it into a short video script for [PLATFORM]. Rework the static points into a spoken, paced video with a hook that promises the payoff, quick delivery of each point, and on screen text reinforcing the keywords. My audience: [TARGET AUDIENCE], target length: [TARGET LENGTH]. Note where a visual or text animation would strengthen each point. End with a CTA to [DESIRED ACTION].
description: Convert an existing carousel or list post into a paced video script.
useCase: Use this to get double duty from content that performed well as a carousel. Video and static posts reach different audiences, and adapting a proven list into video is low risk since the ideas already worked in another format.

title: Make a Script Sound Natural When Spoken
promptText: Here is my video script, but it sounds stiff and written when I say it out loud: [PASTE SCRIPT]. Rewrite it to sound like natural speech, the way I would actually talk to a friend. Break formal sentences into shorter spoken phrases, add natural connectors and rhythm, remove anything nobody says aloud, and keep my meaning intact. Show the natural version and mark any spots where an unscripted aside would land well. My tone is [TONE].
description: Rewrite a stiff written script into words that sound natural spoken aloud.
useCase: Use this as the final pass before filming any script. Scripts that read fine on paper often sound robotic on camera, and converting written sentences into spoken rhythm is the difference between reading and talking, which viewers feel instantly.

title: Write a First Touch Cold Email
promptText: Write a cold email from me, [WHAT YOU DO], to [PROSPECT TYPE] at [COMPANY TYPE]. The specific problem I help with: [PROBLEM YOU SOLVE], the result I deliver: [RESULT]. Open with a personalized line based on [PERSONALIZATION DETAIL], name the problem in their words, show one concrete result or proof: [PROOF POINT], and close with a low friction ask: [SOFT ASK, E.G. WORTH A QUICK CHAT]. Keep it under 120 words, no jargon, no fake flattery, and one clear call to action.
description: Write a short, personalized first touch cold email that earns a reply.
useCase: Use this to start a cold outreach sequence to a new prospect. The personalized opener and single soft ask are what separate a reply from a delete, and keeping it under 120 words respects a busy inbox.
variables: SOFT ASK, E.G. WORTH A QUICK CHAT

title: Write 15 Cold Email Subject Lines
promptText: My cold email is to [PROSPECT TYPE] about [PROBLEM YOU SOLVE OR OFFER]. Write 15 subject lines built for a cold inbox: 4 curiosity based, 4 that name a specific pain or outcome, 4 that look like a personal note from a peer, and 3 ultra short under 4 words. None should look like marketing or trigger spam instincts. For each, note whether it works better for a cold prospect or a warmer one. Mark the 3 most likely to get opened by a skeptical reader.
description: Generate cold email subject lines that get opened without looking like marketing.
useCase: Use this for any cold outreach send. The subject line decides whether a cold email is opened at all, and the ones that read like a personal note from a peer consistently beat anything that smells like a campaign.

title: Find Real Personalization Angles for a Prospect
promptText: Here is what I know about a prospect: [PASTE THEIR BIO, WEBSITE COPY, RECENT POST, OR COMPANY INFO]. I offer [YOUR OFFER] that helps with [PROBLEM YOU SOLVE]. Find genuine personalization angles I could open a cold email with: something specific to them, their company, or their recent activity that connects naturally to what I do. Give me 4 opener lines, each tied to a real detail, that feel observed rather than scraped. Flag any angle that would come across as forced or creepy.
description: Extract genuine, non creepy personalization angles from what you know about a prospect.
useCase: Use this before writing outreach to a specific high value prospect. Real personalization tied to an observed detail is the single biggest lever on cold email reply rates, and this finds the angle that connects their world to your offer.
variables: PASTE THEIR BIO, WEBSITE COPY, RECENT POST, OR COMPANY INFO

title: Write a Cold Email Follow Up Sequence
promptText: Write a 4 email follow up sequence for cold prospects who did not reply to my first email about [PROBLEM YOU SOLVE] for [PROSPECT TYPE]. Follow up 1 adds a new angle or proof: [PROOF OR NEW ANGLE], follow up 2 shares a relevant resource or insight with no ask, follow up 3 asks a simple direct question, follow up 4 is a respectful breakup email that leaves the door open. Each under 80 words, each adding something new rather than just bumping. Space them across [DAYS BETWEEN] days.
description: Write a value adding follow up sequence for cold prospects who did not reply.
useCase: Use this because most cold email replies come from follow ups, not the first send. Each message here adds a new reason to respond rather than nagging, which is what keeps prospects engaged instead of annoyed across the sequence.

title: Write a Breakup Email That Gets Replies
promptText: Write a breakup email to a cold prospect I have followed up with a few times about [YOUR OFFER] with no response. It should acknowledge I have not heard back, assume the timing is not right rather than guilt tripping, make one final low pressure offer to help, and close the loop gracefully while leaving the door open for the future. Under 70 words, warm and human. Give me 2 versions: one slightly more direct, one softer.
description: Write a graceful breakup email that often pulls a reply from silent prospects.
useCase: Use this as the final message to an unresponsive prospect. Breakup emails get surprisingly high reply rates because the removal of pressure and the sense of a closing door prompt people who were quietly interested to finally respond.

title: Write a LinkedIn Cold Connection Message
promptText: Write a LinkedIn outreach sequence to [PROSPECT TYPE] at [COMPANY TYPE]. Message 1 is a connection request note under 300 characters that references [PERSONALIZATION DETAIL] and gives a genuine reason to connect, not a pitch. Message 2, after they accept, opens a real conversation about [PROBLEM YOU SOLVE] without selling. Message 3 makes a soft, relevant offer only if the conversation warrants it. Keep it human and peer to peer, never like an automated pitch. I offer [YOUR OFFER].
description: Write a LinkedIn connection and message sequence that opens conversations, not pitches.
useCase: Use this for LinkedIn outreach where an immediate pitch gets you ignored or blocked. Leading with a real reason to connect and a genuine conversation before any offer is what makes LinkedIn outreach convert instead of annoy.

title: Write a Cold DM for Instagram or X
promptText: Write a cold DM sequence for [PLATFORM, E.G. INSTAGRAM OR X] to [PROSPECT TYPE]. Message 1 opens with a genuine comment on their content or work: [WHAT YOU NOTICED], no pitch. Message 2 continues the conversation naturally and surfaces the problem I help with: [PROBLEM YOU SOLVE]. Message 3 offers value or a soft next step only if they engage. Keep each message short and casual, matching DM culture, never a formal pitch pasted into a DM. I offer [YOUR OFFER].
description: Write casual, conversational cold DMs that fit social platform culture.
useCase: Use this for DM outreach on Instagram or X, where a formal pitch dies instantly. Matching the casual conversational tone of DMs and leading with genuine engagement is what earns a reply in a channel built for conversation, not sales.

title: Write a Value First Cold Email
promptText: Write a cold email that leads with free value, not a pitch, to [PROSPECT TYPE]. I noticed [SPECIFIC OBSERVATION ABOUT THEIR BUSINESS] and I can offer a genuinely useful insight, tip, or quick win related to [PROBLEM YOU SOLVE]: [THE VALUE YOU CAN GIVE]. Deliver the value in the email itself so it stands alone even if they never reply, then mention what I do in one soft line at the end. Under 130 words. I offer [YOUR OFFER].
description: Write a cold email that gives real value upfront so it lands even without a reply.
useCase: Use this for high value prospects where a normal pitch would get ignored. Leading with a genuine insight or quick win earns goodwill and stands out in an inbox full of asks, making the soft mention of your offer land far better.

title: Write a Cold Email Built Around a Case Study
promptText: Write a cold email to [PROSPECT TYPE] built around a relevant result. My client, similar to them because [SIMILARITY], had this problem: [CLIENT PROBLEM], and I helped them achieve [RESULT WITH NUMBER IF POSSIBLE]. Open by connecting their likely situation to my client's starting point, share the result briefly, and offer to explain how in a short conversation. Under 120 words. Make the prospect think if it worked for a business like mine, it could work for me. I offer [YOUR OFFER].
description: Write a cold email that leads with a relevant client result to build instant credibility.
useCase: Use this when you have a strong result from a client similar to your prospect. A relevant case study is the fastest way to build credibility in a cold email, because it lets the prospect see themselves in the before and imagine the after.

title: Respond to a Cold Email Reply
promptText: A cold prospect replied to my outreach. Here is their reply: [PASTE THEIR REPLY]. My offer: [YOUR OFFER], my goal is to [GOAL, E.G. BOOK A CALL]. Write my response that matches their tone and energy, addresses whatever they raised, moves the conversation toward my goal without rushing, and keeps it human. If their reply was lukewarm or had a soft objection, handle it gently. Give me the response plus a one line note on what their reply signals about their interest level.
description: Craft the right reply to a cold prospect's response and keep the deal moving.
useCase: Use this the moment a cold prospect replies, when the right response determines whether the conversation advances or dies. Matching their tone and addressing their actual words, rather than jumping to a pitch, is what turns a reply into a booked call.

title: Write the Email That Asks for the Meeting
promptText: A prospect is warm and it is time to ask for a call. Context of our conversation so far: [BRIEF CONTEXT]. Write the email that asks for the meeting. Make the ask specific and easy: propose the purpose of the call in one line, keep the commitment small: [CALL LENGTH], reduce friction by offering [SCHEDULING METHOD, E.G. A BOOKING LINK OR TWO TIME OPTIONS], and reinforce what they get from the call. Under 90 words. Give me 2 versions, one more direct and one more consultative.
description: Write the low friction email that converts a warm prospect into a booked call.
useCase: Use this when a prospect is engaged and you need to move to a call. Vague asks like let me know if you want to chat stall, while a specific purpose, a small time commitment, and an easy way to book are what actually get the meeting on the calendar.
variables: SCHEDULING METHOD, E.G. A BOOKING LINK OR TWO TIME OPTIONS

title: Write a Warm Referral Introduction Email
promptText: Write an outreach email to [PROSPECT TYPE] where I was referred or have a warm connection: [THE CONNECTION, E.G. MUTUAL CONTACT OR SHARED GROUP]. Open by establishing the connection clearly and early so I am not a stranger, briefly explain why I am reaching out about [PROBLEM YOU SOLVE], and make a low pressure ask. Under 110 words. The warm connection should do the heavy lifting on trust, so the email can be more direct than a fully cold one. I offer [YOUR OFFER].
description: Write outreach that leverages a warm connection or referral for instant trust.
useCase: Use this whenever you have any connection to a prospect, a mutual contact, a shared community, a referral. Leading with the connection borrows its trust, so these emails can be more direct and convert far better than fully cold outreach.
variables: THE CONNECTION, E.G. MUTUAL CONTACT OR SHARED GROUP

title: Ask a Contact for an Introduction
promptText: Write an email asking [YOUR CONTACT] to introduce me to [TARGET PERSON OR TYPE] at [COMPANY]. Make it easy for them to say yes: explain briefly why the intro makes sense, what is in it for the person being introduced, and include a short forwardable blurb they can pass along with no editing. Give my contact an easy out so there is no pressure. Under 130 words including the blurb. What I do: [WHAT YOU DO], the value for the target: [VALUE FOR TARGET].
description: Write an easy to say yes intro request with a ready to forward blurb.
useCase: Use this to turn your existing network into warm introductions. Including a forwardable blurb removes the work from your contact, which dramatically raises the odds they actually make the intro instead of leaving your request in their to do pile.

title: Write a Trigger Based Cold Email
promptText: A prospect just had a relevant trigger event: [THE TRIGGER, E.G. NEW ROLE, FUNDING, EXPANSION, PRODUCT LAUNCH, OR HIRING]. They are [PROSPECT TYPE] at [COMPANY]. Write a timely cold email that references the trigger naturally as the reason for reaching out now, connects it to the problem I solve: [PROBLEM YOU SOLVE], and makes a relevant soft offer. The timing should feel like good attentiveness, not surveillance. Under 110 words. I offer [YOUR OFFER].
description: Write a timely cold email triggered by a relevant event in the prospect's world.
useCase: Use this when a prospect has a trigger event that creates a natural reason to reach out now, such as a new role, funding, or expansion. Trigger based timing dramatically lifts relevance and reply rates because your email arrives exactly when the need is fresh.
variables: THE TRIGGER, E.G. NEW ROLE, FUNDING, EXPANSION, PRODUCT LAUNCH, OR HIRING

title: Tighten and Sharpen a Cold Email Draft
promptText: Here is my cold email draft: [PASTE DRAFT]. Sharpen it for a cold, skeptical, busy reader. Cut it by at least a third, remove anything that sounds like marketing or self focus, make sure the first line earns attention without a personalization gimmick, ensure there is exactly one clear ask, and flag any line that would trigger delete on sight. Show the tightened version, then 3 bullets on what you cut and why so I learn the pattern. My prospect: [PROSPECT TYPE].
description: Cut and sharpen a cold email draft for a skeptical, time poor reader.
useCase: Use this on any cold email before sending. Cold drafts are almost always too long and too self focused, and a disciplined edit that removes marketing language and enforces one clear ask is often the difference between a reply and a delete.

title: Check a Cold Email for Spam Triggers
promptText: Review this cold email for anything that could hurt deliverability or trigger spam filters: [PASTE EMAIL]. Flag: spam trigger words, too many links, image heavy or salesy formatting, a subject line that looks like marketing, aggressive urgency, and anything that reads as mass sent rather than personal. For each flag, suggest a fix. Also assess whether it looks like a genuine one to one email or a blast, since that perception affects both filters and replies. Note this is general guidance, not a deliverability guarantee.
description: Catch the spam triggers and mass send signals that hurt cold email deliverability.
useCase: Use this before sending cold outreach at any volume. Emails that land in spam or look mass blasted never get a chance to work, and a quick check for trigger words and blast signals protects both your deliverability and your reply rate.

title: Write A/B Test Versions of a Cold Email
promptText: Here is my current cold email: [PASTE EMAIL] to [PROSPECT TYPE]. Create 2 test versions that each change exactly one meaningful variable so I can learn what works: version A tests a different [VARIABLE TO TEST, E.G. HOOK, OFFER FRAMING, OR CALL TO ACTION]. Keep everything else identical between the control and each version. Tell me what each version is testing, what result would prove the change worked, and which single variable is the highest leverage one to test first.
description: Create controlled A/B versions of a cold email that isolate one variable.
useCase: Use this when running outreach at enough volume to test. Changing one variable at a time is how you actually learn what moves reply rates, rather than rewriting everything and never knowing which change helped.
variables: VARIABLE TO TEST, E.G. HOOK, OFFER FRAMING, OR CALL TO ACTION

title: Define Who to Target Before You Write
promptText: Help me sharpen who I should be cold emailing. My offer: [YOUR OFFER], the problem it solves: [PROBLEM YOU SOLVE], my best past clients were: [DESCRIBE BEST CLIENTS OR WRITE NONE YET]. Define my ideal outreach target: the specific role, company type, size, and situation that makes them most likely to need and buy this now. Then list the buying signals or trigger events that indicate a prospect is a good fit right now, and where I might find prospects matching this profile.
description: Define your ideal cold outreach target and the signals that they are ready now.
useCase: Use this before writing any outreach, because the best cold email to the wrong person still fails. Getting specific about who to target and what signals readiness makes every downstream email more relevant and dramatically raises your hit rate.
variables: DESCRIBE BEST CLIENTS OR WRITE NONE YET

title: Write a Cold Email for a Partnership
promptText: Write a cold email proposing a partnership or collaboration to [TARGET PERSON OR BUSINESS], who [WHAT THEY DO]. My business: [WHAT YOU DO]. The partnership idea: [PARTNERSHIP IDEA], and the clear benefit to them: [BENEFIT TO THEM]. Lead with what is in it for them, not what I want, show I understand their audience or business, and propose a small easy first step to explore it. Under 130 words. Peer to peer tone, since this is a collaboration between equals, not a sales pitch.
description: Write partnership outreach that leads with the other side's benefit.
useCase: Use this to propose collaborations, cross promotions, or partnerships with complementary businesses. Partnership outreach fails when it centers what you want, and leading with a clear, specific benefit to them is what earns a yes between equals.

title: Re Engage an Old Cold Lead
promptText: I want to re engage a prospect who went cold [TIME SINCE LAST CONTACT] ago. Last context: [WHAT HAPPENED OR WHERE IT STALLED]. Since then, something new gives me a reason to reach out: [NEW REASON, E.G. NEW RESULT, NEW OFFER, OR RELEVANT UPDATE]. Write a re engagement email that references our past contact lightly without guilt, leads with the new reason, and makes a fresh low pressure ask. Under 100 words. My offer: [YOUR OFFER]. Warm and easy, like reconnecting, not chasing.
description: Reconnect with a prospect who went cold using a fresh, genuine reason.
useCase: Use this to revive stalled prospects sitting in your pipeline. Old leads are warmer than cold ones because you already have context, and a fresh reason to reach out reopens the conversation without the awkwardness of just bumping an old thread.
variables: WHAT HAPPENED OR WHERE IT STALLED
NEW REASON, E.G. NEW RESULT, NEW OFFER, OR RELEVANT UPDATE

title: Write an Ultra Short Cold Email
promptText: Write an ultra short cold email, under 50 words, to [PROSPECT TYPE] about [PROBLEM YOU SOLVE]. It must feel like a quick personal note from a busy person to another busy person: one line of context or personalization from [PERSONALIZATION DETAIL], one line naming the value, one line with a simple question as the ask. No signature block padding, no marketing language. Give me 3 versions with slightly different angles. I offer [YOUR OFFER].
description: Write a sub 50 word cold email that reads like a quick personal note.
useCase: Use this when targeting busy executives or anyone whose inbox is overwhelmed. Ultra short emails that read like a quick note from a peer often outperform polished longer ones, because they respect the reader's time and are easy to answer on a phone.

title: Preempt the Main Objection in a Cold Email
promptText: The most common reason [PROSPECT TYPE] ignores or declines outreach like mine is: [MAIN OBJECTION, E.G. NO BUDGET, NO TIME, ALREADY HAVE A SOLUTION, OR NEVER HEARD OF YOU]. Write a cold email that quietly disarms this objection before it stops them, without naming it defensively. Address it through framing, proof, or a low commitment ask that sidesteps it. Connect to the problem I solve: [PROBLEM YOU SOLVE]. Under 120 words. I offer [YOUR OFFER].
description: Write a cold email that neutralizes the prospect's main objection before it fires.
useCase: Use this when you know the recurring reason prospects say no. Disarming the top objection through framing and proof, rather than waiting for it to surface, removes the silent barrier that kills most cold outreach before a conversation even starts.
variables: MAIN OBJECTION, E.G. NO BUDGET, NO TIME, ALREADY HAVE A SOLUTION, OR NEVER HEARD OF YOU

title: Make a Cold Email Sound Human
promptText: Here is my cold email, but it sounds robotic or templated: [PASTE EMAIL]. Rewrite it to sound like a real person wrote it just for this prospect. Remove templated phrasing, add natural conversational rhythm, cut corporate filler, and make it read like a peer reaching out, not a sales sequence. Keep it professional but human. Here are 2 samples of how I naturally write so you can match my voice: [PASTE 2 SAMPLES OF YOUR WRITING]. Show the humanized version.
description: Strip the templated feel from a cold email so it reads as genuinely personal.
useCase: Use this as the final pass on any cold email, especially AI drafted ones. Prospects delete anything that smells automated, and matching your natural voice while cutting templated phrasing is what makes outreach feel like a real person reaching out.

title: Set Up a Simple Cold Outreach Tracking System
promptText: Help me set up a simple system to track my cold outreach as a solo operator. I send roughly [OUTREACH VOLUME] per week and use [YOUR TOOLS, E.G. GMAIL AND A SPREADSHEET]. Design a lightweight tracking approach that captures: who I contacted and when, follow up timing so no one falls through the cracks, response status, and which message angles are working. Keep it low friction so I actually maintain it. Give me the exact columns or fields to use and a simple weekly review routine.
description: Build a low friction system to track cold outreach and follow ups as a solo operator.
useCase: Use this once to stop losing prospects to missed follow ups. Most cold outreach revenue is lost to disorganization rather than bad emails, and a simple tracking system ensures every prospect gets their follow ups and you learn which angles convert.
variables: YOUR TOOLS, E.G. GMAIL AND A SPREADSHEET

title: Write a Post Sales Call Follow Up Sequence
promptText: Write a 3 email follow up sequence after a sales call with [PROSPECT TYPE] about [YOUR OFFER]. On the call we discussed [WHAT WAS DISCUSSED] and their main hesitation was [MAIN HESITATION]. Email 1, sent same day, thanks them, recaps what we agreed, and restates the value in their words. Email 2, a few days later, addresses the hesitation with proof: [PROOF POINT]. Email 3 makes a clear decision ask with a gentle deadline: [DEADLINE OR NEXT STEP]. Keep each under 130 words and human.
description: Follow up after a sales call with a sequence that recaps, reassures, and asks for the decision.
useCase: Use this right after any sales call to keep momentum. Most deals stall in the gap after the call, and a structured sequence that recaps value then handles the specific hesitation is what moves an interested prospect to a yes.

title: Write a Follow Up Sequence After Sending a Proposal
promptText: Write a follow up sequence for after I send a proposal to [PROSPECT TYPE] for [YOUR OFFER] priced at [PRICE]. Email 1, two days after sending, checks they received it and offers to answer questions. Email 2, four days later, adds a relevant proof point or addresses the likely sticking point: [LIKELY STICKING POINT]. Email 3 asks directly if we are moving forward and what they need to decide. Keep each short, confident, and free of desperation. The tone assumes the proposal has value, not that I am begging.
description: Follow up on a sent proposal with a confident sequence that drives a decision.
useCase: Use this after sending any proposal or quote. Proposals often go silent not from disinterest but from busyness, and a confident follow up sequence that offers help then asks directly is what closes the gap between sent and signed.

title: Write a Nurture Sequence After a Lead Magnet
promptText: Write a 5 email nurture sequence for leads who downloaded my [LEAD MAGNET] and are interested in [TOPIC] but not ready to buy. My offer: [YOUR OFFER], my audience: [TARGET AUDIENCE].
