Give me natural questions that uncover: who else is involved in the decision, what steps or approvals it goes through, what criteria they will judge options on, what their timeline is, and what has caused decisions like this to stall for them before. Frame these as helping us both plan a smooth process. Knowing the real decision path lets me support it rather than being surprised by hidden steps. Keep it collaborative.
description: Write questions that map the prospect's real path to a buying decision.
useCase: Use this to avoid deals stalling on hidden decision steps. Mapping who is involved, what criteria matter, and what has stalled past decisions lets you support the real buying process, rather than being blindsided by an approval or stakeholder you never knew existed.

title: Write Questions to Close for the Next Step
promptText: Write questions and language to close a discovery call for [YOUR OFFER] with a clear next step. Depending on how the call went, the next step might be [POSSIBLE NEXT STEPS, E.G. A PROPOSAL, A SECOND CALL, OR STARTING]. Give me: a question that gauges their interest level honestly, a way to confirm whether they see my offer as a fit, and language to secure a specific committed next step with a date rather than a vague I will think about it. Include how to handle it gracefully if they are not ready. I want every call to end with clarity, not ambiguity.
description: Write closing questions that secure a specific, committed next step.
useCase: Use this to end discovery calls with clarity instead of a vague maybe. Questions that gauge real interest and secure a dated next step prevent the ambiguous ending where a promising call fades into silence, and give you a clear read on whether to invest further.
variables: POSSIBLE NEXT STEPS, E.G. A PROPOSAL, A SECOND CALL, OR STARTING

title: Tailor Discovery Questions to an Industry
promptText: Tailor my discovery questions for [YOUR OFFER] to a prospect in [SPECIFIC INDUSTRY OR NICHE]. This industry has specific characteristics: [INDUSTRY CONTEXT, CHALLENGES, OR LANGUAGE]. Give me discovery questions that use their industry language, reference challenges specific to their world, and show I understand their context rather than asking generic questions. Include the industry specific pains I should probe for and the outcomes they typically care about. Speaking their language during discovery builds instant credibility. Keep the questions natural, informed, and specific to their situation.
description: Adapt your discovery questions to speak a specific industry's language.
useCase: Use this when selling into a specific industry or niche. Discovery questions that use their language and reference their specific challenges build instant credibility, signaling you understand their world, which sets you apart from generalist sellers asking interchangeable generic questions.

title: Write Questions That Reframe Their Thinking
promptText: Write consultative discovery questions that gently reframe how a prospect thinks about [PROBLEM] related to [YOUR OFFER]. Many prospects misunderstand the problem in this way: [COMMON MISCONCEPTION], when the reality is [THE REALITY]. Give me questions that lead them to reconsider their assumption and see the problem more accurately, without telling them they are wrong. The questions should create an insight moment where they realize the issue is different or bigger than they thought. This positions me as an expert who sees what they miss. Keep it curious and respectful, never condescending.
description: Write questions that reframe a prospect's misunderstanding into an insight.
useCase: Use this when prospects misunderstand their own problem. Questions that lead them to an insight moment, seeing the issue is different or bigger than they thought, position you as the expert who sees what they miss, which is one of the strongest ways to earn trust and the sale.

title: Create a Discovery Call Note Template
promptText: Create a discovery call note taking template for [YOUR OFFER] that helps me capture what matters during the call and use it afterward. It should have clear sections for: their stated problem in their words, the deeper problem, the cost of inaction, their desired outcome, budget and timeline signals, decision process, objections raised, and their exact quotable language. Make it quick to fill in live without breaking the flow of conversation. Include a section for my next steps and follow up. The notes should make writing a tailored proposal or follow up effortless afterward.
description: Create a note template that captures the right discovery details for later use.
useCase: Use this to make your discovery calls pay off afterward. A structured note template captures the prospect's exact language and key signals live, so writing a tailored proposal or follow up becomes effortless, rather than relying on memory of a call that happened hours ago.

title: Write Questions for a Short Discovery Call
promptText: I only have a short call, about [SHORT CALL LENGTH], to do discovery for [YOUR OFFER] with [TARGET AUDIENCE]. Give me the highest value questions to ask when time is limited: the few that uncover the most about their problem, urgency, fit, and buying readiness quickly. Prioritize ruthlessly, since I cannot ask everything. Include the one question I should not skip and how to still build enough rapport in a compressed call. The goal is to learn what I need to know and set a next step efficiently without the prospect feeling rushed.
description: Write the highest leverage discovery questions for a time limited call.
useCase: Use this for quick calls where full discovery is not possible. Prioritizing the few questions that reveal the most about problem, urgency, and fit lets you qualify and diagnose efficiently in a compressed call, without the prospect feeling processed or rushed.

title: Write Discovery Questions for a Warm Referral
promptText: Write discovery questions for a call with a warm referral for [YOUR OFFER], referred by [WHO REFERRED THEM]. Since they come pre trusted, I want discovery that respects the existing warmth rather than treating them like a cold prospect. Give me: how to acknowledge the referral naturally, questions that still properly diagnose their situation without over interrogating someone who already trusts me, and how to move a bit faster to solution given the warm start. Balance efficiency with still doing real discovery, since warm leads can be under qualified if I skip diagnosis. My tone: [TONE].
description: Write discovery for a warm referral that respects the trust without skipping diagnosis.
useCase: Use this for calls with warm referrals. The existing trust lets you move faster and skip heavy qualifying, but proper diagnosis still matters, and this balances honoring the warm start with the real discovery that prevents under qualified referrals from becoming bad fit clients.

title: Write a Post Discovery Recap Message
promptText: Write a recap message to send after a discovery call for [YOUR OFFER]. Here is what I learned on the call: [KEY POINTS FROM THE CALL, THEIR PROBLEM, GOALS, AND CONTEXT]. Write a message that summarizes their situation and goals back to them in their own language so they feel deeply understood, confirms I captured it correctly, and reinforces that I am the right person to help. End by restating the agreed next step. This recap builds trust and momentum between the call and the proposal. Keep it concise and warm. My tone: [TONE].
description: Write a post call recap that reflects their situation back and builds momentum.
useCase: Use this right after a discovery call. A recap that reflects their situation back in their own words makes prospects feel deeply understood, sets you apart from sellers who go silent after calls, and maintains momentum into the proposal while the conversation is still warm.
variables: KEY POINTS FROM THE CALL, THEIR PROBLEM, GOALS, AND CONTEXT

title: Audit and Improve Your Discovery Questions
promptText: Here are the discovery questions I currently ask on sales calls for [YOUR OFFER]: [PASTE YOUR CURRENT QUESTIONS]. Audit them. Tell me: which questions are weak, leading, or yield shallow answers, which important areas I am not asking about, which questions I could reorder for better flow, and where I am talking or pitching when I should be asking. Then give me improved versions and any missing questions. My goal on these calls is [YOUR CALL GOAL]. I want discovery that uncovers more and converts better without feeling like an interrogation.
description: Audit your existing discovery questions and get sharper, more effective versions.
useCase: Use this to improve discovery calls you already run. An audit reveals which questions yield shallow answers, what you are failing to ask, and where you pitch instead of probe, turning a decent discovery process into one that uncovers more and converts better.

title: Write a Natural Upsell Message
promptText: Write an upsell message to a customer who bought [INITIAL PURCHASE] offering them [UPSELL OFFER]. The upsell genuinely helps because [WHY IT HELPS THEM NEXT]. Write it to feel like a helpful recommendation, not a cash grab. Open by acknowledging their progress or success with the first purchase, surface the next problem or goal they will naturally hit, present the upsell as the logical solution to that, and make the ask low pressure. The customer should feel I am looking out for their next win, not just my revenue. My tone: [TONE].
description: Write an upsell that reads as a genuine recommendation for the customer's next win.
useCase: Use this to upsell existing customers without damaging the relationship. Framing the offer as the logical solution to their next problem, rather than a pitch, converts because customers who already trust you welcome help reaching their next goal.

title: Write a Cross Sell Message
promptText: Write a cross sell message to a customer who bought [INITIAL PURCHASE] recommending [COMPLEMENTARY OFFER] that pairs well with it. The connection between them is [HOW THEY RELATE]. Write it to show how the complementary offer enhances or completes what they already have, using the logic of what naturally goes together. Keep it helpful and relevant, positioning it as making their original purchase even more effective. Avoid feeling like a random add on pitch. My audience: [TARGET AUDIENCE], my tone: [TONE].
description: Write a cross sell that positions a complementary offer as completing their purchase.
useCase: Use this to sell complementary offers to existing customers. A cross sell that shows how the second offer enhances the first feels like helpful completion rather than a random pitch, which is why the customers who already bought are your easiest next sale.

title: Plan the Right Timing for an Upsell
promptText: Help me figure out the best moment to upsell customers of [INITIAL PURCHASE] to [UPSELL OFFER]. My customer journey looks like [DESCRIBE THE JOURNEY OR KEY MILESTONES]. Tell me: the moments when a customer is most receptive to an upsell, the moments I should never upsell because it would feel wrong, what signal indicates a customer is ready for the next offer, and how the message should differ depending on when I send it. Timing an upsell right matters as much as the message. Give me a simple timing plan I can follow.
description: Identify the moments when customers are most receptive to an upsell.
useCase: Use this to time upsells for when customers actually welcome them. Upselling too early feels pushy and too late misses the window, and matching the offer to receptive moments like right after a first win dramatically raises acceptance without straining the relationship.
variables: DESCRIBE THE JOURNEY OR KEY MILESTONES

title: Write Order Bump Copy
promptText: Write order bump copy for the checkout of [MAIN OFFER]. The bump offer is [BUMP OFFER] at [BUMP PRICE], which complements the main purchase by [HOW IT COMPLEMENTS]. Write a short, punchy bump that a buyer sees at checkout: a compelling headline, one or two lines on why to add it now, and a clear yes checkbox line. It must be quick to grasp and easy to say yes to in the moment without derailing the main purchase. Keep the friction near zero. Give me 3 variations to test. My tone: [TONE].
description: Write concise checkout order bump copy that lifts average order value.
useCase: Use this to add revenue at checkout with minimal effort. A well written order bump captures impulse yeses at the moment of purchase, and because the buyer is already committed, a small relevant add on can lift average order value with almost no added friction.

title: Write a Post Purchase One Click Upsell
promptText: Write a one click upsell offer shown right after a customer buys [INITIAL PURCHASE]. The upsell is [UPSELL OFFER] at [UPSELL PRICE], and it helps them by [BENEFIT]. Write the upsell page copy: a headline that congratulates them and introduces the offer, a short pitch on why adding it now makes sense, why this price is only available now, and clear accept and decline options. It should capitalize on the buying momentum without feeling like a bait and switch. Keep it honest and benefit focused. Give me the accept and a graceful decline line. My tone: [TONE].
description: Write a post purchase one click upsell that rides the buying momentum honestly.
useCase: Use this for a one click upsell immediately after purchase, when buying momentum is highest. A relevant offer at that moment converts well because the customer is already in buying mode, and honest benefit focused copy captures the upside without the bait and switch feel that damages trust.

title: Upsell Right After a Customer Win
promptText: A customer just experienced a win with [INITIAL PURCHASE]: [DESCRIBE THEIR WIN OR RESULT]. Write an upsell message that rides this positive moment to offer [UPSELL OFFER]. Acknowledge and celebrate their result genuinely, connect it to the bigger goal the upsell helps them reach, and position the next offer as the way to build on the momentum they just created. The message should feel like a natural next step for someone on a roll, not an interruption of their success. Keep it warm and genuinely congratulatory first, offer second. My tone: [TONE].
description: Upsell at the peak moment right after a customer experiences a win.
useCase: Use this immediately after a customer gets a result. The post win moment is when customers are most motivated to keep going, and an upsell that celebrates their success and offers to build on the momentum converts far better than one sent during a neutral moment.

title: Write an Upsell Email Sequence
promptText: Write a 3 email upsell sequence for customers of [INITIAL PURCHASE] to move them to [UPSELL OFFER]. The upsell solves [WHAT IT SOLVES NEXT] for them. Email 1 ensures they are getting value from their first purchase and hints at what is next, email 2 surfaces the next challenge and introduces the upsell as the solution, email 3 makes a clear offer with a reason to act now: [INCENTIVE OR DEADLINE, OR WRITE NONE]. Space them over [DAYS]. Each should serve their success first. My tone: [TONE].
description: Write a value led email sequence that moves customers to the next offer.
useCase: Use this to upsell systematically over email rather than a single ask. A sequence that confirms value, surfaces the next challenge, then offers the solution respects the customer's journey and converts better than a lone pitch, while automating your most reliable revenue source.
variables: INCENTIVE OR DEADLINE, OR WRITE NONE

title: Write a Bundle Offer Message
promptText: Write a message offering a bundle of [ITEMS OR OFFERS IN THE BUNDLE] to [TARGET AUDIENCE] at [BUNDLE PRICE], which saves them compared to buying separately. Write it to make the bundle feel like the smart, obvious choice: show the combined value, make the savings clear, and explain why these items belong together and are better as a set. Position it as getting more of the outcome they want for less. Include a reason the bundle is available now: [REASON OR WRITE LIMITED]. Keep it clear and compelling. My tone: [TONE].
description: Write a bundle offer that makes buying the set the obvious smart choice.
useCase: Use this to increase order value by bundling offers. A bundle message that shows combined value and clear savings makes buying the set feel smarter than buying separately, raising your average sale while giving customers more of the outcome they want.
variables: ITEMS OR OFFERS IN THE BUNDLE

title: Write a Message to Upgrade a Tier
promptText: Write a message encouraging a customer on my [CURRENT TIER] to upgrade to [HIGHER TIER]. The higher tier adds [WHAT THE UPGRADE ADDS] and suits customers who [WHO IT SUITS]. Base the pitch on a signal that they are ready: [READINESS SIGNAL, E.G. HITTING A LIMIT OR WANTING MORE]. Show them what they are missing or bumping against on their current tier, what the upgrade unlocks, and why it is worth the difference. Make it feel like a natural graduation to the next level, not a squeeze. My tone: [TONE].
description: Write a tier upgrade message triggered by a customer's readiness signal.
useCase: Use this to move customers up your pricing tiers. An upgrade message triggered by a real signal, like hitting a limit or wanting more, feels like a natural graduation rather than a squeeze, and converts because it addresses a need the customer is already feeling.
variables: READINESS SIGNAL, E.G. HITTING A LIMIT OR WANTING MORE

title: Write a Renewal Plus Upgrade Message
promptText: Write a message to a customer whose [SUBSCRIPTION OR RETAINER] is up for renewal, encouraging them to renew and consider upgrading to [UPGRADE OR EXPANDED OFFER]. Their results or experience so far: [THEIR RESULTS OR HISTORY]. Write it to reaffirm the value they have received, make renewing feel like an easy yes, and present the upgrade as the way to get even more from the next period. Balance securing the renewal with the upsell so I do not risk the renewal by pushing too hard on the upgrade. Renewal first, upgrade as the bonus. My tone: [TONE].
description: Write a renewal message that secures the renewal and offers an upgrade as a bonus.
useCase: Use this at renewal time for subscriptions or retainers. A message that reaffirms value and makes renewal easy while offering an upgrade captures expansion revenue at the natural decision point, without risking the renewal by pushing the upgrade too aggressively.

title: Write an Upsell for Loyal Customers
promptText: Write an upsell message for my most loyal customers who have bought from me multiple times, offering [PREMIUM OR NEXT LEVEL OFFER]. Acknowledge and appreciate their loyalty genuinely, and frame this offer as something for my best customers specifically. Their history with me: [THEIR HISTORY OR REPEAT PURCHASES]. Make them feel recognized and valued, and present the offer as a natural next step for someone this committed, possibly with a loyalty perk: [LOYALTY PERK OR WRITE NONE]. The relationship should come through clearly. Warm and appreciative. My tone: [TONE].
description: Write an upsell for loyal repeat customers that honors the relationship.
useCase: Use this to upsell your best repeat customers. Loyal customers convert on premium offers when the message recognizes their history and frames the offer as something for your best people, deepening a relationship that is already your most valuable business asset.

title: Write a Downsell for a Declined Offer
promptText: A customer or prospect declined [DECLINED OFFER] at [ORIGINAL PRICE], likely due to [LIKELY REASON, E.G. PRICE OR SCOPE]. Write a downsell offering [SMALLER OFFER] at [LOWER PRICE] as an alternative that still helps them. Write it to feel like I am meeting them where they are, not desperate to make any sale. Acknowledge that the first offer was not the right fit right now, present the smaller option as a genuine solution to their core need, and keep the door open to the fuller offer later. Helpful, not pushy. My tone: [TONE].
description: Write a downsell that recovers a declined sale with a smaller genuine option.
useCase: Use this when a prospect declines your main offer. A downsell to a smaller option recovers revenue that would otherwise be lost entirely, and framing it as meeting them where they are keeps the relationship intact and the door open to the full offer later.
variables: LIKELY REASON, E.G. PRICE OR SCOPE

title: Write a Win Back Upsell for Past Customers
promptText: Write a message to past customers who bought [PAST PURCHASE] a while ago but have not bought since, offering them [NEW OR RETURN OFFER]. It has been [TIME SINCE]. Write it to reconnect warmly, remind them of the value they got before, share what is new or why now is a good time to come back, and present the offer as a reason to re engage. Include a returning customer incentive if it helps: [INCENTIVE OR WRITE NONE]. It should feel like a genuine invitation back, not a guilt trip about their absence. My tone: [TONE].
description: Write a win back message that re engages past customers with a fresh offer.
useCase: Use this to re engage lapsed customers, who are far cheaper to win back than new ones are to acquire. A warm reconnection that reminds them of past value and gives a reason to return revives dormant relationships that already proved they will buy from you.

title: Handle Objections to an Upsell
promptText: A customer is hesitant about my upsell offer [UPSELL OFFER] after buying [INITIAL PURCHASE]. Their hesitation is [THEIR OBJECTION, E.G. NOT SURE THEY NEED IT OR SPENT ENOUGH ALREADY]. Write responses that handle this without pressuring an existing customer I want to keep happy. Address the specific concern, reinforce the value of the upsell for their situation, and if they are truly not ready, gracefully accept it while keeping the relationship warm. Protecting the customer relationship matters more than forcing this one upsell. Give me a version that holds and a version that gracefully lets it go. My tone: [TONE].
description: Handle upsell objections while protecting the existing customer relationship.
useCase: Use this when a customer hesitates on an upsell. Because you want to keep them happy long term, handling their objection gently and gracefully accepting a no when needed protects the relationship, which is worth far more than pressuring a single upsell.
variables: THEIR OBJECTION, E.G. NOT SURE THEY NEED IT OR SPENT ENOUGH ALREADY

title: Weave an Upsell Into Onboarding
promptText: Help me naturally introduce a future upsell during onboarding for new customers of [INITIAL PURCHASE], where the eventual upsell is [UPSELL OFFER]. I do not want to sell during onboarding, but I want to plant the seed. Show me how to reference the next level offer within helpful onboarding content so customers become aware of it without feeling sold to before they have even gotten value. Give me the language to seed it and the right moment in onboarding to do so. The seed should feel like helpful information about what is possible, not a pitch. My tone: [TONE].
description: Seed a future upsell during onboarding without selling too early.
useCase: Use this to plant upsell awareness during onboarding. Seeding the next level offer as helpful information, rather than a pitch, means customers already know what is possible when the right upsell moment arrives, warming future expansion without souring the early relationship.

title: Write a Value First Upsell
promptText: Write an upsell to customers of [INITIAL PURCHASE] that leads with genuine value before making the offer for [UPSELL OFFER]. Give me a message that first delivers a useful tip, insight, or quick win related to [TOPIC] that helps them regardless, then transitions naturally to the upsell as the deeper or fuller version of that help. The value must stand alone so the message is worth reading even if they never buy. This keeps my upsells welcome rather than resented. Value first, offer second. My tone: [TONE].
description: Write an upsell that delivers real value before ever making the offer.
useCase: Use this as your default upsell style to keep offers welcome. Leading with a genuine tip or win before the offer means every upsell message is worth opening, which trains customers to read your emails even knowing they contain offers, the opposite of upsell fatigue.

title: Upsell a One Off Client to a Retainer
promptText: Write a message to a client who hired me for a one off project of [PROJECT] proposing an ongoing retainer or package of [RETAINER OFFER]. The project went well: [PROJECT RESULT]. Write it to show the value of continuity, the results we could achieve with ongoing work versus one and done, and how a retainer serves their goals better than repeat one off projects. Frame it around their continued success and make the ongoing arrangement feel like the natural evolution of a good working relationship. Include what the retainer covers. My tone: [TONE].
description: Convert a satisfied one off client into ongoing retainer revenue.
useCase: Use this after a successful one off project to propose ongoing work. Retainers provide the predictable recurring income solo businesses need, and pitching continuity right after a good result, framed as the natural next step, converts far better than waiting until the client has moved on.

title: Write Upsell Section Copy for a Page
promptText: Write copy for an upsell or recommended offer section that appears on [PAGE, E.G. A THANK YOU PAGE, ACCOUNT DASHBOARD, OR PRODUCT PAGE]. The customer context is [WHAT THEY JUST DID OR OWN], and the recommended offer is [UPSELL OFFER]. Write a headline and short supporting copy that catches attention and makes the relevant next offer appealing without disrupting their experience on the page. It should read as a helpful recommendation the customer is glad to see. Keep it concise for on page placement. Give me 2 variations. My tone: [TONE].
description: Write on page upsell copy that recommends the next offer without disrupting flow.
useCase: Use this for upsell placements on thank you pages, dashboards, or product pages. Concise on page copy that recommends a relevant next offer captures attention at natural browsing moments, adding a passive revenue stream that works without any outreach on your part.
variables: PAGE, E.G. A THANK YOU PAGE, ACCOUNT DASHBOARD, OR PRODUCT PAGE
WHAT THEY JUST DID OR OWN

title: Personalize an Upsell to the Customer
promptText: Help me personalize an upsell for a specific customer. What I know about them: [CUSTOMER CONTEXT, THEIR PURCHASE, GOALS, OR BEHAVIOR]. The upsell I want to offer is [UPSELL OFFER]. Write a personalized message that references their specific situation, ties the upsell directly to their stated or evident goals, and shows I actually know who they are rather than blasting the same offer to everyone. Personalized upsells convert far better because they feel relevant and considered. Make it feel one to one. My tone: [TONE].
description: Personalize an upsell to a specific customer's situation and goals.
useCase: Use this for high value customers who deserve a tailored offer. Personalized upsells that reference the customer's actual situation and goals convert far better than mass offers, because relevance signals you know and value them specifically rather than treating them as a number.
variables: CUSTOMER CONTEXT, THEIR PURCHASE, GOALS, OR BEHAVIOR

title: Frame an Upsell with Price Anchoring
promptText: Help me frame an upsell for [UPSELL OFFER] at [UPSELL PRICE] to customers of [INITIAL PURCHASE] using honest price anchoring. Show me how to establish the value context so the upsell price feels small or reasonable by comparison: anchoring against the cost of the problem it solves, the value it delivers, or what they already invested. Give me the framing and language to make the upsell feel like an obvious good deal without deceptive or fake anchoring. The math should be real. Ethical anchoring, not manipulation. My tone: [TONE].
description: Frame an upsell price with honest anchoring so it feels like an obvious deal.
useCase: Use this to make an upsell price feel reasonable. Honest anchoring against the value delivered or the cost of the problem makes the price feel small by comparison, which lifts conversion without the deceptive fake discounts that erode trust when customers notice them.

title: Add Honest Urgency to an Upsell
promptText: Help me add genuine urgency to an upsell for [UPSELL OFFER] to customers of [INITIAL PURCHASE]. The real reason to act now is [HONEST REASON, E.G. A REAL DEADLINE, LIMITED SPOTS, OR AN EXPIRING BONUS]. Write upsell messaging that conveys this urgency truthfully so customers act rather than deferring indefinitely, without any fake scarcity or manufactured pressure. Show me how to make the honest deadline motivating. If there is no genuine reason for urgency, tell me and suggest an ethical way to create one. Honest and effective. My tone: [TONE].
description: Add genuine, non manipulative urgency to an upsell so customers act.
useCase: Use this when a real reason to act now exists on an upsell. Honest urgency around a genuine deadline or limited availability motivates customers past indefinite deferral, while avoiding the fake scarcity that customers increasingly recognize and resent when they spot it.
variables: HONEST REASON, E.G. A REAL DEADLINE, LIMITED SPOTS, OR AN EXPIRING BONUS

title: Build an Upsell Menu for Your Business
promptText: Help me build a full upsell and cross sell map for my business. My core offers are [YOUR OFFERS], and my customers typically start with [ENTRY OFFER]. Map out: the logical upsell and cross sell path from each offer to the next, which offers pair naturally, what the ideal customer journey through my offers looks like, and where I am currently leaving money on the table by not offering an obvious next step. Give me a clear map I can use to know exactly what to offer each customer next based on what they have bought. My audience: [TARGET AUDIENCE].
description: Map the full upsell and cross sell paths across your entire offer suite.
useCase: Use this once to design your whole ascension strategy. A clear map of what to offer each customer next based on what they bought reveals where you are leaving money on the table, and turns ad hoc upselling into a deliberate journey that maximizes customer lifetime value.

title: Refine an Upsell So It Does Not Feel Pushy
promptText: Here is my upsell message: [PASTE YOUR UPSELL MESSAGE]. It feels too salesy or pushy. Rewrite it to feel like a genuine helpful recommendation from someone who cares about the customer's success. Remove pressure tactics, hype, and anything that centers my revenue over their benefit. Keep it persuasive but make the customer feel looked after, not sold to. Show me the refined version and note what specifically made the original feel pushy so I learn to avoid it. My offer context: [OFFER CONTEXT], my tone: [TONE].
description: Rewrite a pushy upsell into a genuine, customer first recommendation.
useCase: Use this on any upsell that feels too salesy before sending. Pushy upsells damage the customer relationships that are your most valuable asset, and rewriting to center their benefit keeps the persuasion while removing the pressure that makes existing customers feel like targets.

title: Outline a How To Blog Post
promptText: Outline a how to blog post teaching [TARGET AUDIENCE] how to [ACHIEVE SPECIFIC OUTCOME]. Give me: a working title, a hook intro that names the problem and promises the outcome, the logical step by step sections a reader needs to follow, a note on what to include in each step, a common mistakes or troubleshooting section, and a conclusion with a clear next action. Structure it so a beginner could actually follow it to the result. Note where an example, screenshot, or template would strengthen a section. My topic area: [YOUR TOPIC].
description: Outline a step by step how to post a beginner can actually follow to the result.
useCase: Use this to plan how to content, which ranks well in search because it matches high intent queries. A clear step by step structure with troubleshooting built in produces a post readers finish and act on, which is what earns rankings and shares.

title: Outline a List Blog Post
promptText: Outline a list blog post titled around [NUMBER] [THINGS, E.G. TOOLS, TIPS, MISTAKES, OR STRATEGIES] for [TARGET AUDIENCE] about [TOPIC]. Give me: a title with the number, an intro that sets up why this list matters and promises value, the list items each with a working subhead and a one line note on what to cover, and a conclusion that ties them together with a next step. Make sure each item is distinct and genuinely useful, not filler to hit the number. Order them for impact. Note which items deserve more depth. My tone: [TONE].
description: Outline a list post where every item is distinct and genuinely useful.
useCase: Use this to plan list content, which is highly shareable and scannable. A well ordered list where each item earns its place, rather than padding to hit a number, keeps readers scrolling and delivers the quick wins that make list posts get saved and shared.
variables: THINGS, E.G. TOOLS, TIPS, MISTAKES, OR STRATEGIES

title: Outline an Ultimate Beginner Guide
promptText: Outline a comprehensive beginner guide to [TOPIC] for [TARGET AUDIENCE] who are starting from zero. Give me: a title, an intro that reassures beginners and sets expectations, a logical progression from the absolute basics through to being able to take real action, section headers that build on each other, and notes on what each section covers including any terms to define. Include a getting started or next steps section. This should be the definitive resource a beginner bookmarks. Note where visuals, examples, or a glossary would help. My tone: [TONE].
description: Outline a definitive beginner guide that takes readers from zero to action.
useCase: Use this to create pillar content that ranks for broad topic searches and earns backlinks. A comprehensive beginner guide becomes a bookmarked resource and an authority signal, anchoring your site's topical relevance and attracting the links that lift your whole domain.

title: Outline a Pillar Content Post
promptText: Outline a comprehensive pillar post on [BROAD TOPIC] for [TARGET AUDIENCE] that will anchor a cluster of related content. Give me: a title targeting the main topic, an intro establishing this as the complete resource, the major subtopics that comprehensively cover the theme, and for each subtopic a note on what to cover plus which could become its own linked article later. Structure it so it covers the topic broadly while leaving room to link out to deeper cluster posts. This is my topic authority hub. Note the internal links to plan. My related subtopics: [RELATED SUBTOPICS OR WRITE SUGGEST].
description: Outline a pillar post that anchors a topic cluster and plans internal links.
useCase: Use this to build a topic cluster strategy, the backbone of modern SEO. A pillar post that comprehensively covers a broad topic and links to deeper cluster articles signals topical authority to search engines, lifting rankings across the whole cluster.

title: Outline an SEO Optimized Blog Post
promptText: Outline an SEO optimized blog post targeting the keyword [TARGET KEYWORD] for [TARGET AUDIENCE]. Give me: an SEO friendly title using the keyword naturally, an intro that satisfies search intent fast, section headers structured as the subtopics a searcher wants answered including related questions people also ask, notes on where to naturally include the keyword and related terms, and a conclusion. Match the likely search intent behind the keyword: [INTENT, E.G. INFORMATIONAL OR COMMERCIAL, OR WRITE INFER]. Structure it to genuinely answer the query better than existing results. Note where to add depth competitors miss.
description: Outline a blog post structured to match search intent and rank for a keyword.
useCase: Use this to plan content built to rank. Structuring the outline around search intent and the questions searchers actually ask, rather than just stuffing a keyword, is what earns rankings, since search engines reward posts that fully satisfy the query.
variables: INTENT, E.G. INFORMATIONAL OR COMMERCIAL, OR WRITE INFER

title: Write Blog Post Intro Hooks
promptText: Write 5 intro hooks for a blog post about [TOPIC] for [TARGET AUDIENCE]. The post delivers [WHAT THE POST DELIVERS]. Give me different opening approaches: one that opens with a surprising statistic or fact, one with a relatable problem scenario, one with a bold claim, one with a question that names their pain, and one that starts mid story. Each should hook the reader in the first two sentences and make them want to keep reading. Keep them tight, no throat clearing or in today's world openers. Mark the strongest for this topic. My tone: [TONE].
description: Write five different intro hooks that stop readers from bouncing.
useCase: Use this because the intro determines whether anyone reads your post. Bounce rate is a ranking and engagement signal, and testing different hook styles that grab readers in the first two sentences keeps them on the page long enough for the content to work.

title: Outline a Problem Solution Blog Post
promptText: Outline a problem solution blog post for [TARGET AUDIENCE] who struggle with [THE PROBLEM] that my [YOUR OFFER OR APPROACH] addresses. Give me: a title that names the problem or promises the solution, an intro that agitates the problem so readers feel understood, a section exploring why the problem happens and why common fixes fail, the solution presented clearly with how to apply it, and a conclusion that points toward [NEXT STEP, E.G. MY OFFER OR A FREE RESOURCE]. Structure it to build the case for the solution naturally. Keep it genuinely helpful, not a thinly veiled pitch.
description: Outline a problem solution post that builds the case for your approach.
useCase: Use this to create content that attracts your ideal buyers and warms them toward your offer. A post that deeply understands the problem and presents your approach as the solution draws in exactly the people who need what you sell, without reading as a pitch.
variables: NEXT STEP, E.G. MY OFFER OR A FREE RESOURCE

title: Outline a Comparison Blog Post
promptText: Outline a comparison blog post comparing [OPTION A] versus [OPTION B] for [TARGET AUDIENCE] trying to decide between them. Give me: a title, an intro that frames the decision and who each option suits, the key comparison criteria that actually matter to the reader, a section on each option covering strengths and weaknesses honestly, a clear recommendation for different situations rather than a single winner, and a conclusion helping them choose. Structure it to be genuinely helpful and balanced so readers trust it. Note where a comparison table would help. My angle or expertise: [YOUR ANGLE].
description: Outline a balanced comparison post that helps readers choose between options.
useCase: Use this to capture high intent comparison searches, where readers are close to a decision. A genuinely balanced comparison earns trust and ranks well, and offering situational recommendations rather than one forced winner makes readers rely on your judgment.

title: Outline a Case Study Blog Post
promptText: Outline a case study blog post about how [CLIENT OR SUBJECT] achieved [RESULT] using [APPROACH OR YOUR OFFER]. Give me: a results focused title, an intro that hooks with the outcome or the starting struggle, a section on the starting situation and challenge, the approach or process taken, the results with specifics and numbers, and the lessons or takeaways a reader can apply. Structure it as a story that also teaches, so readers get value even if they never buy. Note where quotes, data, or visuals strengthen it. My audience: [TARGET AUDIENCE].
description: Outline a case study post that reads as a story and teaches transferable lessons.
useCase: Use this to turn client results into content that both proves your value and teaches. A case study structured as a story with transferable lessons attracts readers and provides powerful social proof, showing prospects the results you deliver in a format they actually read.

title: Outline an Expert Roundup Post
promptText: Outline an expert roundup or resource roundup blog post on [TOPIC] for [TARGET AUDIENCE]. Give me: a title, an intro on why this collection is valuable, the structure for presenting each expert opinion or resource, a note on what to include for each entry, and a synthesis section pulling out common themes or my own takeaways. If it is an expert roundup, include the single question to ask contributors. Structure it so it is genuinely useful and not just a link dump. Note how this could attract backlinks or shares from those featured. My angle: [YOUR ANGLE].
description: Outline a roundup post that collects expert input or resources with real synthesis.
useCase: Use this to create content that attracts backlinks and shares from those featured. A roundup with genuine synthesis, rather than a link dump, provides value while giving contributors a reason to share it, which can drive traffic and links from their audiences to your site.

title: Outline an Opinion or Thought Leadership Post
promptText: Outline an opinion or thought leadership blog post arguing [YOUR POSITION] on [TOPIC] for [TARGET AUDIENCE]. Give me: a title that signals a clear stance, an intro that states the position boldly and why it matters, the supporting arguments and evidence for my view, a fair treatment of the counterargument and my response to it, and a conclusion that leaves the reader with a shifted perspective or a call to think differently. Structure it to be persuasive and credible, not a rant. My reasoning and evidence: [YOUR REASONING AND EVIDENCE]. My tone: [TONE].
description: Outline a thought leadership post that argues a clear position credibly.
useCase: Use this to build authority and stand out with a distinct point of view. Opinion posts that argue a clear position credibly, including a fair treatment of the counterargument, differentiate you from the generic advice everyone publishes and attract an audience that values your perspective.

title: Outline a Story Driven Blog Post
promptText: Outline a story driven blog post built around this experience: [YOUR STORY OR EXPERIENCE]. The lesson or point for [TARGET AUDIENCE] is [THE LESSON]. Give me: a title that intrigues, an intro that drops into the story, the narrative arc with its turning point, the pivot from story to the broader lesson, how the lesson applies to the reader, and a conclusion. Structure it so the story earns attention and the lesson delivers value, blending both so it is neither a pointless anecdote nor a dry lecture. Note the emotional beats. My tone: [TONE].
description: Outline a post that weaves a personal story into a valuable lesson.
useCase: Use this to create memorable content that builds connection. Story driven posts hold attention and stick in memory better than straight advice, and blending a personal narrative with a transferable lesson makes your content both engaging and useful, deepening the reader relationship.

title: Outline an FAQ Style Blog Post
promptText: Outline an FAQ style blog post answering the common questions [TARGET AUDIENCE] have about [TOPIC]. Give me: a title, a brief intro, the key questions organized logically from basic to advanced, and a note on what each answer should cover. Include the questions people actually search for and ask, including the ones they are afraid to ask. Structure it so it can rank for question based searches and be eligible for featured snippets. Note which questions deserve the most depth. My relevant expertise: [YOUR EXPERTISE], my tone: [TONE].
description: Outline an FAQ post structured to rank for question searches and snippets.
useCase: Use this to capture the growing share of question based and voice searches. An FAQ post structured around the questions people actually ask can win featured snippets and answer boxes, putting your content at the very top of relevant search results.

title: Outline a Post from a Keyword Cluster
promptText: Here is a cluster of related keywords I want one blog post to target: [PASTE KEYWORDS]. My audience: [TARGET AUDIENCE]. Outline a single post that naturally covers this cluster: a primary title targeting the main keyword, sections that each address related keywords and the search intent behind them, and a structure that weaves the terms in naturally without keyword stuffing. Group the keywords logically into sections and note which is the primary term versus supporting terms. The goal is one strong post that ranks for the whole cluster rather than thin separate posts. Note the search intent to satisfy.
description: Outline one strong post that targets a whole cluster of related keywords.
useCase: Use this to consolidate related keywords into one authoritative post instead of many thin ones. Targeting a cluster with a single comprehensive post concentrates your ranking power and matches how search engines now group related queries, outperforming scattered shallow pages.

title: Outline a Checklist Blog Post
promptText: Outline a checklist blog post for [TARGET AUDIENCE] on how to [TASK OR PROCESS]. Give me: a title, an intro on why this checklist saves them time or prevents mistakes, the checklist items in the correct logical or chronological order, a short note on what each item involves, and a conclusion offering the checklist as a downloadable resource for a lead magnet. Structure it so the post is genuinely usable and the downloadable version is worth opting in for. Note which items need extra explanation. My topic: [YOUR TOPIC], my tone: [TONE].
description: Outline a checklist post that doubles as a lead magnet opt in.
useCase: Use this to create practical content that also builds your email list. A checklist post is immediately useful and the downloadable version gives readers a reason to opt in, turning content that ranks and helps into a lead generation asset at the same time.

title: Write a Blog Conclusion with a Call to Action
promptText: Write a strong conclusion for a blog post about [TOPIC] aimed at [TARGET AUDIENCE]. The post covered [KEY POINTS], and the action I want readers to take next is [DESIRED ACTION, E.G. SUBSCRIBE, DOWNLOAD, OR READ ANOTHER POST]. Write a conclusion that briefly reinforces the main takeaway without dully summarizing, leaves the reader with a memorable final thought or motivation, and transitions naturally into the call to action so it feels earned rather than tacked on. Give me 2 versions with different CTA approaches. Keep it tight. My tone: [TONE].
description: Write a blog conclusion that reinforces the point and earns the next action.
useCase: Use this to convert readers at the end of a post, the moment most likely to drive action. A conclusion that reinforces the takeaway and transitions naturally into a call to action turns passive readers into subscribers or leads, capturing value from the attention you earned.
variables: DESIRED ACTION, E.G. SUBSCRIBE, DOWNLOAD, OR READ ANOTHER POST

title: Outline a Post Answering One Question
promptText: Outline a blog post that thoroughly answers this single question from [TARGET AUDIENCE]: [THE QUESTION]. Give me: a title that mirrors how they search for or ask this, an intro that gives the short answer immediately then promises depth, sections that fully explore the answer including nuances and related sub questions, and a conclusion. Structure it to satisfy the query completely so the reader does not need another source, which is what search engines reward. Front load the direct answer for snippet eligibility. Note related questions to address. My expertise: [YOUR EXPERTISE].
description: Outline a post that completely answers one search question, snippet ready.
useCase: Use this to target specific question searches with content built to fully satisfy them. Answering a single question completely, with the direct answer front loaded, wins featured snippets and keeps readers from bouncing to another source, both of which search engines reward.

title: Outline a Contrarian Take Blog Post
promptText: Outline a contrarian blog post challenging the common belief that [COMMON BELIEF] in [YOUR INDUSTRY], for [TARGET AUDIENCE]. My actual position: [YOUR POSITION]. Give me: a title that signals the contrarian angle, an intro that states the widely held belief then teases why it is wrong, the argument dismantling the common view with evidence and reasoning, what to do instead, and a conclusion. Structure it to be provocative but credible and well argued, not contrarian for its own sake. Acknowledge where the common belief has some merit. My evidence: [YOUR EVIDENCE OR REASONING], my tone: [TONE].
description: Outline a contrarian post that credibly challenges a common industry belief.
useCase: Use this to stand out and spark discussion by challenging conventional wisdom. A credible contrarian post that dismantles a common belief with real reasoning gets shared and debated, driving traffic and positioning you as an independent thinker rather than an echo of everyone else.

title: Turn Rough Notes into a Blog Outline
promptText: Here are my rough notes and thoughts on [TOPIC]: [PASTE YOUR NOTES]. Turn them into a structured blog post outline for [TARGET AUDIENCE]. Organize my scattered ideas into a logical flow, identify the strongest angle or thesis buried in the notes, suggest a title, structure the points into coherent sections, and flag any gaps where the argument needs more support or an example. Keep my original ideas and insights intact, just give them structure. Tell me if the notes suggest more than one post. My tone: [TONE].
description: Turn scattered notes into a structured, coherent blog outline.
useCase: Use this to convert raw thinking into publishable structure. The best posts come from your own ideas and observations, and this organizes scattered notes into a coherent outline while preserving your insights, turning the hard part of writing into a simple execution task.

title: Turn a Transcript into a Blog Outline
promptText: Here is a transcript from my [PODCAST, VIDEO, OR TALK] on [TOPIC]: [PASTE TRANSCRIPT OR KEY POINTS]. Turn it into a blog post outline for [TARGET AUDIENCE]. Extract the strongest ideas and structure them into a coherent post rather than a literal transcription, identify the best thesis, organize the points logically, suggest a title, and note where the spoken content needs tightening or added context to work as writing. The goal is a blog post that captures the value of what I said, restructured for readers. Flag the most quotable moments. My tone: [TONE].
description: Repurpose a podcast, video, or talk transcript into a structured blog outline.
useCase: Use this to get blog content from audio or video you already created. Repurposing a transcript into a structured post extends the life of your best spoken content, reaching readers who will never listen and adding searchable text that drives traffic to your site.

title: Outline a Multi Part Blog Series
promptText: Outline a multi part blog series on [BROAD TOPIC] for [TARGET AUDIENCE], broken into [NUMBER OF PARTS] posts. Give me: an overarching series title and premise, the individual post titles and what each covers, how they build on each other in a logical sequence, and how each post stands alone while encouraging readers to continue the series. Note the internal linking between parts and how to hook readers to return for the next installment. Structure it so the series comprehensively covers the topic and keeps readers coming back. My angle: [YOUR ANGLE].
description: Outline a multi part blog series that keeps readers returning for each part.
useCase: Use this to plan a content series that builds an engaged returning audience. A well structured series covers a topic comprehensively while training readers to come back, and the internal linking between parts keeps visitors on your site longer, which helps both engagement and SEO.

title: Outline a Data or Research Blog Post
promptText: Outline a data driven blog post using this data or research: [PASTE YOUR DATA, SURVEY RESULTS, OR FINDINGS]. My audience: [TARGET AUDIENCE]. Give me: a title that highlights the most surprising or valuable finding, an intro on why this data matters to the reader, sections that present the findings with interpretation and implications, and a conclusion with actionable takeaways. Structure it so the data tells a story and leads to insight, not just numbers on a page. Note where charts or visuals would strengthen each finding and which stat is most quotable and link worthy. My tone: [TONE].
description: Outline a data driven post that turns findings into an insightful story.
useCase: Use this to turn original data or research into high value content that earns links. Data driven posts attract backlinks because others cite the findings, and structuring the data as a story with clear takeaways makes it both authoritative and genuinely useful to readers.
variables: PASTE YOUR DATA, SURVEY RESULTS, OR FINDINGS

title: Outline a Post That Beats Competing Content
promptText: I want to write a better post on [TOPIC] than what currently ranks. Here is what the top existing posts cover: [PASTE OR DESCRIBE COMPETING CONTENT]. My audience: [TARGET AUDIENCE], my unique angle or expertise: [YOUR ANGLE]. Outline a post that outdoes them: identify the gaps and weaknesses in the existing content, the questions they leave unanswered, and where I can add depth, better examples, more recent information, or a unique perspective. Give me a title and structure designed to be the most complete and useful resource on this topic. Note my unfair advantages to emphasize.
description: Outline a post engineered to outrank and outdo the existing top content.
useCase: Use this to create content built to beat what already ranks. Analyzing the gaps and weaknesses in top posts, then outlining something more complete and useful, is the core of content that earns rankings, since search engines reward the best answer, not just another one.

title: Refine and Strengthen a Blog Outline
promptText: Here is my blog post outline: [PASTE YOUR OUTLINE]. My audience: [TARGET AUDIENCE], the goal of the post: [POST GOAL]. Review and strengthen it. Tell me: where the flow breaks or sections are out of order, where the argument or value is thin, what important angle or point is missing, where I could cut redundancy, and whether the structure will hold a reader's attention start to finish. Then give me the improved outline. Also assess whether the title and intro approach are strong enough to earn the click and the read. Be direct about weaknesses.
description: Audit and strengthen a blog outline before you invest in writing it.
useCase: Use this before writing to fix structural problems while they are cheap to fix. Catching weak flow, thin sections, and missing angles at the outline stage saves you from writing a full post on a flawed foundation, and produces a stronger piece with less rework.

title: Generate SEO Titles for a Post
promptText: Generate 12 SEO title options for a post targeting the keyword [TARGET KEYWORD] for [TARGET AUDIENCE]. Keep each under 60 characters so it does not truncate in search results. Include the keyword naturally near the front, and give me a mix: some straightforward and clear, some with a number or year, some with a curiosity or benefit angle, and some phrased as the question searchers ask. Each must accurately match the content, no clickbait the page cannot deliver. Mark the 3 with the best balance of keyword relevance and click appeal, and note the character count for each.
description: Generate a spread of under 60 character SEO titles that balance ranking and clicks.
useCase: Use this on every post before publishing. The title is the single biggest on page factor for both ranking and click through, and testing a spread of angles under the truncation limit finds the one that earns the click without overpromising.

title: Write Meta Descriptions That Earn Clicks
promptText: Write 5 meta description options for a page about [PAGE TOPIC] targeting the keyword [TARGET KEYWORD] for [TARGET AUDIENCE]. Keep each between 140 and 155 characters so it does not truncate. Include the keyword naturally, lead with the benefit or answer the searcher wants, and end with a subtle reason to click, such as what they will learn or get. Write them as ad copy for the search result, not a summary. Match the actual page content. Mark the strongest and give the character count for each. My tone: [TONE].
description: Write meta descriptions that read as search ad copy and drive click through.
useCase: Use this for every page you want ranking. The meta description is your ad copy in search results, and a benefit led description within the character limit lifts click through rate, which both drives traffic and signals relevance to search engines.

title: Optimize a Title for Click Through Rate
promptText: Here is my current title: [PASTE TITLE] for a post targeting [TARGET KEYWORD]. It ranks but the click through rate is low. Rewrite it to earn more clicks without losing keyword relevance or becoming clickbait. Give me 8 stronger versions using proven CTR levers: numbers, brackets or parentheses with a qualifier, power words, specificity, curiosity gaps that the content actually closes, and the year if relevant. Keep each under 60 characters. Explain which lever each version uses so I learn the patterns. My audience: [TARGET AUDIENCE].
description: Rewrite an underperforming title to lift click through without losing relevance.
useCase: Use this on pages that rank but get few clicks, a common and fixable problem. Applying proven click through levers like specificity, numbers, and honest curiosity to a ranking title captures the traffic you are already positioned for but leaving on the table.

title: Write a Matched Title and Meta Pair
promptText: Write a matched title and meta description pair for a page about [PAGE TOPIC] targeting [TARGET KEYWORD] for [TARGET AUDIENCE]. The title should be under 60 characters and the meta between 140 and 155, and together they should read as one coherent search result that promises a clear benefit and earns the click. Avoid repeating the exact same words between them, let them complement each other so the meta expands on the title rather than echoing it. Give me 3 complete pairs with character counts. My tone: [TONE].
description: Write title and meta pairs that work together as one coherent search result.
useCase: Use this to optimize the full search snippet rather than title and meta in isolation. A title and meta that complement each other, rather than repeat, present a coherent compelling result that maximizes click through from the search page.

title: Write Meta Descriptions by Page Type
promptText: Write meta descriptions tailored to my page type: [PAGE TYPE, E.G. HOMEPAGE, PRODUCT PAGE, BLOG POST, SERVICE PAGE, OR CATEGORY PAGE]. The page is about [PAGE TOPIC] targeting [TARGET KEYWORD] for [TARGET AUDIENCE]. Different page types need different meta approaches, so write it to match the intent of this page type: a homepage sells the brand, a product page drives to buy, a blog post promises value, a service page converts. Keep it 140 to 155 characters with the keyword and the right call to action for this page type. Give me 3 options with counts. My tone: [TONE].
description: Write meta descriptions matched to the intent of each specific page type.
useCase: Use this because a homepage, product page, and blog post each need a different meta approach. Matching the description to the page type's intent, whether selling a brand or promising value, produces snippets that convert the specific searcher each page targets.
variables: PAGE TYPE, E.G. HOMEPAGE, PRODUCT PAGE, BLOG POST, SERVICE PAGE, OR CATEGORY PAGE

title: Write Homepage Title and Meta
promptText: Write the title tag and meta description for my homepage. My business: [BUSINESS NAME] which helps [TARGET AUDIENCE] with [WHAT YOU DO]. My main keyword or category: [MAIN KEYWORD], my key differentiator: [DIFFERENTIATOR]. The title should include my business name and what I do or the main keyword within 60 characters. The meta should sell the brand in 140 to 155 characters, making clear who I help and why to choose me, with a reason to click through. Give me 3 title and meta pairs with character counts. My tone: [TONE].
description: Write a homepage title and meta that establish the brand and earn the click.
useCase: Use this for your homepage, which often ranks for your brand and main category terms. A homepage title and meta that clearly state who you help and why to choose you convert branded and category searchers, making a strong first impression in results.

title: Write Product Page Title and Meta
promptText: Write title and meta options for a product page for [PRODUCT NAME], which is [WHAT IT IS] for [TARGET AUDIENCE]. The main keyword is [TARGET KEYWORD] and the key selling point is [SELLING POINT]. The title should include the product and keyword under 60 characters. The meta should drive the searcher to click and buy in 140 to 155 characters, leading with the benefit or selling point and including any purchase motivator like [PURCHASE MOTIVATOR, E.G. FREE SHIPPING OR A GUARANTEE, OR WRITE NONE]. Give me 3 pairs with character counts.
description: Write product page titles and metas that drive commercial intent clicks to buy.
useCase: Use this for product or offer pages targeting buyers. A product page title and meta that lead with the selling point and a purchase motivator capture commercial intent searchers ready to buy, converting search visibility directly into sales.
variables: PURCHASE MOTIVATOR, E.G. FREE SHIPPING OR A GUARANTEE, OR WRITE NONE

title: Write Service Page Title and Meta
promptText: Write title and meta options for a service page for [SERVICE NAME], which helps [TARGET AUDIENCE] with [PROBLEM OR OUTCOME]. The main keyword is [TARGET KEYWORD], and if I serve a specific area include [LOCATION, OR WRITE NONE]. The title should include the service and keyword under 60 characters. The meta should convert a searcher looking for this service in 140 to 155 characters, leading with the outcome and including a reason to choose me and a soft call to action. Give me 3 pairs with character counts. My tone: [TONE].
description: Write service page titles and metas that convert searchers into inquiries.
useCase: Use this for service pages, often the highest value pages for a solo business. A service page title and meta optimized for the searcher looking to hire convert commercial intent into inquiries, and local terms capture nearby searchers ready to buy.

title: Write Local SEO Titles and Metas
promptText: Write local SEO title and meta options for my [BUSINESS TYPE] serving [CITY OR AREA]. The service is [SERVICE] and the main local keyword is [KEYWORD PLUS LOCATION, E.G. PLUMBER IN AUSTIN]. The title should include the service and location naturally under 60 characters. The meta should convert a local searcher in 140 to 155 characters, signaling local presence, the benefit, and a call to action like call or book. Include a local trust signal if relevant: [LOCAL TRUST SIGNAL, E.G. YEARS SERVING THE AREA, OR WRITE NONE]. Give me 4 pairs with character counts.
description: Write location targeted titles and metas that win local search clicks.
useCase: Use this for any business serving a specific area. Local search has high commercial intent and less competition, and titles and metas that clearly signal your service plus location capture nearby searchers actively looking to hire or buy right now.
variables: KEYWORD PLUS LOCATION, E.G. PLUMBER IN AUSTIN
LOCAL TRUST SIGNAL, E.G. YEARS SERVING THE AREA, OR WRITE NONE

title: Create A/B Title Variations to Test
promptText: My page targeting [TARGET KEYWORD] has this title: [PASTE CURRENT TITLE]. Create A/B test variations so I can learn what lifts click through. Give me 4 variations that each change one meaningful element: one changes the emotional angle, one adds or changes a number, one changes the value proposition, and one changes the framing from statement to question. Keep all under 60 characters and keyword relevant. For each, tell me what it is testing and what a win would indicate about my audience. My audience: [TARGET AUDIENCE].
description: Create controlled title variations that isolate one variable to test click through.
useCase: Use this to systematically improve titles through testing rather than guessing. Changing one element at a time reveals what actually lifts click through for your audience, turning title writing from art into a learnable pattern backed by real data.

title: Refresh Titles and Metas on Old Content
promptText: Here are the current titles and metas for some older pages that have lost rankings or clicks: [PASTE CURRENT TITLES AND METAS WITH TOPICS]. My audience: [TARGET AUDIENCE]. Refresh each one to be more compelling and current: update the year if present, sharpen the click appeal, ensure the keyword is well placed, and align it with current search intent. For each page, give me the improved title and meta with character counts, and a one line note on what you changed and why. Prioritize the pages where a refresh would have the biggest impact.
description: Refresh titles and metas on aging pages to recover lost rankings and clicks.
useCase: Use this to revive older content that has slipped in rankings or click through. Refreshing titles and metas is one of the fastest SEO wins available, recovering traffic on pages you already published without rewriting the content itself.
variables: PASTE CURRENT TITLES AND METAS WITH TOPICS

title: Write Distinct Title Tag and H1 Headline
promptText: For my post on [TOPIC] targeting [TARGET KEYWORD], write a title tag and a separate H1 headline that work together but are not identical. The title tag under 60 characters is optimized for the search result and click through, while the H1 is the on page headline optimized to hook the reader who already clicked and confirm they are in the right place. Explain why they should differ slightly and how each serves its distinct job. Give me 3 pairs. My audience: [TARGET AUDIENCE], my tone: [TONE].
description: Write a title tag and H1 that complement each other and serve their distinct jobs.
useCase: Use this to optimize the two headlines that do different jobs. The title tag earns the click in search while the H1 confirms and hooks the reader on the page, and writing them distinctly lets each serve its purpose rather than wasting the H1 as a duplicate.

title: Add Calls to Action to Meta Descriptions
promptText: Write meta descriptions with strong calls to action for a page about [PAGE TOPIC] targeting [TARGET KEYWORD]. The action I want is [DESIRED ACTION, E.G. LEARN, SHOP, DISCOVER, GET, OR BOOK]. Give me 6 metas each 140 to 155 characters that lead with value and end with a compelling call to action that fits the page intent. Vary the CTA verbs and the value framing. The CTA should feel earned, not tacked on. Mark which suits an informational page versus a commercial one. Character counts included. My tone: [TONE].
description: Write meta descriptions with earned calls to action matched to page intent.
useCase: Use this to lift click through by ending metas with a clear next step. A meta that leads with value and closes with the right call to action for the page intent nudges searchers to click, and matching the CTA to informational versus commercial pages keeps it authentic.
variables: DESIRED ACTION, E.G. LEARN, SHOP, DISCOVER, GET, OR BOOK

title: Add Power Words to a Title
promptText: Here is my title: [PASTE TITLE] for a post targeting [TARGET KEYWORD]. Strengthen it with power words that add emotional pull without becoming clickbait or hype. Give me 8 versions using different categories of power word: words that convey ease, urgency, exclusivity, results, or curiosity. Keep each under 60 characters and keyword relevant, and ensure the content can back up any promise the power word implies. Explain which emotional lever each version pulls. My audience: [TARGET AUDIENCE].
description: Strengthen a title with power words that add pull without tipping into hype.
useCase: Use this to make a flat title more compelling. The right power word adds emotional pull that lifts click through, and testing different levers like ease, urgency, or curiosity, while keeping promises the content can deliver, sharpens titles without crossing into hype.

title: Place Keywords Naturally in Titles and Metas
promptText: I want to target these keywords on one page: primary [PRIMARY KEYWORD], secondary [SECONDARY KEYWORDS]. The page is about [PAGE TOPIC] for [TARGET AUDIENCE]. Write a title and meta that include the primary keyword prominently and weave in secondary keywords naturally without stuffing, so it reads well to humans while covering the terms. Keep the title under 60 and the meta 140 to 155 characters. Show me where each keyword lands and confirm it still reads naturally. Give me 3 options with character counts. My tone: [TONE].
description: Weave primary and secondary keywords into titles and metas without stuffing.
useCase: Use this when a page targets several related terms. Placing the primary keyword prominently and weaving secondary ones in naturally covers more search variations while keeping the snippet readable, since keyword stuffing hurts both rankings and click through.

title: Generate Titles for a List Post
promptText: Generate 10 title options for a list post covering [NUMBER] [ITEMS, E.G. TOOLS, TIPS, OR STRATEGIES] about [TOPIC] for [TARGET AUDIENCE], targeting [TARGET KEYWORD]. Use the number prominently since list titles perform well in search and social. Vary the angles: some emphasizing the benefit of the list, some the pain it solves, some curiosity, and some the year for freshness. Keep each under 60 characters with the keyword. Note that odd numbers and specific counts often outperform round ones. Mark the strongest 3 and give character counts.
description: Generate list post titles that leverage the number for search and social pull.
useCase: Use this for list content, which performs strongly in both search and social sharing. Numbers in titles set clear expectations and stand out in results, and testing angles around the number finds the version that maximizes clicks for your list post.
variables: ITEMS, E.G. TOOLS, TIPS, OR STRATEGIES

title: Write Question Based Titles and Metas
promptText: Write question based title and meta options for a post answering [THE QUESTION] for [TARGET AUDIENCE], targeting [TARGET KEYWORD]. The title should match how people actually search or ask this question, under 60 characters. The meta should hint at the answer to earn the click while leaving enough to make them read, 140 to 155 characters. This format targets question searches and voice search and can win featured snippets. Give me 3 pairs phrased slightly differently to match varied question phrasings. Character counts included.
description: Write question format titles and metas built for question and voice search.
useCase: Use this for content answering specific questions, targeting the growing share of question and voice searches. Question format titles that mirror how people actually search can win featured snippets and answer boxes, placing your page at the top of results.

title: Compare Emotional and Rational Title Angles
promptText: For my post on [TOPIC] targeting [TARGET KEYWORD] for [TARGET AUDIENCE], write titles from two angles so I can choose what fits. Give me 4 emotional titles that lead with feeling, aspiration, or fear, and 4 rational titles that lead with logic, specifics, or clear value. Keep all under 60 characters and keyword relevant. Then advise which angle likely suits this audience and topic better and why, since some audiences respond to emotion and others to logic. Character counts included. My tone: [TONE].
description: Generate emotional and rational title angles and get guidance on which fits.
useCase: Use this when unsure whether your audience responds better to emotion or logic. Seeing both angles side by side, with guidance on which suits your topic and audience, helps you choose the framing that resonates rather than defaulting to one style out of habit.

title: Fix Truncated Titles and Metas
promptText: These titles and metas are getting cut off in search results: [PASTE TITLES AND METAS]. Rewrite each to fit within limits while keeping the key message and keyword: titles under 60 characters and metas between 140 and 155. Make sure the most important words and the keyword appear before any truncation point, and that each still reads as a complete compelling thought rather than a trimmed sentence. Give me the fixed versions with exact character counts and note what you cut. My audience: [TARGET AUDIENCE].
description: Rewrite truncated titles and metas to fit limits while keeping the key message.
useCase: Use this when your snippets are getting cut off with ellipses in search results, which wastes your message and looks unpolished. Rewriting to fit the limits, with key words front loaded, ensures the full compelling snippet displays and earns the click.

title: Decide How to Use Your Brand in Titles
promptText: Help me decide how to handle my brand name [BRAND NAME] in title tags across my site. My pages include [PAGE TYPES YOU HAVE]. Advise when to include the brand name in the title and when to drop it to save characters for keywords, and give me a consistent title format or template for each page type that balances brand presence with keyword and character limits. Explain the tradeoff between brand recognition and keyword space. Give me the recommended template for each page type with an example. My main keyword focus: [MAIN KEYWORDS].
description: Set a consistent, smart strategy for using your brand name in title tags.
useCase: Use this to make a deliberate site wide decision on brand names in titles. Knowing when the brand earns its character cost versus when to give that space to keywords, with a template per page type, keeps your titles consistent and optimized rather than decided ad hoc.

title: Generate Titles and Metas in Bulk
promptText: I have multiple pages needing titles and metas. Here is the list of page topics and their target keywords: [PASTE LIST OF PAGES WITH TOPICS AND KEYWORDS]. My audience: [TARGET AUDIENCE], my tone: [TONE]. Generate one strong title under 60 characters and one meta between 140 and 155 characters for each page. Keep them varied so the pages do not all sound templated, include each keyword naturally, and make each earn its click. Present them in a clear table with page, title, character count, meta, and character count so I can review and paste them in efficiently.
description: Generate optimized titles and metas for many pages at once in a clean table.
useCase: Use this when optimizing a whole site or a batch of new pages. Generating varied titles and metas in bulk saves hours over doing each individually, and the table format lets you review and implement them efficiently across many pages at once.
variables: PASTE LIST OF PAGES WITH TOPICS AND KEYWORDS

title: Optimize How Your Full Snippet Reads
promptText: Here is my full search snippet: title [PASTE TITLE], URL slug [PASTE SLUG], meta [PASTE META], for a page targeting [TARGET KEYWORD]. Evaluate how the whole snippet reads to a searcher scanning results, since the title, URL, and meta are seen together. Tell me if they work as a unit or send mixed signals, whether the slug is clean and keyword relevant, and how the three elements could better reinforce one coherent, clickable result. Give me improved versions of all three with character counts. My audience: [TARGET AUDIENCE].
description: Optimize title, URL, and meta together as one coherent search snippet.
useCase: Use this to optimize the complete snippet searchers actually see, since title, URL, and meta appear together and are judged as a unit. Ensuring all three reinforce one clear message, rather than sending mixed signals, maximizes the click through from your search listing.

title: Write Clean SEO URL Slugs
promptText: Write a clean SEO friendly URL slug for a page titled [PAGE TITLE] targeting [TARGET KEYWORD]. Give me 4 slug options that are short, readable, include the keyword, use hyphens between words, and drop filler words like a, the, and of. Avoid dates or numbers that will make the URL feel outdated unless essential. Explain which is best and why a short keyword focused slug helps both ranking and click through. Also flag if the current slug I have, [CURRENT SLUG OR WRITE NONE], should be changed or left alone to avoid breaking links.
description: Write short, clean, keyword focused URL slugs that support ranking and clicks.
useCase: Use this when publishing new pages or planning a URL structure. A short clean slug with the keyword supports ranking and looks trustworthy in results, and getting it right at publish time avoids the risk of changing URLs later and breaking existing links.

title: Audit Your Titles and Metas Site Wide
promptText: Here are the titles and metas across my key pages: [PASTE TITLES AND METAS]. Audit them as a set. Flag: any that exceed character limits and will truncate, any that miss their target keyword, duplicate or near duplicate titles competing against each other, weak or missing calls to action in metas, and any that read as templated or fail to earn a click. Prioritize the fixes by likely impact, and give me improved versions for the highest priority pages. My audience: [TARGET AUDIENCE], my main keywords: [MAIN KEYWORDS].
description: Audit all your titles and metas as a set and prioritize the highest impact fixes.
useCase: Use this to find the quick SEO wins hiding across your existing pages. A site wide audit surfaces truncated snippets, missing keywords, and duplicate titles competing against each other, then prioritizes fixes by impact so you improve the pages that matter most first.

title: Generate Lead Magnet Ideas
promptText: Generate 12 lead magnet ideas for [BUSINESS TYPE] serving [TARGET AUDIENCE]. My main offer is [YOUR OFFER] and the problem I solve is [PROBLEM]. For each idea give: the format (checklist, template, guide, swipe file, calculator, mini course, or cheat sheet), the specific title, the exact promise it makes, and why it would attract people who are likely to eventually buy my offer. Prioritize ideas that solve a real quick win and naturally lead toward my paid offer. Mark the 3 easiest to create fast and the 3 most likely to attract buyers. My tone: [TONE].
description: Generate targeted lead magnet ideas that attract likely buyers of your offer.
useCase: Use this when you need to build your email list but do not know what to offer. A lead magnet that solves a quick win and naturally leads toward your paid offer attracts the right people, not just freebie seekers, which is what turns a list into revenue.

title: Build a Checklist Lead Magnet
promptText: Build a checklist lead magnet that helps [TARGET AUDIENCE] accomplish [SPECIFIC OUTCOME OR TASK].
