Give me: a compelling title, a short intro explaining the value and how to use it, the checklist items in the correct logical or chronological order, a brief note under any item that needs explanation, and a closing section pointing to my offer [YOUR OFFER] as the next step. Make it genuinely complete and usable on its own so it builds trust. Keep it focused enough to deliver a real quick win without overwhelming. My tone: [TONE].
description: Build a complete, usable checklist lead magnet that delivers a quick win.
useCase: Use this to create a checklist opt in fast. Checklists are among the highest converting lead magnets because they promise a clear, achievable outcome, and one that genuinely helps builds the trust that makes the next step toward your offer feel natural.

title: Build a Template or Swipe File Lead Magnet
promptText: Build a template or swipe file lead magnet for [TARGET AUDIENCE] who need help with [TASK OR DELIVERABLE]. Give me: a title, a short how to use it intro, the actual template or set of swipeable examples with clear fill in the blank placeholders where they customize, guidance notes so they use it correctly, and a closing that positions my offer [YOUR OFFER] as the deeper help. Make the template immediately usable and genuinely time saving, since a template that works builds instant credibility. My topic area: [YOUR TOPIC], my tone: [TONE].
description: Build a ready to use template or swipe file that saves your audience real time.
useCase: Use this to create a template opt in, one of the stickiest lead magnet types. A template that saves real work delivers instant value and gets used repeatedly, keeping your brand present and building the credibility that makes people trust your paid offer.

title: Outline an Ebook or Guide
promptText: Outline an ebook or comprehensive guide titled around helping [TARGET AUDIENCE] achieve [MAIN OUTCOME]. My expertise: [YOUR EXPERTISE], my offer it should lead toward: [YOUR OFFER]. Give me: a working title and subtitle, an introduction that frames the problem and promises the transformation, the chapters in a logical progression that takes the reader from where they are to the outcome, a note on what each chapter covers, and a conclusion that transitions to my offer. Structure it to be genuinely valuable and complete, positioning me as the expert. Note the ideal length per chapter. My tone: [TONE].
description: Outline a valuable ebook or guide that positions you as the expert.
useCase: Use this to plan a substantial lead magnet or authority building ebook. A well structured guide that delivers a real transformation establishes your expertise and captures higher intent leads, since people who download and read a full guide are more invested than casual opt ins.

title: Write an Ebook Chapter
promptText: Write a chapter for my ebook aimed at [TARGET AUDIENCE]. The chapter topic is [CHAPTER TOPIC] and its goal is to help the reader [CHAPTER GOAL]. Here are the key points to cover: [KEY POINTS]. Write it to teach clearly with examples, keep the reader engaged with a mix of explanation and application, include any relevant framework or step by step, and end with a transition to the next chapter. Match my voice using these notes on my style: [YOUR STYLE OR PASTE A SAMPLE]. Keep it valuable and readable, not padded to seem substantial. Target length: [TARGET LENGTH].
description: Write a clear, engaging ebook chapter that teaches and flows to the next.
useCase: Use this to draft ebook chapters efficiently after outlining. Writing chapter by chapter with your key points and voice keeps a long guide consistent and valuable, turning the daunting task of writing an ebook into manageable focused sections.
variables: YOUR STYLE OR PASTE A SAMPLE

title: Write Lead Magnet Landing Page Copy
promptText: Write landing page copy for my lead magnet [LEAD MAGNET NAME], a [FORMAT] that helps [TARGET AUDIENCE] achieve [OUTCOME]. Give me: a headline that leads with the outcome, a subheadline that adds specificity, 3 to 5 bullet points on what they get and the benefit of each, a short trust or credibility line, and a clear call to action for the opt in. Sell the transformation, not the format, and keep it focused since a lead magnet page should convert fast with minimal friction. Give me 2 headline options to test. My tone: [TONE].
description: Write high converting opt in page copy that sells the lead magnet's outcome.
useCase: Use this for the landing page where visitors decide to give their email. Copy that sells the transformation rather than the format, with clear benefit bullets and low friction, is what converts traffic into subscribers, the entire point of a lead magnet.

title: Generate Lead Magnet Titles
promptText: Generate 15 title options for my lead magnet, a [FORMAT] that helps [TARGET AUDIENCE] achieve [OUTCOME OR SOLVE PROBLEM]. Give me a mix: some that lead with the specific result, some with a timeframe or number, some that name the pain it removes, and some with a curiosity angle. The title should make the value instantly clear and irresistible enough to trade an email for. Avoid vague or generic titles. Mark the 3 strongest and explain what makes each compelling. My tone: [TONE], my topic: [YOUR TOPIC].
description: Generate irresistible lead magnet titles that make the value instantly clear.
useCase: Use this because the title largely determines your opt in rate. A title that makes the value instantly clear and feels worth an email address converts browsers into subscribers, while a vague one lets even a great lead magnet go undownloaded.

title: Write an Ebook Title and Subtitle
promptText: Write title and subtitle options for my ebook that helps [TARGET AUDIENCE] achieve [MAIN OUTCOME] and covers [WHAT IT COVERS]. Give me 8 title and subtitle pairs where the title is memorable and intriguing and the subtitle clarifies the specific promise and audience. Vary the approaches: some benefit driven, some intriguing or metaphorical with a clear subtitle, some straightforward and authoritative. The pair together should signal both appeal and substance. Mark the strongest 3 and explain why. My tone: [TONE].
description: Write ebook title and subtitle pairs that balance intrigue with a clear promise.
useCase: Use this to name an ebook or major guide. A strong title and subtitle pair signals both appeal and substance, which drives downloads and shares, and getting the naming right frames how readers perceive the value of everything inside.

title: Write an Ebook Introduction
promptText: Write the introduction for my ebook that helps [TARGET AUDIENCE] achieve [MAIN OUTCOME]. Give me an intro that hooks the reader immediately, shows I understand their problem and current frustration, promises the transformation the book delivers, briefly establishes why I am qualified to guide them using [YOUR CREDIBILITY], sets expectations for what is ahead, and motivates them to keep reading. Keep it warm and compelling, not a dry preface. The intro should make readers feel they found the right resource. Target length: [TARGET LENGTH], my tone: [TONE].
description: Write an ebook introduction that hooks readers and earns their commitment.
useCase: Use this to write the intro that determines whether people read your ebook. A strong introduction that shows understanding, promises transformation, and establishes credibility earns the reader's commitment, since a guide that gets read builds far more trust than one that gets downloaded and abandoned.

title: Build a Resource List Lead Magnet
promptText: Build a curated resource list lead magnet for [TARGET AUDIENCE] who want to [GOAL]. Give me: a title, a short intro on why this curated list saves them time and confusion, the resources or tools organized into logical categories, a one or two line note on each explaining what it is and when to use it, and a closing pointing to my offer [YOUR OFFER]. My commentary and curation are the value, so make the notes genuinely useful. Here are resources to include or the categories to cover: [RESOURCES OR CATEGORIES]. My tone: [TONE].
description: Build a curated resource list where your commentary is the real value.
useCase: Use this to create a resource list opt in that positions you as a trusted filter. Curation with genuine commentary saves your audience the work of finding and vetting tools themselves, and being the source of the definitive list in your niche is a durable authority position.

title: Design an Email Mini Course Lead Magnet
promptText: Design a free email mini course that helps [TARGET AUDIENCE] achieve [OUTCOME] over [NUMBER OF DAYS OR LESSONS] emails, leading toward my offer [YOUR OFFER]. Give me: a course title and promise, the learning arc across the lessons so each builds on the last, the specific topic and takeaway for each lesson email, and where and how to introduce my offer naturally near the end. Each lesson should deliver one clear win so subscribers stay engaged through the sequence. Note the subject line for each email. My tone: [TONE].
description: Design a multi day email mini course that teaches and warms leads toward your offer.
useCase: Use this to create a mini course lead magnet, which builds deeper engagement than a one time download. A multi day course trains subscribers to open your emails and delivers repeated wins, warming them toward your offer far more effectively than a single freebie.

title: Write Opt In Form Copy
promptText: Write opt in form copy for my lead magnet [LEAD MAGNET NAME] that helps [TARGET AUDIENCE] with [OUTCOME]. This is the compact form itself, such as a popup, inline box, or sidebar widget. Give me: a short punchy headline, one line of supporting text on the benefit, the button text that beats the generic submit, and a reassurance line about no spam or easy unsubscribe. Keep it tight since form space is limited and every word must earn its place. Give me 3 versions for different placements: popup, inline, and footer. My tone: [TONE].
description: Write tight opt in form copy that converts across popups, inline, and footer.
useCase: Use this for the actual opt in forms across your site. Compact form copy with a benefit driven headline and a button that beats generic submit text lifts conversion at the exact moment of decision, and different placements need different tuned versions.

title: Write the Lead Magnet Delivery Email
promptText: Write the delivery email that sends [LEAD MAGNET NAME] to new subscribers who just opted in. Give me: a subject line confirming their download is inside, a warm welcome, the clear link or access to the lead magnet, a quick tip on how to get the most from it, a taste of what else they can expect from me, and a soft first mention of my offer [YOUR OFFER] without pushing. This email has a high open rate so it is prime real estate to start the relationship well. Keep it warm and concise. My tone: [TONE].
description: Write the high open rate delivery email that starts the subscriber relationship right.
useCase: Use this for the email that delivers your lead magnet, which gets opened almost every time. This prime real estate is where you set expectations and begin the relationship, and a warm delivery email that adds value beyond the download starts subscribers on the path toward your offer.

title: Build a Quiz Lead Magnet
promptText: Design a quiz lead magnet for [TARGET AUDIENCE] that helps them discover [WHAT THE QUIZ REVEALS, E.G. THEIR TYPE, THEIR BIGGEST GAP, OR WHICH APPROACH FITS]. It should lead toward my offer [YOUR OFFER]. Give me: a compelling quiz title, the questions that segment respondents meaningfully, the result categories or types, a description of what each result means with tailored advice, and how each result naturally points to a relevant next step or my offer. The quiz should be genuinely insightful and shareable, and the results should feel personalized. My topic: [YOUR TOPIC], my tone: [TONE].
description: Build an insightful, shareable quiz that segments leads and points to your offer.
useCase: Use this to create a quiz lead magnet, which converts and shares exceptionally well. Quizzes engage through curiosity about the personalized result, and segmenting subscribers by their answers lets you tailor follow up and recommend the right offer to each type.
variables: WHAT THE QUIZ REVEALS, E.G. THEIR TYPE, THEIR BIGGEST GAP, OR WHICH APPROACH FITS

title: Build a Cheat Sheet Lead Magnet
promptText: Build a one page cheat sheet lead magnet for [TARGET AUDIENCE] that distills [TOPIC OR PROCESS] into a quick reference they will keep and use repeatedly. Give me: a title, the key information organized for fast scanning such as steps, formulas, do and do not lists, or quick reference tables, and a note on layout so it fits usefully on one page. Include the most important things they need at a glance without hunting. End with a small pointer to my offer [YOUR OFFER]. A great cheat sheet earns a permanent spot on their desk or screen. My tone: [TONE].
description: Build a one page cheat sheet people keep and reference again and again.
useCase: Use this to create a cheat sheet lead magnet, valued for its repeated usefulness. A quick reference people keep on their desk or screen keeps your brand present over time, and the perceived high value relative to the small effort of downloading drives strong opt in rates.

title: Turn Existing Content into a Lead Magnet
promptText: Here is existing content I have created: [PASTE BLOG POSTS, VIDEOS, OR MATERIAL, OR DESCRIBE THEM]. Help me repackage it into a lead magnet for [TARGET AUDIENCE]. Identify the strongest reusable material, recommend the best lead magnet format to turn it into, and outline how to restructure and upgrade it into a cohesive downloadable resource that feels more valuable than the free scattered version. It should lead toward my offer [YOUR OFFER]. Note what to add to make it worth opting in for rather than just a repackage. My tone: [TONE].
description: Repackage your existing content into a cohesive, opt in worthy lead magnet.
useCase: Use this to create a lead magnet from work you already did. Repurposing your best existing content into a cohesive downloadable resource is far faster than starting fresh, and packaging scattered free material into one valuable asset gives people a reason to opt in.
variables: PASTE BLOG POSTS, VIDEOS, OR MATERIAL, OR DESCRIBE THEM

title: Build a Workbook or Worksheet Lead Magnet
promptText: Build a workbook or worksheet lead magnet that guides [TARGET AUDIENCE] through [PROCESS OR EXERCISE] to reach [OUTCOME]. Give me: a title, a short intro on what they will accomplish, the exercises or prompts in sequence with space for them to fill in their own answers, guiding questions that produce real insight or a tangible result by the end, and a closing that connects their result to my offer [YOUR OFFER]. Make it interactive so they invest effort, which increases commitment and results. Note where an example answer would help. My topic: [YOUR TOPIC], my tone: [TONE].
description: Build an interactive workbook that guides your audience to a real result.
useCase: Use this to create a workbook lead magnet, which drives high engagement through active participation. When subscribers fill in exercises and produce a tangible result, they invest effort that deepens commitment, and reaching a real outcome makes your paid offer the obvious next step.

title: Bridge Your Lead Magnet to Your Offer
promptText: My lead magnet is [LEAD MAGNET NAME], which helps [TARGET AUDIENCE] with [WHAT IT SOLVES]. My paid offer is [YOUR OFFER], which delivers [OFFER OUTCOME]. Help me design the bridge between them so the lead magnet naturally leads to the offer. Show me: the logical connection where the lead magnet solves the first problem and the offer solves the next, how to end the lead magnet so it opens the door to the offer without a hard sell, and the follow up messaging that continues the journey. The transition should feel like a natural next step, not a bait and switch. My tone: [TONE].
description: Design the natural bridge from your free lead magnet to your paid offer.
useCase: Use this when your lead magnet gets downloads but does not lead to sales. Designing the bridge so the freebie solves the first problem and your offer solves the next makes the transition feel like a natural next step, which is what turns a list into revenue.

title: Audit an Underperforming Lead Magnet
promptText: My lead magnet [LEAD MAGNET NAME] for [TARGET AUDIENCE] is not converting well. Here is the current title and landing page copy: [PASTE TITLE AND COPY], and here is what it delivers: [WHAT IT DELIVERS]. Audit it to find why it may be underperforming. Assess: whether the promise is clear and compelling enough, whether it targets a real urgent problem, whether the title earns the opt in, whether the landing copy sells the transformation, and whether it attracts the right people who would buy my offer [YOUR OFFER]. Then give me specific fixes prioritized by likely impact. Be direct about weaknesses.
description: Audit a low converting lead magnet and get prioritized fixes.
useCase: Use this when a lead magnet gets traffic but few opt ins, or attracts the wrong people. An honest audit of the promise, title, copy, and targeting reveals why it underperforms, so you fix the specific weak points rather than scrapping it or guessing at improvements.

title: Build a Case Study Lead Magnet
promptText: Build a case study lead magnet showing how [CLIENT OR EXAMPLE] achieved [RESULT] using [YOUR APPROACH OR OFFER], for [TARGET AUDIENCE] considering similar results. Give me: a results focused title, the starting situation and challenge, the approach and steps taken presented so readers can learn from it, the results with specifics, and the transferable lessons. Structure it to teach and prove at once, so it both delivers value and demonstrates my offer works. End with a natural invitation to explore working with me. The reader should think this could work for me too. My tone: [TONE].
description: Build a case study lead magnet that teaches while proving your offer works.
useCase: Use this to create a lead magnet that doubles as powerful proof. A case study attracts leads who want the same result and simultaneously demonstrates your offer works, capturing high intent prospects who are already imagining themselves achieving the outcome you show.

title: Create a Content Upgrade for a Blog Post
promptText: I have a blog post about [BLOG POST TOPIC] for [TARGET AUDIENCE]. Design a content upgrade, a bonus resource offered within the post in exchange for an email, that perfectly complements it. Given the post covers [WHAT THE POST COVERS], suggest the ideal upgrade format and content that extends the post's value, such as a checklist, template, expanded guide, or worksheet version of the post's advice. Give me the upgrade concept, its title, an outline of what it contains, and the in post copy to offer it. A tightly matched content upgrade converts far better than a generic sitewide lead magnet. My tone: [TONE].
description: Design a tightly matched content upgrade that boosts opt ins within a blog post.
useCase: Use this to dramatically lift opt ins on your best blog posts. A content upgrade matched to a specific post converts far better than a generic sitewide lead magnet, because readers already engaged with the topic want the bonus that extends exactly what they are reading.

title: Write a Design Brief for Your Ebook
promptText: I have written my ebook [EBOOK NAME] for [TARGET AUDIENCE] and need it to look professional. Write a design brief I can hand to a designer or use myself in a tool like Canva. Based on the content covering [WHAT IT COVERS] and my brand feel of [BRAND STYLE OR VIBE], recommend: the overall visual direction, cover concept, layout structure for readability, where to place visuals or callouts, and any elements that increase perceived value. The goal is a polished, credible looking resource that matches the quality of the content. Keep the guidance practical for a non designer. My tone: [TONE].
description: Write a practical design brief that makes your ebook look professional.
useCase: Use this once your ebook content is written but looks plain. A clear design brief turns good content into a polished, credible resource, since a professional looking lead magnet raises perceived value and trust, whether you hand the brief to a designer or execute it yourself in Canva.

title: Plan How to Promote Your Lead Magnet
promptText: I created a lead magnet [LEAD MAGNET NAME] for [TARGET AUDIENCE] but need to get it in front of people. My channels are [YOUR CHANNELS, E.G. INSTAGRAM, EMAIL, BLOG, LINKEDIN]. Give me a promotion plan: where and how to place it for ongoing passive opt ins across my site and profiles, the social posts and hooks to actively promote it, how to work it into my content naturally, and any partnership or one time push ideas. Prioritize the highest leverage promotion for a solo operator with limited time. A great lead magnet nobody sees builds no list. My tone: [TONE].
description: Build a practical promotion plan so your lead magnet actually gets seen.
useCase: Use this after creating a lead magnet, when the real work of getting it seen begins. A promotion plan covering both passive placements and active pushes ensures your lead magnet actually builds a list, since even the best magnet grows nothing if nobody finds it.
variables: YOUR CHANNELS, E.G. INSTAGRAM, EMAIL, BLOG, LINKEDIN

title: Write Full Podcast Show Notes
promptText: Write complete show notes for my podcast episode. The episode is titled [EPISODE TITLE], featuring [GUEST OR SOLO], about [EPISODE TOPIC] for [TARGET AUDIENCE]. Here is the transcript or key points: [PASTE TRANSCRIPT OR KEY POINTS]. Give me: a compelling episode summary of two to three paragraphs, a bulleted list of key takeaways, notable quotes worth highlighting, any resources or links mentioned, and a short call to action. Make it scannable and genuinely useful for someone deciding whether to listen or wanting a reference after. My tone is [TONE].
description: Turn an episode transcript into complete, scannable show notes with takeaways.
useCase: Use this after recording each episode to produce show notes fast. Good show notes make episodes searchable, help listeners decide to press play, and give a reference afterward, which drives more listens and repeat engagement.

title: Write a Podcast Episode Summary
promptText: Write a compelling episode summary for my podcast episode about [EPISODE TOPIC] for [TARGET AUDIENCE]. Here are the key points covered: [PASTE KEY POINTS OR TRANSCRIPT]. Give me three summary versions of different lengths: a one sentence hook for a preview, a short two to three sentence version for a podcast directory, and a fuller paragraph for the show notes page. Each should sell the value of listening without giving everything away, leading with what the listener gains. My tone is [TONE].
description: Write episode summaries in three lengths that sell the listen.
useCase: Use this for every episode since the summary is what people read before deciding to listen. Having three lengths ready covers podcast directories, previews, and show notes pages, each tuned to hook the listener where they see it.

title: Generate Podcast Episode Titles
promptText: Generate 15 title options for my podcast episode about [EPISODE TOPIC] featuring [GUEST OR SOLO] for [TARGET AUDIENCE]. The main value or takeaway is [MAIN TAKEAWAY]. Give me a mix: some benefit driven, some curiosity based, some featuring the guest name and their credibility if relevant, some with a number or specific angle, and some phrased as the question the episode answers. Keep them clear enough to work in a podcast app list yet compelling enough to earn the tap. Mark the three strongest and explain why. My tone is [TONE].
description: Generate podcast episode titles that earn the tap in a crowded app list.
useCase: Use this for every episode since the title competes for attention in podcast apps and search. A title that balances clarity with curiosity earns more taps, and testing angles helps you learn what makes your specific audience press play.

title: Create Episode Timestamps and Chapters
promptText: Create timestamped chapters for my podcast episode about [EPISODE TOPIC]. Here is the transcript with timing or a description of the segments in order: [PASTE TRANSCRIPT WITH TIMESTAMPS OR SEGMENT DESCRIPTIONS]. Give me a clean list of timestamps with a short descriptive label for each major segment or topic shift, so listeners can jump to what interests them. Keep labels concise and scannable, capturing the actual content of each section. Include an intro and outro marker. If the timing is approximate, note that. My tone is [TONE].
description: Turn an episode into scannable timestamped chapters listeners can navigate.
useCase: Use this to add chapter markers that improve the listener experience and searchability. Timestamps let people jump to what interests them and signal a well produced show, which increases completion rates and repeat listens.
variables: PASTE TRANSCRIPT WITH TIMESTAMPS OR SEGMENT DESCRIPTIONS

title: Extract Key Takeaways from an Episode
promptText: Extract the key takeaways from my podcast episode about [EPISODE TOPIC] for [TARGET AUDIENCE]. Here is the transcript or notes: [PASTE TRANSCRIPT OR NOTES]. Give me the main actionable takeaways as a scannable list, each phrased as a clear insight or action the listener can apply, not a vague summary. Order them by importance. For each, keep it to one or two sentences that capture the value without needing the full episode. These should stand alone as useful even for someone who skims. My tone is [TONE].
description: Pull an episode's actionable takeaways into a scannable, standalone list.
useCase: Use this to give listeners and skimmers the core value at a glance. Clear actionable takeaways make an episode more useful and shareable, and they feed directly into show notes, social posts, and newsletter mentions.

title: Write a Podcast Guest Bio and Intro
promptText: Write a guest bio and intro for my podcast episode with [GUEST NAME], who is [WHAT THEY DO AND THEIR CREDIBILITY]. The episode covers [EPISODE TOPIC]. Give me: a short written bio for the show notes that establishes their credibility and why listeners should care, a one or two sentence version for previews, and a warm spoken introduction I can read at the top of the episode that welcomes them and frames why this conversation matters. Make the credibility clear without being a dry resume. My tone is [TONE].
description: Write a guest bio for the notes and a warm spoken intro for the episode.
useCase: Use this whenever you host a guest to save time and introduce them well. A strong bio establishes credibility for listeners while the spoken intro sets a warm tone, both of which make the episode feel professional and give the guest a reason to share it.
variables: WHAT THEY DO AND THEIR CREDIBILITY

title: Pull Quotable Moments from an Episode
promptText: Pull the most quotable, shareable moments from my podcast episode about [EPISODE TOPIC]. Here is the transcript: [PASTE TRANSCRIPT]. Identify the lines that are punchy, insightful, or emotionally resonant enough to work as pull quotes in show notes, quote graphics, or social posts. Give me the exact quote, who said it, and a note on where it would work best. Prioritize lines that stand alone without context and capture a memorable idea from the episode. Aim for the strongest handful rather than every decent line. My tone is [TONE].
description: Extract the most shareable pull quotes from an episode transcript.
useCase: Use this to mine each episode for standout quotes that fuel promotion. Pull quotes work as social graphics, show note highlights, and newsletter teasers, turning one recorded conversation into multiple shareable assets that drive people back to the episode.

title: Turn an Episode into Social Posts
promptText: Turn my podcast episode about [EPISODE TOPIC] for [TARGET AUDIENCE] into social media posts to promote it. Here are the key points or transcript: [PASTE KEY POINTS OR TRANSCRIPT]. Give me: a few posts sharing individual insights from the episode as standalone value, a post teasing a surprising or compelling moment, a quote based post, and a straightforward promotion post with a link. Vary them for [PLATFORMS] so they are not identical. Each should give people a reason to listen without requiring them to. My tone is [TONE].
description: Repurpose one episode into a set of varied social posts that drive listens.
useCase: Use this to promote each episode across social without writing posts from scratch. Turning one episode into several standalone valuable posts extends its reach, drives listens, and keeps your feed full from content you already created.

title: Turn an Episode into a Newsletter
promptText: Turn my podcast episode about [EPISODE TOPIC] into a newsletter issue for [TARGET AUDIENCE]. Here are the key points or transcript: [PASTE KEY POINTS OR TRANSCRIPT]. Do not just summarize the episode. Instead, pull the strongest idea, expand it with my perspective in a more personal written voice, add value beyond what a listener would get, and link to the full episode for those who want more. Include a subject line and a short personal opening. The newsletter should stand on its own while driving listens. My tone is [TONE].
description: Adapt an episode into a standalone newsletter that also drives listens.
useCase: Use this to feed your email list from your podcast without extra creation work. A newsletter built on an episode's best idea reaches subscribers who will not listen, adds value for those who did, and drives traffic back to the full episode.

title: Turn an Episode into a Blog Post
promptText: Turn my podcast episode about [EPISODE TOPIC] for [TARGET AUDIENCE] into a blog post. Here is the transcript: [PASTE TRANSCRIPT]. Do not transcribe it literally. Restructure the key ideas into a coherent, readable article with a strong intro, logical sections, and a conclusion, keeping the best insights and quotes while tightening spoken language for reading. Optimize it lightly for the search term [TARGET KEYWORD OR WRITE NONE] so it can bring in new listeners. End by pointing readers to the full episode. My tone is [TONE].
description: Repurpose an episode transcript into a readable, lightly SEO optimized blog post.
useCase: Use this to get searchable written content from your audio. A blog version of an episode reaches readers who never open podcast apps and can rank in search, turning each episode into a discovery channel that brings new listeners to the show.

title: Write a Podcast Directory Description
promptText: Write the show description for my podcast for directories like Apple Podcasts and Spotify. My show is called [PODCAST NAME], it is for [TARGET AUDIENCE], and it covers [WHAT THE SHOW IS ABOUT]. What makes it worth subscribing to is [WHAT MAKES IT DIFFERENT]. Give me: a compelling one or two sentence hook that appears first, a fuller description explaining who it is for and what they will get, and a closing line that invites the subscribe. Include natural keywords a listener might search. Make it clear who should press subscribe and why. My tone is [TONE].
description: Write a searchable, compelling show description that earns subscribes.
useCase: Use this to write or refresh the description that represents your whole show in directories. A clear, keyword aware description helps new listeners find you in search and understand instantly whether the show is for them, driving subscribes.

title: Write a Podcast Trailer Script
promptText: Write a short trailer script for my podcast [PODCAST NAME], which is for [TARGET AUDIENCE] and covers [WHAT THE SHOW IS ABOUT]. What makes it worth their time is [THE VALUE OR ANGLE]. Write a trailer of about [TARGET LENGTH] that hooks a potential listener fast, conveys the vibe and value of the show, tells them what to expect and how often, and ends with a clear invitation to subscribe. Write it for spoken delivery with natural energy, not read aloud ad copy. Note where music or a pause would land. My tone is [TONE].
description: Write a spoken trailer script that hooks new listeners and earns subscribes.
useCase: Use this to create the trailer that introduces your show to new listeners in podcast apps. A tight trailer that conveys the value and vibe fast converts browsers into subscribers, since many people sample the trailer before committing to a full episode.

title: Prepare Podcast Interview Questions
promptText: Prepare interview questions for my podcast conversation with [GUEST NAME], who is [WHAT THEY DO AND WHY THEY ARE INTERESTING]. My audience is [TARGET AUDIENCE], and the angle I want to explore is [EPISODE ANGLE OR THEME]. Give me: a warm opening question to ease in, a set of substantive questions that draw out their unique expertise and stories rather than generic ones they answer everywhere, a few unexpected questions that surface fresh material, and a strong closing question. Order them into a natural conversational arc. Note which questions to prioritize if time runs short. My tone is [TONE].
description: Prepare a conversational arc of interview questions that draw out fresh material.
useCase: Use this before every guest interview to get beyond the generic answers they give on every show. Well prepared questions that draw out unique stories make for a better episode, respect the guest, and give you more standout material to repurpose.
variables: WHAT THEY DO AND WHY THEY ARE INTERESTING

title: Outline a Solo Podcast Episode
promptText: Outline a solo podcast episode where I teach [TARGET AUDIENCE] about [EPISODE TOPIC]. The main takeaway I want them to leave with is [MAIN TAKEAWAY]. Give me: a hook to open with that earns attention in the first thirty seconds, a clear structure of the points to cover in logical order, a story or example to include, natural transitions between sections, and a strong close with a call to action. Keep it structured enough to stay on track but loose enough to sound natural, not scripted. Note roughly how long each section should run for a [TARGET LENGTH] episode. My tone is [TONE].
description: Outline a solo episode with a hook, clear structure, and a strong close.
useCase: Use this to plan solo episodes that stay focused and deliver value. A clear outline prevents the rambling that loses listeners, while leaving room to sound natural, which is what keeps a solo show engaging and easy to record consistently.

title: Pick Audiogram Snippets from an Episode
promptText: Identify the best short clips from my podcast episode about [EPISODE TOPIC] to turn into audiograms or video clips for social promotion. Here is the transcript with timing: [PASTE TRANSCRIPT WITH TIMESTAMPS]. Find the segments that work as standalone clips of roughly thirty to sixty seconds: self contained, punchy, and compelling enough to make someone want the full episode. For each, give me the start and end point, why it works as a clip, and a caption or hook to pair with it on social. Prioritize the strongest few over quantity. My tone is [TONE].
description: Find the best short clips in an episode to promote as audiograms or video.
useCase: Use this to pull the shareable clips that drive discovery on social. Short audiograms and video clips travel far beyond your existing audience, and picking genuinely compelling standalone moments is what makes them convert viewers into listeners.

title: Write Guest Outreach and Thank You Messages
promptText: Write the messages around a podcast guest appearance for my show [PODCAST NAME] for [TARGET AUDIENCE]. The guest is [GUEST NAME], who [WHAT THEY DO]. Give me: a warm invitation message that explains why I want them specifically and what is in it for them, a pre recording logistics and prep note, and a post episode thank you message that includes ready to share assets and links so they promote it to their audience. Keep each concise and genuine, peer to peer, not fawning. The thank you especially should make it effortless for them to share. My tone is [TONE].
description: Write guest invite, prep, and thank you messages that make sharing effortless.
useCase: Use this to handle guest communication smoothly and maximize the reach each guest brings. A warm invite lands more yeses, and a thank you with ready to share assets makes guests promote the episode to their audience, which is how guest shows grow.

title: Write Podcast Calls to Action
promptText: Write calls to action for my podcast episode about [EPISODE TOPIC] for [TARGET AUDIENCE]. The action I most want listeners to take is [PRIMARY ACTION, E.G. SUBSCRIBE, LEAVE A REVIEW, JOIN MY LIST, OR CHECK OUT MY OFFER]. Give me: a natural spoken CTA to say in the episode that does not feel forced, a written version for the show notes, and a couple of alternate phrasings so I can rotate them across episodes. Keep them genuine and specific, tied to the value of the episode rather than a generic please subscribe. My offer or list is [YOUR OFFER OR LIST]. My tone is [TONE].
description: Write genuine spoken and written podcast CTAs tied to the episode's value.
useCase: Use this to convert listeners without the awkward forced ask most shows use. CTAs tied to the episode's value and rotated across episodes feel natural and get more subscribes, reviews, and list signups, which is how a podcast builds an audience and a business.
variables: PRIMARY ACTION, E.G. SUBSCRIBE, LEAVE A REVIEW, JOIN MY LIST, OR CHECK OUT MY OFFER

title: Optimize Show Notes for Search
promptText: Here are my show notes for an episode about [EPISODE TOPIC]: [PASTE SHOW NOTES]. Optimize them so the episode page can be found in search. My audience searches for terms like [RELATED SEARCH TERMS OR WRITE SUGGEST]. Improve: the page title and any headings for keyword relevance, the summary so it includes natural search terms, and the structure so search engines can parse it. Suggest a strong meta description for the episode page. Keep it readable and genuinely useful, not stuffed with keywords. Note what you changed and why. My tone is [TONE].
description: Optimize episode show notes so the page ranks and brings in new listeners.
useCase: Use this to turn show note pages into a discovery channel. Search optimized notes help new listeners find episodes through search rather than only through podcast apps, quietly growing your audience from content you already publish.
variables: RELATED SEARCH TERMS OR WRITE SUGGEST

title: Create a Reusable Show Notes Template
promptText: Create a reusable show notes template for my podcast [PODCAST NAME] for [TARGET AUDIENCE], so every episode follows a consistent professional format and I can produce notes fast. My show format is [SOLO, INTERVIEW, OR MIXED]. Give me a template with clearly labeled sections such as episode summary, key takeaways, timestamps, guest bio if applicable, resources mentioned, quotes, and calls to action, with a short note on what goes in each and any fill in the blank prompts. Make it easy for me or an assistant to complete for any episode. My tone and style is [TONE].
description: Build a reusable show notes template for fast, consistent episode notes.
useCase: Use this once to standardize your show notes and speed up production. A consistent template means every episode looks professional, notes get done faster, and the work can be handed to an assistant, which is how a solo podcaster keeps a show sustainable.

title: Adjust the Tone of Your Show Notes
promptText: Here are my show notes: [PASTE SHOW NOTES]. They do not match the voice of my podcast, which is [DESCRIBE YOUR SHOW VIBE, E.G. CASUAL AND FUNNY, OR AUTHORITATIVE AND CALM]. Rewrite them to match that voice while keeping the structure and information intact. Adjust word choice, sentence rhythm, and energy so the notes sound like the show. Show me the rewritten version and note the specific changes that shifted the tone, so I can keep it consistent going forward. My audience is [TARGET AUDIENCE].
description: Rewrite show notes to match the voice and energy of your podcast.
useCase: Use this when your written notes feel disconnected from your show's personality. Matching the notes to the podcast voice keeps your brand consistent across audio and text, which strengthens the listener relationship and makes the whole show feel intentional.
variables: DESCRIBE YOUR SHOW VIBE, E.G. CASUAL AND FUNNY, OR AUTHORITATIVE AND CALM

title: Write an Episode Teaser or Cold Open
promptText: Write a teaser or cold open for my podcast episode about [EPISODE TOPIC] for [TARGET AUDIENCE]. Here is the most compelling moment or takeaway: [THE HOOK MOMENT]. Give me a short spoken teaser I can place at the very top of the episode that pulls a listener in before the intro music, using the strongest moment or a provocative question to make them commit to staying. Keep it under [TARGET LENGTH]. Also give me a written teaser line for the show notes and previews. Make it genuinely intriguing without overselling. My tone is [TONE].
description: Write a spoken cold open and written teaser that hook listeners immediately.
useCase: Use this to grab listeners in the critical first seconds before they skip. A cold open built on the episode's strongest moment lifts retention, and the matching written teaser gives previews and show notes a hook that earns the play.

title: Plan a Podcast Season or Batch of Episodes
promptText: Help me plan a season or batch of [NUMBER OF EPISODES] podcast episodes for my show [PODCAST NAME] for [TARGET AUDIENCE] about [SHOW THEME OR TOPIC AREA]. Give me: a cohesive theme or arc for the batch if useful, individual episode topics and angles that build interest and cover the theme well, a mix of formats such as solo, interview, and Q and A so it stays varied, and working titles for each. Note which episodes could feature guests and which topics are strongest to lead with. The goal is a planned run I can record efficiently rather than scrambling each week. My tone is [TONE].
description: Plan a cohesive batch of episodes with topics, formats, and titles.
useCase: Use this to plan ahead so you can batch record and never scramble for a topic. A planned season with varied formats keeps the show fresh and consistent, which is the hardest part of sustaining a podcast as a busy solo operator.

title: Clean Up a Raw Podcast Transcript
promptText: Here is a raw auto generated transcript from my podcast episode about [EPISODE TOPIC]: [PASTE RAW TRANSCRIPT]. Clean it up into a readable transcript. Remove filler words, false starts, and repeated words, fix obvious transcription errors from context, add proper punctuation and paragraph breaks, and label speakers clearly if it is an interview: [SPEAKER NAMES OR WRITE HOST AND GUEST]. Keep the actual meaning and the speakers' voices intact, do not rewrite what they said, just make it readable. Flag anything that seems garbled where I should check the audio. My tone is [TONE].
description: Turn a messy auto transcript into a clean, readable transcript.
useCase: Use this to publish readable transcripts without hours of manual editing. Clean transcripts improve accessibility, add searchable text that helps discovery, and give you source material for blog posts and social clips, all from the raw file you already have.
variables: SPEAKER NAMES OR WRITE HOST AND GUEST

title: Atomize One Piece into Many Posts
promptText: Here is one substantial piece of content: [PASTE CONTENT OR DESCRIBE IT]. My audience is [TARGET AUDIENCE] and my platforms are [PLATFORMS]. Break it into as many distinct standalone pieces as it can genuinely support. Pull out each core idea, statistic, quote, example, and step as its own potential post. For each, tell me the format and platform it suits best and a one line hook. Only include pieces that stand on their own with real value, not filler. Present them as a list I can work through. My tone is [TONE].
description: Break one substantial piece into every distinct standalone post it can support.
useCase: Use this after creating anything substantial so one effort fuels weeks of content. Atomizing a strong piece into its component ideas is how a solo operator maintains presence everywhere without constantly creating from scratch.

title: Turn a Blog Post into a Social Thread
promptText: Turn this blog post into a social thread for [PLATFORM, E.G. X OR THREADS]: [PASTE BLOG POST]. My audience is [TARGET AUDIENCE]. Open with a hook post that promises the payoff and stops the scroll, break the key points into individual connected posts that each stand alone yet pull the reader forward, keep the language punchy for the platform, and end with a post that drives to [DESIRED ACTION, E.G. READ THE FULL POST OR FOLLOW]. Do not just chop the article into pieces, adapt it into native thread writing. My tone is [TONE].
description: Adapt a blog post into a native social thread that pulls readers forward.
useCase: Use this to reach the audience that reads threads but never clicks to a blog. A thread adapted into native writing, not chopped from the article, drives engagement and sends a portion of readers back to the full post.
variables: DESIRED ACTION, E.G. READ THE FULL POST OR FOLLOW

title: Turn a Blog Post into a Carousel
promptText: Turn this blog post into a carousel for [PLATFORM, E.G. INSTAGRAM OR LINKEDIN]: [PASTE BLOG POST OR KEY POINTS]. My audience is [TARGET AUDIENCE]. Give me a slide by slide breakdown: a cover slide that hooks and promises the value, middle slides that each carry exactly one idea with a short headline and supporting line, and a final slide with a call to action to [DESIRED ACTION]. Keep text per slide minimal and scannable, and note a visual suggestion for each slide. Aim for [NUMBER OF SLIDES] slides. My tone is [TONE].
description: Convert a blog post into a slide by slide carousel with one idea per slide.
useCase: Use this to repurpose written content into the carousel format that earns strong engagement on Instagram and LinkedIn. One idea per slide keeps viewers swiping, and the format reaches people who never read long form.
variables: PASTE BLOG POST OR KEY POINTS

title: Adapt One Piece Across Every Platform
promptText: Here is one piece of content: [PASTE CONTENT OR KEY POINTS]. Adapt it for each of my platforms so it fits the native style of each rather than being cross posted identically. My platforms are [PLATFORMS] and my audience is [TARGET AUDIENCE]. For each platform, rewrite the piece to match its format, length, tone, and hook conventions, and note what changed and why. Flag any platform where this content is a poor fit and should be skipped. My tone is [TONE].
description: Adapt one piece to the native style of each platform instead of cross posting.
useCase: Use this to get more reach from one idea across channels. Identical cross posting underperforms because each platform has its own norms, and adapting rather than duplicating makes the same idea land natively everywhere.

title: Turn a Blog Post into a Video Script
promptText: Turn this blog post into a short video script for [PLATFORM]: [PASTE BLOG POST OR KEY POINTS]. My audience is [TARGET AUDIENCE]. Do not narrate it word for word. Pull the single strongest idea, open with a scroll stopping hook, deliver the value in spoken paced beats with on screen text cues, and close with a call to action to [DESIRED ACTION]. Target length is [TARGET LENGTH]. Flag any other points from the post strong enough to become their own separate videos. My tone is [TONE].
description: Adapt written content into a spoken video script built around the strongest idea.
useCase: Use this to get video from writing you already published. Pulling one strong idea rather than narrating the whole post produces a video that works as video, reaching the large audience that watches but does not read.
variables: PASTE BLOG POST OR KEY POINTS

title: Turn a Video into Written Content
promptText: Here is a transcript or the key points from my video on [TOPIC]: [PASTE TRANSCRIPT OR KEY POINTS]. My audience is [TARGET AUDIENCE]. Turn it into written content, not a literal transcription. Restructure the ideas into a clean, readable format suited for [DESIRED FORMAT, E.G. A BLOG POST, NEWSLETTER, OR LINKEDIN POST], tightening spoken language for reading while keeping the best insights and quotes. Add structure and any context the spoken version assumed. Flag the most quotable lines. My tone is [TONE].
description: Convert a video transcript into clean, structured written content.
useCase: Use this to turn your video or spoken content into searchable writing. Written versions reach readers who never watch and can rank in search, extending the life of content you already recorded.
variables: DESIRED FORMAT, E.G. A BLOG POST, NEWSLETTER, OR LINKEDIN POST

title: Plan a Repurposing System
promptText: Help me build a repurposing system around one core piece of content I create each week. My core format is [YOUR CORE FORMAT, E.G. A NEWSLETTER, VIDEO, OR PODCAST] and my platforms are [PLATFORMS]. My audience is [TARGET AUDIENCE]. Design a repeatable chain: from the anchor piece, exactly what gets created for each other platform and in what order, so one creative act each week generates a full week of presence everywhere. Keep it realistic for a solo operator. Give me the chain as a simple repeatable workflow. My tone is [TONE].
description: Build a repeatable chain where one weekly anchor piece feeds every platform.
useCase: Use this to escape creating separately for each platform. A defined repurposing chain from one anchor piece is the only sustainable content model for one person, turning a single weekly effort into presence everywhere.
variables: YOUR CORE FORMAT, E.G. A NEWSLETTER, VIDEO, OR PODCAST

title: Turn a Newsletter into Social Posts
promptText: Turn this newsletter issue into social posts: [PASTE NEWSLETTER]. My audience is [TARGET AUDIENCE] and my platforms are [PLATFORMS]. Extract the strongest ideas and lines and adapt them into standalone posts that deliver value without needing the newsletter, plus one post that teases the newsletter and invites people to subscribe at [SIGNUP LINK OR WRITE NONE]. Vary the posts by platform so they are not identical. Note which post is strongest for driving subscribers. My tone is [TONE].
description: Repurpose a newsletter into social posts that also grow your list.
useCase: Use this to promote your newsletter and fill your feed at once. Standalone posts from a newsletter reach people who are not subscribed yet, and the teaser post converts some of them into subscribers.

title: Turn Your Best Posts into a Newsletter
promptText: Here are some of my recent social posts that performed well: [PASTE POSTS]. My audience is [TARGET AUDIENCE]. Combine and expand them into a newsletter issue. Find the common thread or pick the strongest idea, expand it with more depth and my perspective in a written voice, and shape it into a cohesive issue rather than a list of posts stitched together. Include a subject line and a short personal opening. Add value beyond what the posts already gave. My tone is [TONE].
description: Expand your best performing posts into a cohesive newsletter issue.
useCase: Use this to feed your newsletter from social content that already proved it resonates. Posts that performed well are validated ideas, and expanding them into a newsletter is lower risk than betting on untested topics.

title: Repurpose a Webinar or Workshop
promptText: I ran a webinar or workshop on [TOPIC] for [TARGET AUDIENCE]. Here is the transcript or the outline and key points: [PASTE TRANSCRIPT OR KEY POINTS]. Break it into a full repurposing plan: the blog posts, social posts, short video clips, a lead magnet, and email content it can become. For each, note what to pull from the session and the format. Prioritize the highest value pieces to create first. A single webinar should generate weeks of content across formats. My platforms are [PLATFORMS] and my tone is [TONE].
description: Turn one webinar into a full multi format content plan.
useCase: Use this to extract maximum value from a webinar or workshop you already delivered. A single long session contains blog posts, clips, a lead magnet, and emails, so mapping the full plan turns one event into weeks of content.

title: Turn Content into a Lead Magnet
promptText: Here is existing content I have made: [PASTE OR DESCRIBE THE CONTENT]. My audience is [TARGET AUDIENCE]. Repackage it into a lead magnet. Identify the strongest reusable material, recommend the best lead magnet format for it, and outline how to restructure and upgrade it into a cohesive downloadable worth opting in for. It should lead toward my offer [YOUR OFFER]. Note what to add so it feels more valuable than the scattered free version rather than a plain repackage. My tone is [TONE].
description: Repackage existing content into a cohesive, opt in worthy lead magnet.
useCase: Use this to build a list building asset from work already done. Packaging scattered content into one valuable downloadable is far faster than starting fresh and gives people a reason to hand over their email.

title: Recycle Evergreen Content on a Schedule
promptText: Here are my evergreen posts and content that still hold up: [PASTE OR LIST THE CONTENT]. My audience is [TARGET AUDIENCE] and my platforms are [PLATFORMS]. Help me recycle them intelligently. For each piece, give me a fresh way to repost it: a new hook, an updated angle, a different format, or new framing so it does not feel like a rerun. Recommend a rough rotation schedule for reposting evergreen content without my audience noticing repetition. My tone is [TONE].
description: Recycle evergreen content with fresh hooks and formats on a rotation.
useCase: Use this to keep posting on weeks you cannot create. Evergreen pieces reframed with new hooks or formats often outperform scrambling for something new, and a rotation keeps your feed active without endless fresh creation.

title: Remix Your Best Performing Content
promptText: Here is a piece of my content that performed exceptionally well: [PASTE THE CONTENT]. It worked because [WHY YOU THINK IT WORKED, OR WRITE NOT SURE]. My audience is [TARGET AUDIENCE]. Help me remix it into several new pieces that capture what made it resonate: a follow up that goes deeper, the same idea in a different format, an updated or expanded version, and a related angle. Explain what element to preserve in each remix since that is what worked. My platforms are [PLATFORMS] and my tone is [TONE].
description: Remix a proven hit into several new pieces that preserve what worked.
useCase: Use this when something performs well, since your hits are your best guide to what resonates. Remixing a proven piece while preserving its winning element is lower risk than starting fresh and compounds the value of a topic your audience clearly wants.
variables: WHY YOU THINK IT WORKED, OR WRITE NOT SURE

title: Cut a Long Video into Short Clips
promptText: Here is the transcript with timing from my long video on [TOPIC]: [PASTE TRANSCRIPT WITH TIMESTAMPS]. My audience is [TARGET AUDIENCE]. Identify the best segments to cut into short clips for [PLATFORMS]. For each, give the start and end point, why it works as a standalone clip, a hook or caption to pair with it, and a suggested title. Find the moments that are self contained, punchy, and compelling enough to make someone want the full video. Prioritize the strongest few over quantity. My tone is [TONE].
description: Find the best standalone clips in a long video for short form platforms.
useCase: Use this to feed short form platforms from long video. Short clips reach far beyond your existing audience and drive discovery, and picking genuinely compelling standalone moments is what makes them convert viewers into followers.

title: Repurpose Testimonials into Content
promptText: Here are testimonials and positive feedback I have collected: [PASTE TESTIMONIALS]. My offer is [YOUR OFFER] and my audience is [TARGET AUDIENCE]. Repurpose them into content beyond a plain quote graphic: a before and after story post, a mini case study, a post addressing the objection the testimonial disproves, and a post highlighting a specific result. For each, note the format and platform. Keep the customer's real words where they are strong. Make the proof feel like a story, not bragging. My tone is [TONE].
description: Turn testimonials into story driven proof content across formats.
useCase: Use this to get more from testimonials than a screenshot. Framing customer results as stories that address objections or highlight outcomes makes proof persuasive and gives you a steady stream of credibility content.

title: Turn Customer Questions into Content
promptText: Here are the questions my customers and prospects keep asking: [PASTE OR LIST THE QUESTIONS]. My business is [BUSINESS TYPE] and my audience is [TARGET AUDIENCE]. Turn these into a content plan: for each recurring question, the piece of content that answers it and the best format, such as a short post, a video, or a blog post. Prioritize the questions that come up most or that block a sale. Answering these publicly builds trust and shortens future sales conversations. My platforms are [PLATFORMS] and my tone is [TONE].
description: Turn recurring customer questions into a trust building content plan.
useCase: Use this to convert the questions you answer repeatedly into content. Publicly answering common questions builds trust, ranks in search, and shortens future sales conversations, all from questions your audience is already asking.

title: Update and Republish Old Content
promptText: Here is an older piece of content that is dated but has good bones: [PASTE OLD CONTENT]. My audience is [TARGET AUDIENCE]. Help me update it for republishing. Identify what is outdated or no longer accurate, what to add given what has changed since, where to refresh examples or data, and how to strengthen the piece overall. Give me the update plan and the refreshed version. Also suggest how to reintroduce it so it feels new rather than recycled. My topic area is [YOUR TOPIC] and my tone is [TONE].
description: Refresh a dated but solid piece of content for republishing.
useCase: Use this to revive content that has aged rather than writing new pieces. Updating and republishing a strong older piece is one of the fastest content and SEO wins, recovering value from work already done.

title: Audit Your Content for Repurposing Opportunities
promptText: Here is a list of content I have created with rough performance notes: [LIST CONTENT WITH PERFORMANCE NOTES]. My audience is [TARGET AUDIENCE] and my current focus is [CURRENT FOCUS]. Audit it for repurposing opportunities. Identify my top performers worth remaking in new formats, evergreen pieces that could run again, dated pieces worth updating, and topics that clearly resonate and deserve a follow up. For each, suggest the specific repurpose move. Prioritize the highest leverage opportunities. My platforms are [PLATFORMS].
description: Audit your content archive to find the best repurposing opportunities.
useCase: Use this when you have a back catalog but keep making everything new. An audit surfaces your proven pieces worth reusing, which is lower risk and less effort than betting on untested fresh ideas.

title: Pull Quote Graphics from Your Content
promptText: Here is a piece of my content: [PASTE CONTENT]. My audience is [TARGET AUDIENCE]. Pull the lines that would work as quote graphics for social: punchy, insightful, or resonant enough to stand alone on an image. For each, give me the exact quote, a note on why it works, and a suggested caption to pair with the graphic when posted. Prioritize lines that capture a memorable idea without needing context. Aim for the strongest handful. My tone is [TONE].
description: Extract standalone quote graphic lines from any piece of content.
useCase: Use this to turn existing content into shareable quote graphics. Standalone quotes work as visual social posts that travel and get saved, giving you easy graphics content from writing you already have.

title: Expand One Topic into a Content Series
promptText: I have one strong topic or idea: [THE TOPIC OR IDEA]. My audience is [TARGET AUDIENCE]. Instead of one piece, help me expand it into a content series. Break the topic into its distinct angles, sub questions, and facets, each substantial enough to be its own piece. Give me the series concept, a title for each installment, what each covers, and how they connect so people follow the series. Note which installment to lead with. My platforms are [PLATFORMS] and my tone is [TONE].
description: Expand one strong topic into a connected multi part content series.
useCase: Use this to get more from a topic you would otherwise cover in one post. Breaking it into a series covers it more thoroughly, keeps your audience returning, and turns a single idea into weeks of connected content.

title: Adapt Content to a Specific New Format
promptText: Here is my content in its original format: [PASTE CONTENT]. It is currently a [ORIGINAL FORMAT]. Adapt it into a [TARGET FORMAT, E.G. AN INFOGRAPHIC OUTLINE, A CHECKLIST, A QUIZ, A SCRIPT, OR AN EMAIL]. My audience is [TARGET AUDIENCE]. Restructure the content to suit the conventions and strengths of the target format, not just copy the words across. Note what to cut, add, or reshape for the new format to work well. My tone is [TONE].
description: Adapt a piece of content from one format into a different target format.
useCase: Use this to move content between specific formats when you know what you need. Reshaping to fit the target format's strengths, rather than pasting words across, is what makes a repurposed piece actually work in its new form.
variables: TARGET FORMAT, E.G. AN INFOGRAPHIC OUTLINE, A CHECKLIST, A QUIZ, A SCRIPT, OR AN EMAIL

title: Plan a Repurposing Batch Session
promptText: I have this anchor content ready to repurpose: [PASTE OR DESCRIBE THE ANCHOR CONTENT]. My platforms are [PLATFORMS] and my audience is [TARGET AUDIENCE]. Plan a single focused batch session to repurpose it into a full set of posts. Tell me everything to create from it, the order to work in so similar tasks are grouped, and realistic time blocks for a solo operator with [HOURS AVAILABLE] available. The goal is to leave the session with a queue of ready to post content from one anchor piece. My tone is [TONE].
description: Plan a focused batch session to repurpose one anchor piece into a full queue.
useCase: Use this to repurpose efficiently in one sitting rather than piecemeal. Grouping similar repurposing tasks into a focused session produces a full content queue from one anchor piece, which is how solo operators reclaim their time.
variables: PASTE OR DESCRIBE THE ANCHOR CONTENT

title: Repurpose Content for a Different Audience
promptText: Here is content that worked for one audience: [PASTE CONTENT]. It was aimed at [ORIGINAL AUDIENCE]. I want to adapt it for a different audience: [NEW AUDIENCE], who differ because [KEY DIFFERENCES]. Rewrite it so the core idea lands for the new audience: adjust the examples, language, pain points, and framing to their world while keeping the underlying value. Note what changed and why. Flag anything that does not translate and should be dropped for this audience. My tone is [TONE].
description: Adapt content that worked for one audience to resonate with a different one.
useCase: Use this to reach a new segment with an idea already proven elsewhere. Adjusting examples and framing to a different audience's world lets a validated piece work for people it was not originally written for, expanding reach with low risk.

title: Turn Comments and Replies into Content
promptText: Here are comments, replies, or DMs my content received: [PASTE THE COMMENTS OR DISCUSSION]. My audience is [TARGET AUDIENCE]. Mine them for new content. Identify the questions raised worth a dedicated piece, the debates or disagreements worth addressing, the points people wanted expanded, and any great reactions worth featuring. For each, give me the content idea and format. Audience reactions are a goldmine for content that is guaranteed to be relevant. My platforms are [PLATFORMS] and my tone is [TONE].
description: Mine comments and replies for guaranteed relevant new content ideas.
useCase: Use this to source content from the reactions your posts already generate. Comments reveal exactly what your audience wants more of, so content built from them is guaranteed relevant and keeps a conversation going that draws more engagement.

title: Generate New Offer Ideas
promptText: Generate 12 offer ideas for my business. I am a [BUSINESS TYPE] serving [TARGET AUDIENCE], and I currently offer [CURRENT OFFERS OR WRITE NONE YET]. My skills and assets are [YOUR SKILLS AND ASSETS], and the problems my audience has are [AUDIENCE PROBLEMS]. For each idea give me the offer name, what it delivers, who exactly it serves, the problem it solves, and a rough price range. Include a mix of low ticket, mid ticket, and high ticket ideas. Mark the 3 easiest to launch fast and the 3 with the highest revenue potential. My tone is [TONE].
description: Generate a spread of offer ideas across price points from your skills and audience.
useCase: Use this when you want new revenue streams but do not know what to build. A range of ideas across price points, mapped to your skills and your audience's real problems, gives you validated directions to pursue instead of guessing.
variables: CURRENT OFFERS OR WRITE NONE YET

title: Design Your Signature Offer
promptText: Help me design a signature offer for my business. I am a [BUSINESS TYPE] and I help [TARGET AUDIENCE] achieve [MAIN OUTCOME]. My unique approach or method is [YOUR APPROACH]. Design a flagship offer that becomes the thing I am known for: give it a name, define exactly what it delivers and the transformation it promises, the format and structure, what makes it distinct from what competitors offer, and a suggested price positioning. Explain why this offer would anchor my business. My tone is [TONE].
description: Design a flagship signature offer that becomes what your business is known for.
useCase: Use this when your offers feel scattered or generic and you want one anchor. A well designed signature offer becomes your reputation, simplifies your marketing, and gives customers one clear thing to buy and refer.

title: Build an Offer Ladder
promptText: Help me build an offer ladder for my business. I am a [BUSINESS TYPE] serving [TARGET AUDIENCE], and my main offer is [MAIN OFFER OR WRITE THE ONE YOU HAVE]. Design a ladder of offers at increasing price and commitment: an entry level low cost or free offer to start the relationship, a core mid tier offer, and a premium high ticket offer. For each rung give the offer, its price positioning, who it serves, and how a customer naturally moves up to the next. Explain how the ladder maximizes customer lifetime value. My tone is [TONE].
description: Build a full offer ladder from entry level to premium that raises lifetime value.
useCase: Use this to design an ascension path rather than relying on one offer. An offer ladder captures buyers at every readiness level and moves them up over time, which is how a solo business maximizes revenue from each customer.
variables: MAIN OFFER OR WRITE THE ONE YOU HAVE

title: Design a Low Ticket Entry Offer
promptText: Help me design a low ticket entry offer for my business. I am a [BUSINESS TYPE] serving [TARGET AUDIENCE], and my main offer is [MAIN OFFER]. Design an affordable entry offer that gives a quick win, builds trust, and naturally leads people toward my main offer. Give me the offer concept, what it delivers, a price point that feels like an easy yes, the format, and how it bridges to my main offer. It should deliver real standalone value while being the first step in the relationship. My tone is [TONE].
description: Design an affordable entry offer that delivers a quick win and leads to your main offer.
useCase: Use this to create a low friction first purchase that turns followers into buyers. An entry offer converts skeptical prospects into paying customers who then trust you enough to buy your bigger offers.

title: Design a Premium High Ticket Offer
promptText: Help me design a premium high ticket offer for my business. I am a [BUSINESS TYPE] serving [TARGET AUDIENCE], and I help them achieve [OUTCOME]. My best clients want [WHAT YOUR BEST CLIENTS WANT]. Design a premium offer that justifies a high price: define the elevated transformation, the high touch delivery or access, what is included that makes it worth a premium, who exactly it is for, and how to position and price it. Explain what makes buyers happy to pay a premium for it. My tone is [TONE].
description: Design a premium high ticket offer that justifies its price through real transformation.
useCase: Use this to add a high value offer for clients ready to invest more. A well designed premium offer dramatically raises revenue per client and lets you serve your most committed customers without needing more of them.

title: Turn a Service into a Productized Offer
promptText: Help me productize my service. I currently offer [YOUR CUSTOM SERVICE] to [TARGET AUDIENCE] on a custom basis, which is hard to scale. Help me turn it into a productized offer with a fixed scope, fixed price, and clear deliverables. Give me the productized version: a name, exactly what is included, the fixed scope and boundaries, a set price, and how to position the predictability as a benefit. Note what to standardize and what would need to stay flexible. Explain how this makes my business easier to sell and scale. My tone is [TONE].
description: Turn a custom service into a fixed scope, fixed price productized offer.
useCase: Use this when custom work is capping your growth. Productizing a service into a fixed scope and price makes it far easier to sell, deliver, and scale, removing the uncertainty that slows custom projects.

title: Map the Transformation Your Offer Delivers
promptText: Help me map the transformation my offer delivers so I can sell the outcome, not the features. My offer is [YOUR OFFER] for [TARGET AUDIENCE]. Map the before and after: where the customer starts (their situation, feelings, and frustrations before), where they end up (their situation, feelings, and results after), and the journey between. Then translate this into the language of the outcome they actually want. Show me how to describe my offer in terms of the transformation rather than the deliverables. My tone is [TONE].
description: Map your offer's before and after transformation so you can sell the outcome.
useCase: Use this when your offer is described by what it includes rather than what it delivers. Mapping the transformation lets you sell the outcome customers actually want, which converts far better than a list of features.

title: Find Offers Hidden in Your Skills
promptText: Help me find offers I could create from skills and experience I already have. My background and skills are [YOUR SKILLS, EXPERIENCE, AND KNOWLEDGE]. The people who ask me for help or advice tend to want [WHAT PEOPLE ASK YOU FOR]. Analyze this and surface offer opportunities I may be overlooking: things I know how to do that others would pay for, problems I have solved that others struggle with, and expertise I take for granted that is valuable. For each, suggest the offer and audience. Help me see the value I am sitting on. My tone is [TONE].
description: Surface monetizable offers hidden in skills and experience you already have.
useCase: Use this when you want to create an offer but think you have nothing to sell. People consistently undervalue their own expertise, and this surfaces offers hidden in skills you take for granted but others would pay for.

title: Build an Offer from Audience Pain
promptText: Help me build an offer around a specific pain my audience has. My audience is [TARGET AUDIENCE] and a pain point I keep noticing is [THE PAIN POINT]. They currently deal with it by [HOW THEY CURRENTLY COPE OR WRITE NOT SURE]. Design an offer that solves this pain directly: what it delivers, the format, how it removes the pain better than their current approach, who exactly it is for, and how to price it. Explain why this pain is worth building an offer around. My skills relevant to this are [YOUR RELEVANT SKILLS]. My tone is [TONE].
description: Design an offer built directly around a specific, real audience pain point.
useCase: Use this when you have spotted a recurring pain in your audience. Offers built around a real pain that people already feel and try to solve are the easiest to sell, because the demand already exists.
variables: HOW THEY CURRENTLY COPE OR WRITE NOT SURE

title: Name Your Offer
promptText: Generate name options for my offer. It helps [TARGET AUDIENCE] achieve [OUTCOME] through [FORMAT OR APPROACH]. Give me 15 name options: some that state the benefit clearly, some clever or memorable, some that sound premium, and some that name the method or system. The name should be easy to say, easy to remember, and signal the value. Avoid generic or overused terms. Mark the 3 strongest and explain what makes each work, plus flag any that might be confusing or hard to trademark. My tone is [TONE].
description: Generate memorable, value signaling name options for your offer.
useCase: Use this when you have an offer but not a name that sticks. A strong name makes an offer easier to remember, refer, and sell, and testing a range of styles helps you find one that signals value and stands out.

title: Design a Recurring Revenue Offer
promptText: Help me design an offer that generates recurring revenue. I am a [BUSINESS TYPE] serving [TARGET AUDIENCE], and I help with [WHAT YOU DO]. Design a subscription, membership, or retainer offer that customers pay for repeatedly: what ongoing value they receive that justifies recurring payment, the format and cadence, the price, and how to keep churn low by delivering continuous value. Explain why this ongoing need exists and how to make the recurring offer genuinely worth renewing. My tone is [TONE].
description: Design a subscription, membership, or retainer offer that customers renew.
useCase: Use this to build the predictable recurring income a solo business needs. A recurring offer smooths cash flow and raises customer lifetime value, but only works when it delivers continuous value worth renewing for, which this helps you design.

title: Design a Community or Membership Offer
promptText: Help me design a community or membership offer for [TARGET AUDIENCE] around [TOPIC OR SHARED GOAL]. Design what would make people join and stay: the core value and transformation, what members get access to, the ongoing experience that keeps it alive, the format and platform considerations, and pricing. Address the hard part of community, keeping it active and worth paying for over time. Explain who this suits and how it complements my other offers, which are [YOUR OTHER OFFERS OR WRITE NONE]. My tone is [TONE].
description: Design a community or membership offer people join and stay in.
useCase: Use this when your audience would benefit from ongoing connection and access. A community offer creates recurring revenue and deep loyalty, but the design must solve the hard part of keeping it active, which this addresses directly.
variables: YOUR OTHER OFFERS OR WRITE NONE

title: Brainstorm a Digital Product
promptText: Help me brainstorm a digital product I can create once and sell repeatedly. My expertise is [YOUR EXPERTISE] and my audience is [TARGET AUDIENCE]. Suggest digital product ideas such as templates, courses, toolkits, guides, or swipe files that solve a real problem for them. For each, give the product concept, what it delivers, the format, who it serves, and a price range. Prioritize products that are realistic for me to create solo and that leverage what I already know. Mark the 3 with the best effort to revenue ratio. My tone is [TONE].
description: Brainstorm digital products you can create once and sell repeatedly.
useCase: Use this to add scalable passive revenue to a service business. Digital products are created once and sold indefinitely, letting a solo operator earn beyond the hours they can personally work.

title: Define the Scope of an Offer
promptText: Help me define the exact scope of my offer so it is clear and protects me from scope creep. My offer is [YOUR OFFER] for [TARGET AUDIENCE] at [PRICE OR WRITE TBD]. Define precisely: what is included and delivered, what is explicitly not included, the boundaries and limits, what I need from the customer, and how many revisions or how much support is covered. Make the scope clear enough that both sides know exactly what they are agreeing to. Flag where scope creep typically sneaks in for offers like this and how to prevent it. My tone is [TONE].
description: Define an offer's exact scope and boundaries to prevent scope creep.
useCase: Use this before launching an offer to protect your time and margins. Clear scope boundaries set expectations upfront and prevent the unpaid extra work and disputes that erode profit on poorly defined offers.

title: Design a Beta or Pilot Offer
promptText: Help me design a beta or pilot version of my new offer so I can test it before a full launch. The offer idea is [YOUR OFFER IDEA] for [TARGET AUDIENCE]. Design a beta: how to scope it as a first version, how to price it given it is unproven, what to promise and what to be honest about, how many people to run it with, and what feedback and proof to collect. Explain how to position the beta so people want to join early despite the risk, and how the beta feeds into the full launch. My tone is [TONE].
description: Design a beta or pilot offer to test an idea before a full launch.
useCase: Use this before fully building a new offer. A beta lets you validate demand, refine the offer with real feedback, and collect early proof and testimonials, all while earning revenue and reducing the risk of a failed launch.

title: Quick Check if an Offer Idea Is Worth Building
promptText: Give me a quick reality check on an offer idea before I invest in building it. The offer is [YOUR OFFER IDEA] for [TARGET AUDIENCE], solving [THE PROBLEM] at roughly [PRICE]. Assess honestly: whether the problem is painful and urgent enough that people will pay, whether this audience actually has budget for it, whether the value is clear, what the biggest risk or flaw is, and what would need to be true for it to succeed. Be direct about weaknesses. Then tell me the fastest cheap way to test real demand before building. My tone is [TONE].
description: Get an honest reality check on an offer idea before you build it.
useCase: Use this before investing time into building an offer. An honest assessment of demand, urgency, and risk catches weak ideas early and points you to the cheapest way to validate, saving you from building something nobody buys.

title: Design a Workshop or Live Offer
promptText: Help me design a workshop, masterclass, or live training offer. I help [TARGET AUDIENCE] with [WHAT YOU DO], and I want to teach [TOPIC OR OUTCOME] live. Design the offer: the specific transformation attendees leave with, the format and length, what makes it worth attending live versus a recording, the price, and how it fits with my other offers.
