Help me set: the recurring price and billing cadence, whether to offer monthly and annual options and how to price annual to encourage commitment, whether to have tiers, and how to price so the ongoing value clearly justifies the recurring payment and keeps churn low. Explain the tradeoffs of monthly versus annual and how to make renewing feel worth it. Recommend a pricing structure with the reasoning. My tone is [TONE].
description: Design recurring pricing with cadence and tiers that justify renewal and reduce churn.
useCase: Use this to price a subscription, membership, or retainer. Setting the right cadence, monthly versus annual options, and tiers so the ongoing value clearly justifies each payment is what keeps churn low, since recurring pricing only works when renewing consistently feels worth it to the customer.

title: Plan a Pricing Experiment
promptText: Help me plan a low risk experiment to test a new price for my offer. My offer is [YOUR OFFER], currently at [CURRENT PRICE], and I want to test [NEW PRICE OR APPROACH] for [TARGET AUDIENCE]. Help me design a test: how to try the new price with a subset of prospects without disrupting existing clients, what to measure such as conversion rate, revenue, and client quality, how long to run it, and how to read the results to know whether the new price is better. Warn me against changing everything at once or reading too much into small samples. Give me a simple test plan and the decision criteria. My tone is [TONE].
description: Plan a low risk experiment to test a new price before committing to it.
useCase: Use this to test a price change safely before rolling it out. A controlled experiment with clear metrics lets you learn whether a new price improves conversion, revenue, and client quality without betting the whole business on a guess, turning pricing from anxiety into evidence based decisions.

title: Identify Your Real Competitors
promptText: Help me identify my real competitors. I am a [BUSINESS TYPE] serving [TARGET AUDIENCE], offering [YOUR OFFER]. Help me map the full competitive landscape: direct competitors who do what I do, indirect competitors who solve the same problem differently, the do it yourself or do nothing option my audience defaults to, and any surprising alternatives they spend time or money on instead. For each category give examples relevant to my business and explain why they compete for the same customer. Help me see beyond the obvious rivals to who I am truly up against for my customer's attention and budget. My tone is [TONE].
description: Map your full competitive landscape beyond the obvious direct rivals.
useCase: Use this to understand who you truly compete with. Seeing indirect competitors, the do nothing option, and surprising alternatives reveals the real battle for your customer's attention and budget, which is often lost to substitutes rather than the obvious rivals you were watching.

title: Plan Your Competitor Research
promptText: Help me plan efficient competitor research as a solo operator with limited time. My competitors are [YOUR COMPETITORS], and my goal is [YOUR GOAL, E.G. BETTER POSITIONING, PRICING, OR FINDING GAPS]. Give me a focused research plan: what to actually look at for each competitor such as their offers, pricing, messaging, content, and reviews, where to find this information, what questions to answer, and how to organize what I find so it is useful rather than overwhelming. Prioritize the highest value things to look at first given my goal. Keep it realistic for someone doing this alone in limited time. My tone is [TONE].
description: Plan focused, efficient competitor research that fits a solo operator's time.
useCase: Use this before diving into competitor research so you gather what matters. A focused plan tied to your goal keeps research from becoming an endless rabbit hole, and organizing what to look at and where to find it makes the analysis actually useful rather than a pile of scattered notes.
variables: YOUR GOAL, E.G. BETTER POSITIONING, PRICING, OR FINDING GAPS

title: Build a Competitor Profile
promptText: Help me build a profile of a specific competitor. The competitor is [COMPETITOR NAME], and here is what I know about them: [WHAT YOU KNOW, E.G. THEIR OFFERS, PRICING, MESSAGING, AUDIENCE]. My business is a [BUSINESS TYPE] serving [TARGET AUDIENCE]. Help me organize this into a useful profile: their positioning and who they target, their offers and pricing, their apparent strengths and weaknesses, their marketing approach, and what makes them succeed or struggle. Then draw out what this means for me, where they are vulnerable, and what I can learn or do differently. Flag anything I should look into further. My tone is [TONE].
description: Turn what you know about a competitor into a structured, actionable profile.
useCase: Use this to make sense of a key competitor. Organizing scattered observations into a structured profile of their positioning, offers, and weaknesses reveals where they are vulnerable and what you can do differently, turning raw information into strategic insight you can act on.
variables: WHAT YOU KNOW, E.G. THEIR OFFERS, PRICING, MESSAGING, AUDIENCE

title: Build a Competitor Comparison Matrix
promptText: Help me build a comparison matrix of my competitors and me. My business is a [BUSINESS TYPE] serving [TARGET AUDIENCE]. My competitors are [YOUR COMPETITORS], and here is what I know about each: [WHAT YOU KNOW ABOUT EACH]. Help me compare us across the dimensions that matter to my customer, such as offering, price, quality, specialization, service, and unique features. Lay it out as a clear matrix so I can see at a glance where each of us is strong and weak, and where I stand out or fall short. Then highlight the dimensions where I have a real advantage and where I have a gap to address. My tone is [TONE].
description: Build a matrix comparing you and competitors across what customers care about.
useCase: Use this to see at a glance how you stack up against competitors. A comparison matrix across the dimensions customers actually weigh reveals your genuine advantages to emphasize and your gaps to address, giving you a clear map of where to compete and where to differentiate.

title: Analyze Competitor Strengths and Weaknesses
promptText: Help me analyze the strengths and weaknesses of a competitor. The competitor is [COMPETITOR NAME], and what I know about them is [WHAT YOU KNOW]. My business is a [BUSINESS TYPE] serving [TARGET AUDIENCE]. Help me honestly assess: what they genuinely do well that I can learn from or should respect, and where they are weak, frustrating customers, or leaving needs unmet. Look for weaknesses in their offer, service, pricing, experience, or positioning. Then tell me how to learn from their strengths without copying, and how to exploit their weaknesses by being better where they fall short. Be objective, not dismissive. My tone is [TONE].
description: Honestly assess a competitor's strengths to learn from and weaknesses to exploit.
useCase: Use this to learn from and outmaneuver a competitor. Objectively assessing what they do well, so you can respect and learn from it, and where they fall short, so you can be better there, turns a competitor into both a teacher and a target rather than just a threat to fear.

title: Find Gaps Competitors Leave Open
promptText: Help me find gaps in the market that my competitors leave open. My business is a [BUSINESS TYPE] serving [TARGET AUDIENCE]. Here is what my competitors offer and how they position: [COMPETITOR OFFERS AND POSITIONING]. Help me identify the openings: underserved segments nobody focuses on, needs or problems no one addresses well, service or experience gaps that frustrate customers, price points nobody occupies, and angles or messaging everyone ignores. For each gap, assess whether it is a real opportunity for me given my strengths, which are [YOUR STRENGTHS]. Recommend the most promising gap to pursue and why. My tone is [TONE].
description: Find the underserved gaps competitors leave open for you to claim.
useCase: Use this to find where to compete instead of fighting head on. Identifying underserved segments, unmet needs, and empty price points that competitors ignore reveals openings where you can win, since claiming a gap is far easier than out competing everyone on the crowded ground they all fight over.

title: Differentiate Yourself from Competitors
promptText: Help me differentiate my business from competitors who look similar. My business is a [BUSINESS TYPE] serving [TARGET AUDIENCE], offering [YOUR OFFER]. My competitors are [YOUR COMPETITORS], and the ways we look alike are [HOW YOU LOOK SIMILAR]. My genuine strengths and differences are [YOUR STRENGTHS AND DIFFERENCES]. Help me identify and sharpen what truly sets me apart: my real points of difference, how to make them matter to customers, and how to communicate them so I am the obvious choice rather than one of many similar options. Avoid generic claims everyone makes. Give me a clear differentiation angle and messaging. My tone is [TONE].
description: Sharpen and communicate what genuinely sets you apart from similar competitors.
useCase: Use this when you blend in with similar competitors. Identifying your genuine points of difference and making them matter to customers is what turns you from one of many options into the obvious choice, since in a crowded market clear differentiation often beats being marginally better.

title: Position Yourself Against Competitors
promptText: Help me position my business strategically against my competitors. My business is a [BUSINESS TYPE] serving [TARGET AUDIENCE]. My main competitors and how they position are [COMPETITORS AND THEIR POSITIONING]. My strengths are [YOUR STRENGTHS]. Help me find the positioning space I can own: where competitors are clustered and crowded, where there is open ground that fits my strengths, and the specific position that makes me distinct and preferable for my ideal customer. Give me a positioning statement and the angle to lead with. Explain how to plant myself where I can win rather than fighting on their turf. My tone is [TONE].
description: Find and claim the positioning space where you can win against competitors.
useCase: Use this to position where you can win rather than where everyone crowds. Finding the open ground that fits your strengths, away from where competitors cluster, lets you own a distinct position, which is far more effective than fighting for the same customers on the same claims as everyone else.

title: Research How Competitors Price
promptText: Help me analyze how my competitors price so I can position my own pricing. My business is a [BUSINESS TYPE] serving [TARGET AUDIENCE]. Here is what I know about competitor pricing: [COMPETITOR PRICES AND WHAT THEY INCLUDE]. Help me interpret it: the price ranges in my market and what each tier includes, how competitors justify their prices, where the gaps and opportunities are, and how I should price relative to them given my positioning. Warn me against simply undercutting. Recommend where my pricing should sit and how to justify it through value rather than being the cheapest. Note what to investigate further. My tone is [TONE].
description: Analyze competitor pricing to position yours through value, not undercutting.
useCase: Use this to price intelligently relative to competitors. Understanding their price ranges, how they justify them, and where the gaps sit lets you position your pricing through differentiated value rather than undercutting, avoiding the race to the bottom that erodes solo business margins.
variables: COMPETITOR PRICES AND WHAT THEY INCLUDE

title: Analyze Competitor Messaging
promptText: Help me analyze how my competitors talk about themselves so I can message better. Here is competitor copy, taglines, or positioning: [PASTE COMPETITOR MESSAGING]. My business is a [BUSINESS TYPE] serving [TARGET AUDIENCE]. Help me identify: the common themes and claims they all make, the language and angles they rely on, where they sound identical to each other, and the messaging space none of them occupy. Then show me how to message differently to stand out, including the angle and words competitors are ignoring that I could own. Give me messaging directions that break from the pack. My tone is [TONE].
description: Analyze competitor messaging to find the angle none of them own.
useCase: Use this when competitors all sound the same, which is most markets. Analyzing their shared themes and claims reveals the messaging space none occupy, and claiming that ignored angle lets you stand out, since differentiated messaging often matters more than a differentiated offer.

title: Mine Competitor Reviews for Insight
promptText: Help me extract insight from my competitors' customer reviews. Here are reviews of a competitor, both positive and negative: [PASTE COMPETITOR REVIEWS]. My business is a [BUSINESS TYPE] serving [TARGET AUDIENCE]. Help me mine them for gold: what customers love that I should make sure I also deliver, what customers complain about that I could do better, the exact language customers use to describe their needs and frustrations, and unmet needs or requests hiding in the reviews. Then tell me how to use these insights to improve my offer, sharpen my messaging, and win over their dissatisfied customers. My tone is [TONE].
description: Mine competitor reviews for unmet needs, complaints, and customer language.
useCase: Use this to turn competitor reviews into a goldmine of insight. Their reviews reveal what customers love, what frustrates them, and the exact words they use, letting you improve your offer, sharpen your messaging with real customer language, and win over the customers your competitors are disappointing.

title: Turn Competitor Weaknesses into Your Advantage
promptText: Help me turn my competitors' weaknesses into my competitive advantage. My competitors' weaknesses and common customer complaints about them are [COMPETITOR WEAKNESSES AND COMPLAINTS]. My business is a [BUSINESS TYPE] serving [TARGET AUDIENCE], and my relevant strengths are [YOUR STRENGTHS]. Help me: identify which weaknesses I can genuinely do better on, build my offer and experience to solve exactly what frustrates their customers, and message my advantage so their dissatisfied customers see me as the better choice, without directly bashing competitors. Give me the specific advantages to build and how to communicate them. My tone is [TONE].
description: Build and message advantages around exactly where competitors fall short.
useCase: Use this to convert competitor weaknesses into your selling points. Building your offer and experience to solve exactly what frustrates their customers, then messaging it without bashing anyone, positions you as the better choice for the many customers quietly unhappy with the alternatives.

title: Win Over a Competitor's Customers
promptText: Help me win over customers who currently use a competitor. My business is a [BUSINESS TYPE] serving [TARGET AUDIENCE], offering [YOUR OFFER]. The competitor they use is [COMPETITOR], and common frustrations with that competitor are [KNOWN FRUSTRATIONS]. My genuine advantages are [YOUR ADVANTAGES]. Help me: reach and speak to people already using a competitor, address the frustrations that would make them consider switching, lower the perceived cost or risk of switching to me, and position myself as the clear upgrade without trashing their current provider. Give me the approach and the messaging to convert switchers. My tone is [TONE].
description: Reach and convert a competitor's dissatisfied customers into your own.
useCase: Use this to target customers already using a competitor. Speaking to their known frustrations, lowering the risk of switching, and positioning yourself as the upgrade converts switchers who are the warmest kind of prospect, since they already have the problem and are primed to leave when a better option appears.

title: Build an Us Versus Them Comparison
promptText: Help me build a fair comparison of my offer against a competitor or the typical alternative, for a comparison page or sales conversation. My offer is [YOUR OFFER] for [TARGET AUDIENCE], and the alternative is [COMPETITOR OR TYPICAL ALTERNATIVE]. My genuine advantages are [YOUR ADVANTAGES], and where the alternative is stronger is [THEIR STRENGTHS, IF ANY]. Help me create an honest comparison that highlights where I win on the dimensions that matter to my customer, acknowledges the alternative fairly to build trust, and helps the right customer choose me. Avoid unfair or misleading claims. Give me the comparison structure and content. My tone is [TONE].
description: Build an honest us versus them comparison that helps the right customer choose you.
useCase: Use this to help prospects comparing you against an alternative. An honest comparison that highlights where you win while fairly acknowledging the alternative builds trust and helps the right customer choose you, converting comparison shoppers better than one sided claims that read as biased.

title: Analyze Indirect Competitors
promptText: Help me analyze my indirect competitors, the alternatives my customers choose instead of any direct option. My business is a [BUSINESS TYPE] serving [TARGET AUDIENCE], solving [THE PROBLEM]. The indirect alternatives my audience uses are [INDIRECT ALTERNATIVES, E.G. DIY, A DIFFERENT TYPE OF SOLUTION, OR DOING NOTHING]. Help me understand: why customers choose these alternatives over a solution like mine, what those alternatives get right that I must match or beat, and how to position my offer as clearly better than the workaround they currently rely on. Give me how to compete against these non obvious rivals. My tone is [TONE].
description: Analyze the indirect alternatives customers choose instead of any direct option.
useCase: Use this because your biggest competitor is often not another business. Understanding why customers choose DIY, a different type of solution, or doing nothing, and how to position against those workarounds, addresses the real reason many prospects never buy from anyone in your category.
variables: INDIRECT ALTERNATIVES, E.G. DIY, A DIFFERENT TYPE OF SOLUTION, OR DOING NOTHING

title: Analyze Competitor Content and Marketing
promptText: Help me analyze a competitor's content and marketing to inform my own. Here is what I know about their content and marketing: [WHAT THEY DO, E.G. TOPICS, CHANNELS, FREQUENCY, STYLE]. My business is a [BUSINESS TYPE] serving [TARGET AUDIENCE]. Help me learn from it: what topics and content types seem to work for them, which channels they focus on, the gaps in what they cover that I could own, and where I could do content better or differently. Then tell me how to use this to shape a content approach that stands apart rather than imitating them. Give me the content opportunities I should pursue. My tone is [TONE].
description: Analyze competitor content and marketing to find gaps and better approaches.
useCase: Use this to inform your content strategy with competitor insight. Learning what works for them, which channels they use, and the topics they leave uncovered lets you find content gaps to own and do things better, rather than imitating an approach that is already crowded.
variables: WHAT THEY DO, E.G. TOPICS, CHANNELS, FREQUENCY, STYLE

title: Learn from a Successful Competitor
promptText: Help me learn from a competitor who is clearly succeeding, without just copying them. The successful competitor is [COMPETITOR], and what seems to be working for them is [WHAT SEEMS TO WORK]. My business is a [BUSINESS TYPE] serving [TARGET AUDIENCE]. Help me understand: the likely reasons behind their success, which of their moves are worth adapting to my situation and which would not fit me, the principles behind what they do rather than the surface tactics, and how to apply the lessons in my own authentic way. Warn me against copying tactics that only work because of their specific context. Give me the transferable lessons. My tone is [TONE].
description: Extract transferable lessons from a successful competitor without copying them.
useCase: Use this to learn from a thriving competitor intelligently. Understanding the principles behind their success, rather than copying surface tactics that only work in their context, lets you adapt the real lessons to your own situation authentically, which beats imitation that rarely transfers.

title: Run a Competitive SWOT Analysis
promptText: Help me run a SWOT analysis of my business relative to my competitors. My business is a [BUSINESS TYPE] serving [TARGET AUDIENCE], offering [YOUR OFFER]. My main competitors are [YOUR COMPETITORS], and relevant context about my situation is [YOUR CONTEXT]. Help me map: my strengths versus competitors, my weaknesses relative to them, the opportunities in the market they are not capturing, and the threats they or the market pose to me. Make each point specific and competitive, not generic. Then draw out the strategic priorities: which strengths to lean into, which weaknesses to fix, and which opportunities to pursue. My tone is [TONE].
description: Run a competitive SWOT that produces specific strategic priorities.
useCase: Use this to get a strategic overview of your competitive position. A SWOT framed specifically against competitors, rather than in a vacuum, surfaces which strengths to lean into, weaknesses to fix, and opportunities to pursue, turning a familiar framework into a concrete action plan.

title: Set Up Ongoing Competitor Monitoring
promptText: Help me set up a simple system to keep an eye on my competitors over time without it consuming me. My competitors are [YOUR COMPETITORS], and my business is a [BUSINESS TYPE]. Give me a lightweight ongoing monitoring approach: what to check periodically such as their offers, pricing, and messaging changes, how often to check without obsessing, easy ways to stay informed like following their channels or setting alerts, and how to capture and act on anything important I notice. Warn me against unhealthy competitor obsession that distracts from my own business. Give me a realistic routine for a solo operator. My tone is [TONE].
description: Set up lightweight ongoing competitor monitoring without obsessing.
useCase: Use this to stay aware of competitor moves without losing focus on your own business. A lightweight routine for checking their changes periodically keeps you informed of shifts that matter, while guarding against the unhealthy obsession that wastes time better spent building your own advantage.

title: Plan a Response to a Competitor Move
promptText: Help me decide how to respond to a competitor move. A competitor just [WHAT THEY DID, E.G. DROPPED PRICES, LAUNCHED A NEW OFFER, OR CHANGED POSITIONING]. My business is a [BUSINESS TYPE] serving [TARGET AUDIENCE]. Help me think it through calmly: whether this actually threatens me or just feels alarming, whether to respond at all or hold my course, the range of possible responses and their tradeoffs, and how to react from strategy rather than panic. Warn me against knee jerk reactions like matching a price cut that could hurt me more than them. Give me a clear recommendation and reasoning. My tone is [TONE].
description: Decide how to respond to a competitor move from strategy, not panic.
useCase: Use this when a competitor makes a move that rattles you. Assessing calmly whether it truly threatens you and weighing responses from strategy rather than panic prevents damaging knee jerk reactions, like matching a price cut, that often hurt you more than the competitor who provoked them.
variables: WHAT THEY DID, E.G. DROPPED PRICES, LAUNCHED A NEW OFFER, OR CHANGED POSITIONING

title: Turn Competitor Analysis into an Action Plan
promptText: Help me turn my competitor research into a concrete action plan. Here is what I learned from analyzing my competitors: [YOUR KEY FINDINGS]. My business is a [BUSINESS TYPE] serving [TARGET AUDIENCE], and my goal is [YOUR GOAL]. Help me convert insight into action: the specific changes to make to my offer, pricing, messaging, or positioning based on what I learned, prioritized by impact and effort, and what to do first. Keep me from letting the research just sit as interesting notes. Give me a short prioritized action plan I can actually execute as a solo operator. My tone is [TONE].
description: Convert competitor research findings into a prioritized, executable action plan.
useCase: Use this so competitor research leads to action, not just notes. Converting findings into specific prioritized changes to your offer, pricing, and positioning ensures the analysis actually improves your business, since most competitor research is wasted sitting as interesting observations nobody acts on.

title: Keep Competitor Focus Healthy
promptText: Help me keep a healthy relationship with watching competitors. I find myself [YOUR PATTERN, E.G. CONSTANTLY CHECKING COMPETITORS, FEELING DISCOURAGED, OR COPYING THEM]. My business is a [BUSINESS TYPE] serving [TARGET AUDIENCE]. Help me: know how much competitor attention is genuinely useful versus counterproductive, refocus on my own customers and strengths rather than reacting to competitors, use competitor awareness as input without letting it drive my decisions or dent my confidence, and build a mindset where I run my own race. Give me practical guidance to stay informed without the obsession or comparison spiral. My tone is [TONE].
description: Keep competitor attention useful without spiraling into obsession or comparison.
useCase: Use this when watching competitors starts hurting more than helping. Refocusing on your own customers and strengths, and using competitor awareness as input rather than a driver, protects your confidence and direction, since obsessive comparison drains energy better spent building your own advantage.
variables: YOUR PATTERN, E.G. CONSTANTLY CHECKING COMPETITORS, FEELING DISCOURAGED, OR COPYING THEM

title: Create a Market Positioning Map
promptText: Help me create a positioning map of my market to see where I fit. My business is a [BUSINESS TYPE] serving [TARGET AUDIENCE]. My competitors are [YOUR COMPETITORS]. Help me: choose the two most meaningful dimensions to map my market on, such as price versus specialization or premium versus accessible, plot where each competitor sits on those axes, identify where the crowded and empty spaces are, and find the open quadrant I could own. Then recommend where I should position myself on the map and why that spot is winnable. Describe the map clearly since I will sketch it out. My tone is [TONE].
description: Map your market on two key dimensions to find the open space you can own.
useCase: Use this to visualize where you and competitors sit in the market. Plotting everyone on two meaningful dimensions reveals the crowded zones to avoid and the empty quadrant you could own, turning abstract positioning into a clear picture of where you can win.

title: Analyze Competition Before Entering a Market
promptText: Help me analyze the competitive landscape before I enter a new market or niche. The market I am considering entering is [THE MARKET OR NICHE], and my business is a [BUSINESS TYPE]. What I know about who is already there is [WHAT YOU KNOW ABOUT EXISTING PLAYERS]. Help me assess: how crowded or open the market is, whether the existing competition signals healthy demand or a saturated field, where the gaps and openings are for a new entrant, how hard it would be to compete given the incumbents, and whether I have a realistic path to stand out. Be honest if the market looks too crowded to enter well. Give me your assessment and recommendation. My tone is [TONE].
description: Assess the competitive landscape before committing to enter a new market.
useCase: Use this before entering a new market or niche. Assessing whether the existing competition signals healthy demand or saturation, and whether you have a realistic path to stand out, prevents entering a field too crowded to win, one of the costliest mistakes when expanding.
variables: WHAT YOU KNOW ABOUT EXISTING PLAYERS

title: Respond to a Positive Review
promptText: Write a response to this positive review for my [BUSINESS TYPE]: [PASTE THE REVIEW]. The customer praised [WHAT THEY PRAISED]. Write a warm, genuine reply that thanks them specifically for what they mentioned rather than a generic thank you, reinforces the thing they loved, adds a personal touch that shows a real person read it, and where natural, gently invites them back or to try something else. Keep it authentic and not overly formal or salesy. Match my brand voice, which is [BRAND VOICE]. Give me two versions of different lengths.
description: Write a warm, specific reply to a positive review that feels genuinely personal.
useCase: Use this when a happy customer leaves a good review. A specific personal response reinforces their goodwill, shows future readers you engage with customers, and turns a review into a relationship, since generic thank you replies waste the opportunity a positive review gives you.

title: Respond to a Negative Review
promptText: Write a response to this negative review for my [BUSINESS TYPE]: [PASTE THE REVIEW]. The complaint is about [THE COMPLAINT], and here is my side or context: [YOUR CONTEXT OR WHAT HAPPENED]. Write a calm, professional reply that acknowledges their experience without being defensive, takes appropriate responsibility, avoids arguing publicly, offers to make it right or move the conversation offline, and shows other readers that I handle problems with grace. Do not make excuses or blame the customer. Keep my dignity while prioritizing de escalation. My brand voice is [BRAND VOICE]. Give me one firmer and one softer version.
description: Write a calm, non defensive response to a negative review that reassures readers.
useCase: Use this when facing a negative review, where your reply matters more than the review itself. A calm, gracious response reassures the many future customers who read it that you handle problems well, while defensiveness or arguing publicly does far more damage than the original complaint.

title: Respond to an Angry Review
promptText: Write a response to this angry, emotional review for my [BUSINESS TYPE]: [PASTE THE REVIEW]. The customer is clearly upset about [THE ISSUE]. Write a reply that de escalates: leads with genuine empathy for their frustration, stays completely calm and never matches their tone, validates the emotion without necessarily agreeing with every claim, takes a step toward resolution, and invites them to continue privately. The goal is to lower the temperature and show other readers I stay composed under pressure. Do not get drawn into a public argument. My brand voice is [BRAND VOICE]. Keep it measured and human.
description: Write a de escalating response to an angry, emotional review that lowers the temperature.
useCase: Use this for a heated, emotional review. Leading with empathy and staying composed lowers the temperature and shows readers you handle even angry customers with grace, since matching their tone or getting defensive turns one bad review into a public spectacle that scares off prospects.

title: Respond to an Unfair or False Review
promptText: Write a response to this review that I believe is unfair or inaccurate for my [BUSINESS TYPE]: [PASTE THE REVIEW]. What is unfair or false about it is [WHAT IS INACCURATE], and the real situation was [WHAT ACTUALLY HAPPENED]. Write a reply that calmly and professionally sets the record straight for other readers without attacking the reviewer, states my side factually and briefly, stays gracious even though the review feels unjust, and lets readers judge for themselves. Do not sound bitter, defensive, or combative, since that hurts me more than the review. My brand voice is [BRAND VOICE]. Give me a version that corrects the record with dignity.
description: Respond to an unfair or false review by calmly setting the record straight.
useCase: Use this when a review is inaccurate or unjust. Calmly stating your side for other readers, without attacking the reviewer or sounding bitter, lets prospects judge fairly, since a defensive or combative reply to an unfair review makes you look worse than the false claim itself.

title: Move a Review Conversation Offline
promptText: Write a public reply to this review that acknowledges the issue and moves the conversation offline to resolve it, for my [BUSINESS TYPE]: [PASTE THE REVIEW]. The issue is [THE ISSUE], and how I want them to reach me is [CONTACT METHOD]. Write a short reply that shows I take it seriously, expresses a genuine desire to resolve it, and clearly invites them to contact me directly to make it right, without airing all the details publicly. It should signal to other readers that I resolve problems, while keeping the resolution private. My brand voice is [BRAND VOICE]. Keep it brief and constructive.
description: Write a public reply that acknowledges an issue and moves it offline to resolve.
useCase: Use this when a review needs real resolution best handled privately. A short public reply that takes it seriously and invites direct contact shows readers you fix problems, while moving the details offline avoids a drawn out public back and forth that nobody benefits from watching.

title: Thank a Customer for a Detailed Review
promptText: Write a response to this detailed, thoughtful positive review for my [BUSINESS TYPE]: [PASTE THE REVIEW]. The customer took real time to describe [WHAT THEY DESCRIBED IN DETAIL]. Write a reply that matches their effort with a genuine, specific response, acknowledges the particular things they highlighted, shows real appreciation for the time they took, and reinforces the details that would matter most to future readers. Avoid a short generic thanks that would feel dismissive of their effort. My brand voice is [BRAND VOICE]. Make it warm and personal.
description: Write a thoughtful reply that matches the effort of a detailed positive review.
useCase: Use this when a customer writes a long, detailed positive review. Matching their effort with a specific genuine response honors the time they took and highlights the details that persuade future readers, since a brief generic thanks feels dismissive of a review that was clearly written with care.

title: Build a Bank of Review Response Templates
promptText: Build me a bank of review response templates for my [BUSINESS TYPE] so I can reply quickly while staying personal. My brand voice is [BRAND VOICE]. Create adaptable templates with fill in the blank spots for: a glowing five star review, a positive review with a minor gripe, a lukewarm middle review, a negative review, and an angry review. Each template should have a genuine, on brand structure I can quickly personalize rather than paste verbatim. Include notes on what to customize each time so responses never feel canned. Keep them warm and human, not corporate.
description: Build adaptable review response templates that stay personal and on brand.
useCase: Use this once to speed up review responses without sounding canned. A bank of adaptable templates for each review type lets you reply quickly and consistently while still personalizing each one, which is how a busy solo operator keeps up with reviews without either ignoring them or sounding robotic.

title: Adjust a Review Response to Your Voice
promptText: Here is a draft review response: [PASTE YOUR DRAFT]. It does not sound like my brand. Rewrite it to match my voice, which is [DESCRIBE YOUR BRAND VOICE OR PASTE A SAMPLE]. Keep the substance and intent intact but adjust the tone, warmth, and word choice so it sounds authentically like my business. My [BUSINESS TYPE] serves [TARGET AUDIENCE]. Show me the adjusted version and note what changed so I can keep future responses consistent. Do not make it more formal or corporate than my actual voice.
description: Rewrite a review response to authentically match your brand voice.
useCase: Use this when a review reply feels generic or off brand. Adjusting it to match your authentic voice keeps your public responses consistent with how your business actually sounds, since reviews are a visible touchpoint where a corporate or mismatched tone undermines the personal brand customers came for.
variables: DESCRIBE YOUR BRAND VOICE OR PASTE A SAMPLE

title: Respond to a Mixed Review
promptText: Write a response to this mixed review for my [BUSINESS TYPE]: [PASTE THE REVIEW]. They liked [WHAT THEY LIKED] but were unhappy about [WHAT THEY DISLIKED]. Write a balanced reply that genuinely thanks them for the positive, acknowledges the criticism without being defensive, addresses or offers to resolve the negative part, and leaves the overall impression positive and constructive. Do not gloss over the complaint or over apologize for it. Show future readers I take both praise and criticism gracefully. My brand voice is [BRAND VOICE]. Keep it balanced and sincere.
description: Write a balanced response to a mixed review that handles praise and criticism.
useCase: Use this for a review that mixes praise with a complaint. A balanced reply that thanks them for the good and constructively addresses the bad shows readers you handle both gracefully, since these nuanced reviews are common and often more believable to prospects than purely glowing ones.

title: Turn a Critic into a Fan Through Your Response
promptText: Help me respond to this critical review in a way that could turn the unhappy customer into a fan, for my [BUSINESS TYPE]: [PASTE THE REVIEW]. Their complaint is [THE COMPLAINT], and what I can genuinely offer to make it right is [YOUR RESOLUTION OR GESTURE]. Write a reply that responds so well they feel heard and valued, makes a genuine effort to fix the situation, and gives them a reason to update their opinion of my business. Explain how an exceptional response to a complaint can create more loyalty than if nothing had gone wrong. My brand voice is [BRAND VOICE]. Aim to win them back sincerely.
description: Respond to a critical review in a way that can turn the critic into a loyal fan.
useCase: Use this when you have a genuine chance to win back an unhappy reviewer. An exceptional response that makes them feel heard and fixes the problem can create more loyalty than if nothing had gone wrong, turning a public complaint into a public example of how well you treat customers.

title: Follow Up After Resolving a Review Issue
promptText: Help me follow up with a customer after I resolved the issue behind their negative review for my [BUSINESS TYPE]. The original complaint was [THE COMPLAINT], and how I resolved it was [HOW YOU FIXED IT]. Write a message to send them privately that confirms the resolution, checks they are now satisfied, thanks them for giving me the chance to fix it, and, if it feels appropriate and genuine, gently invites them to update their review to reflect the resolution, without pressuring them. Keep the focus on their satisfaction, not on getting the review changed. My brand voice is [BRAND VOICE].
description: Follow up after resolving a review issue and gently invite an update.
useCase: Use this after fixing the problem behind a negative review. A follow up that confirms satisfaction and gently invites an updated review, without pressure, often recovers your rating, since a resolved customer will frequently update or add a review when asked genuinely at the right moment.

title: Ask a Customer to Update a Review
promptText: Write a message asking a customer to update their old negative review for my [BUSINESS TYPE], now that the situation has changed or improved. The original issue was [ORIGINAL ISSUE], and what has changed or been resolved is [WHAT CHANGED]. Write a genuine, low pressure message that reconnects warmly, reminds them of the resolution or improvement, and politely invites them to update their review if they feel it no longer reflects their experience. Make it easy and pressure free, and be gracious whether or not they do it. Do not sound like I am only reaching out to fix my rating. My brand voice is [BRAND VOICE].
description: Ask a customer to update an old negative review after things have improved.
useCase: Use this to address an old negative review after resolving or improving things. A genuine low pressure request to update it, framed around their changed experience rather than your rating, gives satisfied former critics an easy path to revise a review that no longer reflects reality.

title: Ask Happy Customers for Reviews
promptText: Write a message asking a happy customer to leave a review for my [BUSINESS TYPE]. The customer recently [WHAT THEY EXPERIENCED OR ACHIEVED], and I want the review on [PLATFORM]. Write a warm, genuine request that acknowledges their positive experience, explains simply why reviews help my small business, makes it as easy as possible to leave one with a clear next step, and stays low pressure so it never feels like begging. Time it to a moment when they are happy. Give me a version for email and a shorter version for text or in person. My brand voice is [BRAND VOICE].
description: Write a warm, easy request for happy customers to leave a review.
useCase: Use this to proactively build reviews from satisfied customers. A warm, easy request timed to a happy moment turns good experiences into public proof, since most happy customers never think to review unless asked, and a steady flow of reviews is one of the strongest trust signals for a small business.

title: Plan the Best Timing to Request Reviews
promptText: Help me figure out the best moments to ask customers for reviews for my [BUSINESS TYPE] serving [TARGET AUDIENCE]. My customer journey looks like [DESCRIBE YOUR JOURNEY OR KEY MOMENTS]. Help me identify: the peak satisfaction moments when a review request will land best, the moments to avoid asking, the natural triggers that signal a customer is happy enough to ask, and how to build review requests into my process so I ask consistently rather than sporadically. Give me a simple timing plan I can follow. My brand voice is [BRAND VOICE].
description: Identify the peak moments to request reviews and build them into your process.
useCase: Use this to ask for reviews when customers are most likely to say yes. Identifying peak satisfaction moments and building requests into your process, rather than asking randomly, dramatically increases how many reviews you collect, turning review generation from an afterthought into a reliable system.
variables: DESCRIBE YOUR JOURNEY OR KEY MOMENTS

title: Write a Gentle Review Request Sequence
promptText: Write a short, gentle sequence to request a review from a customer of my [BUSINESS TYPE], for those who do not respond to a single ask. The customer experienced [POSITIVE EXPERIENCE], and I want reviews on [PLATFORM]. Write two messages: a warm initial request, and a light friendly reminder a few days later for those who did not act, without any guilt or pressure. Make each easy to act on with a clear link or step. Keep it respectful, since a review is a favor. Give me both messages and a note on timing. My brand voice is [BRAND VOICE].
description: Write a gentle two step review request sequence with a pressure free reminder.
useCase: Use this because most customers who would review need a gentle reminder. A warm initial ask plus one light follow up captures the many happy customers who meant to review but forgot, meaningfully increasing your review count without pressuring anyone or feeling like nagging.

title: Build a System to Generate Reviews Consistently
promptText: Help me build a simple system to consistently generate reviews for my [BUSINESS TYPE] serving [TARGET AUDIENCE]. I currently get reviews [YOUR CURRENT SITUATION, E.G. RARELY OR ONLY WHEN I REMEMBER]. Design a lightweight repeatable system: the trigger point in my process to ask, the message to send, how to make it effortless for customers, how to track who was asked, and how to keep it running without it becoming a burden. The goal is a steady stream of reviews on autopilot rather than sporadic scrambles. Keep it realistic for a solo operator. My brand voice is [BRAND VOICE].
description: Build a lightweight repeatable system for generating reviews on autopilot.
useCase: Use this to turn review generation into a consistent system rather than sporadic effort. A repeatable process with a set trigger and message produces a steady stream of reviews without constant thought, which compounds over time into the volume of social proof that builds trust and wins new customers.
variables: YOUR CURRENT SITUATION, E.G. RARELY OR ONLY WHEN I REMEMBER

title: Turn a Great Review into Marketing
promptText: Help me turn this great review into marketing content for my [BUSINESS TYPE]: [PASTE THE REVIEW]. My audience is [TARGET AUDIENCE], and my platforms are [YOUR PLATFORMS]. Help me repurpose it: pull the strongest quotable line, suggest how to present it as a social proof post or graphic, frame it as a mini story rather than just a screenshot, and write caption or context to pair with it so it persuades rather than brags. Keep the customer's real words where they are strong. Give me a couple of ways to feature this review across my marketing. My brand voice is [BRAND VOICE].
description: Turn a great review into persuasive social proof marketing content.
useCase: Use this to get marketing value from your best reviews. Repurposing a strong review into social proof content, framed as a story rather than a bare screenshot, gives you persuasive material that reassures prospects, extending the value of a single review far beyond the platform it was left on.

title: Respond to a Vague or Low Star Review With No Detail
promptText: Write a response to this low star review that gives no explanation, for my [BUSINESS TYPE]: [PASTE THE REVIEW OR NOTE IT IS JUST A LOW RATING]. I do not know what went wrong. Write a reply that professionally acknowledges the low rating, expresses genuine desire to understand and make it right, and invites them to share what happened so I can address it, without sounding defensive or assuming the worst. Show other readers I care even about vague criticism. Keep it short, sincere, and constructive. My brand voice is [BRAND VOICE]. Give me a version that opens the door without pleading.
description: Respond to a vague low star review that gives no explanation of the problem.
useCase: Use this for the frustrating low rating left with no detail. A sincere reply that invites the customer to explain shows other readers you care and want to improve, and it sometimes surfaces the real issue, turning an unexplained rating into a chance to resolve and learn.
variables: PASTE THE REVIEW OR NOTE IT IS JUST A LOW RATING

title: Set Your Review Response Policy
promptText: Help me set a simple policy for how I handle reviews for my [BUSINESS TYPE], so I respond consistently and never react emotionally. Help me decide: which reviews to respond to, how quickly to respond, the tone and approach for positive versus negative reviews, when to take things offline, how to handle unfair or fake reviews, and rules to keep myself from responding while angry. Give me a short clear policy I can follow every time a review comes in. The goal is consistent, professional, on brand responses without agonizing over each one. My brand voice is [BRAND VOICE].
description: Set a clear personal policy for handling reviews consistently and calmly.
useCase: Use this to handle all reviews consistently without emotional reactions. A simple policy for which reviews to answer, how fast, in what tone, and rules against responding while angry keeps your public responses professional and on brand every time, removing both the agonizing and the risk of a heated reply you regret.

title: Find Patterns Across Your Reviews
promptText: Help me find patterns across my reviews to improve my [BUSINESS TYPE]. Here are my recent reviews, positive and negative: [PASTE YOUR REVIEWS]. Help me analyze them for insight: the themes customers consistently praise that are my real strengths, the recurring complaints or friction points I should fix, the language customers use that I could adopt in my marketing, and any surprising patterns. Then give me a short list of what to keep doing and what to improve, prioritized by how often it comes up. Turn my reviews from scattered feedback into an improvement roadmap. My audience is [TARGET AUDIENCE].
description: Analyze your reviews for patterns that reveal strengths to keep and issues to fix.
useCase: Use this to mine your reviews for business insight, not just individual replies. Finding the recurring praise and complaints across many reviews reveals your real strengths and the friction points costing you customers, turning scattered feedback into a prioritized roadmap for what to keep and what to improve.

title: Respond to a Review About a Team Member or Product
promptText: Write a response to this review that specifically mentions [WHAT OR WHO THEY MENTIONED, E.G. A TEAM MEMBER, A SPECIFIC PRODUCT, OR AN EXPERIENCE], for my [BUSINESS TYPE]: [PASTE THE REVIEW]. The review is [POSITIVE OR NEGATIVE], and the relevant context is [YOUR CONTEXT]. Write a reply that responds appropriately to the specific mention: if positive, celebrating and reinforcing it, and if negative, addressing the specific concern with care and appropriate accountability. Keep it genuine and specific to what they named rather than generic. My brand voice is [BRAND VOICE]. Give me the response.
description: Respond to a review that names a specific team member, product, or experience.
useCase: Use this for reviews that call out something specific, like a product, a team member, or a particular experience. A response tailored to exactly what they named, whether celebrating praise or addressing a concern, feels genuine and attentive, unlike a generic reply that ignores the specific thing the customer took time to mention.
variables: WHAT OR WHO THEY MENTIONED, E.G. A TEAM MEMBER, A SPECIFIC PRODUCT, OR AN EXPERIENCE

title: Write a Quick Review Response Fast
promptText: I need to respond to this review quickly for my [BUSINESS TYPE]: [PASTE THE REVIEW]. Give me a genuine, on brand response fast, with no fuss. The review is [POSITIVE, NEGATIVE, OR MIXED]. Keep the reply appropriately warm for a positive review or calm and constructive for a negative one, specific to what they said, and ready to post with only minor tweaks. My brand voice is [BRAND VOICE]. Just give me a solid ready to use response I can post in under a minute.
description: Get a solid, ready to post review response quickly with minimal effort.
useCase: Use this when you just need a good review response fast. A quick on brand reply ready to post in under a minute keeps you responsive to reviews even on busy days, since prompt responses signal an engaged business and prevent reviews from sitting unanswered, which reads as neglect to prospects.

title: Tailor a Review Response to the Platform
promptText: Help me tailor my response to this review for the specific platform it is on, for my [BUSINESS TYPE]. The review is on [PLATFORM, E.G. GOOGLE, YELP, FACEBOOK, OR A MARKETPLACE], and here it is: [PASTE THE REVIEW]. Write a response suited to that platform's norms and audience: the appropriate length, tone, and formality for that platform, awareness of how public and searchable it is, and any platform specific considerations. Explain what makes a response work well on this particular platform. My brand voice is [BRAND VOICE]. Give me the platform appropriate response.
description: Tailor a review response to the norms and audience of the specific platform.
useCase: Use this because a response that fits Google may feel wrong on Facebook or a marketplace. Tailoring length, tone, and formality to each platform's norms makes your responses land better with that platform's audience, since the same reply rarely works identically across the different places customers leave reviews.
variables: PLATFORM, E.G. GOOGLE, YELP, FACEBOOK, OR A MARKETPLACE

title: Respond to a Review About a Genuine Mistake
promptText: Write a response to this review about a genuine mistake I made, for my [BUSINESS TYPE]: [PASTE THE REVIEW]. I did get it wrong: [WHAT ACTUALLY HAPPENED AND WHAT WENT WRONG], and how I am making it right is [YOUR RESOLUTION]. Write a reply that takes honest, full ownership without excuses, sincerely apologizes, explains briefly how I am fixing it and preventing it in future, and shows other readers I handle my mistakes with integrity. Do not be defensive or minimize it, since owning a real mistake gracefully builds more trust than pretending it did not happen. My brand voice is [BRAND VOICE]. Aim for sincere accountability.
description: Respond to a review about a real mistake with honest, graceful accountability.
useCase: Use this when a negative review is genuinely deserved. Taking honest full ownership, apologizing sincerely, and showing how you are fixing it builds more trust with readers than any defense could, since prospects respect a business that owns its mistakes gracefully far more than one that never admits fault.
variables: WHAT ACTUALLY HAPPENED AND WHAT WENT WRONG

title: Build a Complete FAQ Page
promptText: Build a complete FAQ page for my [BUSINESS TYPE] serving [TARGET AUDIENCE]. My offer is [YOUR OFFER], and the questions I already get asked are [QUESTIONS YOU GET, OR WRITE NOT SURE]. Help me create a full FAQ: the essential questions customers need answered before buying, organized into logical groups, with a clear helpful answer for each written in my voice, which is [BRAND VOICE]. Cover the practical, the trust related, and the objection questions. For each answer, keep it clear and reassuring, and where useful, guide them toward the next step. Flag any questions I should add that customers have but do not always voice.
description: Build a complete, well organized FAQ page that answers what customers need before buying.
useCase: Use this to create an FAQ page that removes buying friction. A well built FAQ answers the practical and trust questions that stall a purchase, reducing the questions landing in your inbox and reassuring prospects who would otherwise hesitate or leave without asking.
variables: QUESTIONS YOU GET, OR WRITE NOT SURE

title: Turn Real Customer Questions into an FAQ
promptText: Here are the actual questions customers keep asking me about my [BUSINESS TYPE]: [PASTE OR LIST THE QUESTIONS]. My offer is [YOUR OFFER] for [TARGET AUDIENCE]. Turn these into a polished FAQ: group related questions, rewrite each into a clear standard question format, write a helpful answer for each in my voice, which is [BRAND VOICE], and order them by how commonly they come up. Flag any duplicate or overlapping questions to merge. The goal is to answer the real recurring questions so I stop repeating myself. Note any question I answer often that reveals a gap in my messaging.
description: Convert the questions customers actually ask into a polished, organized FAQ.
useCase: Use this to build an FAQ from the questions you genuinely field. Answering the real recurring questions stops you repeating yourself and, because these are the questions customers actually have, it addresses their true concerns far better than an FAQ you guess at, while revealing gaps in your messaging.

title: Write a Clear FAQ Answer
promptText: Write a clear, helpful answer to this frequently asked question about my [BUSINESS TYPE]: [THE QUESTION]. The honest answer or relevant facts are [YOUR ANSWER OR CONTEXT], and my audience is [TARGET AUDIENCE]. Write an answer that is direct and easy to understand, addresses the real concern behind the question, is honest and reassuring, and where appropriate guides the reader toward the next step or resolves the hesitation. Keep it concise but complete. Match my voice, which is [BRAND VOICE]. Give me the answer, plus a shorter version for a compact FAQ if the full one runs long.
description: Write a clear, reassuring answer to a single frequently asked question.
useCase: Use this to write a strong answer to one specific FAQ. A clear answer that addresses the real concern behind the question and guides toward the next step resolves the hesitation that made the customer ask, turning a common question into a reason to trust and proceed rather than a moment of doubt.

title: Write FAQs That Handle Objections
promptText: Help me write FAQ entries that quietly handle the objections stopping people from buying my [YOUR OFFER], for [TARGET AUDIENCE]. The main hesitations and objections are [YOUR OBJECTIONS, E.G. PRICE, TRUST, TIME, OR IS IT RIGHT FOR ME]. For each, write it as a natural FAQ question a customer might ask, then an honest, reassuring answer that addresses the objection without being defensive or salesy. The answers should resolve doubt and move the reader toward buying, while feeling like genuine helpful information. Match my voice, which is [BRAND VOICE]. Give me the objection handling FAQ entries.
description: Write FAQ entries that resolve buying objections as helpful information.
useCase: Use this to turn your FAQ into a quiet conversion tool. Framing objections as natural questions with honest reassuring answers resolves the doubts that stop purchases, letting your FAQ handle hesitations before they become reasons to leave, all while reading as helpful information rather than a sales pitch.
variables: YOUR OBJECTIONS, E.G. PRICE, TRUST, TIME, OR IS IT RIGHT FOR ME

title: Write Pre Sale FAQs
promptText: Write pre sale FAQs for my [YOUR OFFER] aimed at [TARGET AUDIENCE] who are considering buying but not yet decided. Cover the questions a prospect needs answered to feel confident purchasing: what exactly they get, whether it is right for them, how it works, what results to expect, what happens after they buy, and any risk or guarantee. Write honest, confidence building answers in my voice, which is [BRAND VOICE], that remove uncertainty and make buying feel safe. These should reduce the questions that stall a sale. Give me the pre sale FAQ set, ordered by what a prospect wonders first.
description: Write pre sale FAQs that give prospects the confidence to buy.
useCase: Use this to answer the questions prospects have right before buying. Pre sale FAQs that clarify what they get, whether it fits them, and what happens next remove the uncertainty that stalls purchases, giving hesitant prospects the confidence to commit rather than leaving to think it over.

title: Write Post Purchase FAQs
promptText: Write post purchase FAQs for customers who just bought my [YOUR OFFER], to help them get started and reduce support questions. My audience is [TARGET AUDIENCE], and common early questions or stumbling points are [EARLY QUESTIONS OR STUMBLES, OR WRITE NOT SURE]. Cover: how to get started, what to expect, how to access or use what they bought, common early issues and fixes, and where to get help. Write clear reassuring answers in my voice, which is [BRAND VOICE], that make new customers feel supported and confident. These should prevent the repetitive early questions that eat my time. Give me the post purchase FAQ set.
description: Write post purchase FAQs that onboard customers and cut repetitive support questions.
useCase: Use this to support new customers and reduce onboarding questions. Post purchase FAQs that cover getting started and common early stumbles help customers succeed faster while cutting the repetitive questions that eat a solo operator's time, improving both the customer experience and your workload.
variables: EARLY QUESTIONS OR STUMBLES, OR WRITE NOT SURE

title: Find Missing Questions in Your FAQ
promptText: Here is my current FAQ for my [BUSINESS TYPE]: [PASTE YOUR FAQ]. My offer is [YOUR OFFER] for [TARGET AUDIENCE]. Help me find what is missing: the important questions customers likely have that my FAQ does not answer, the unspoken concerns that stop purchases but are not addressed, and any question my audience is afraid or embarrassed to ask that I should answer proactively. For each gap, give me the question to add and a note on why it matters. Also flag any existing answer that is unclear, incomplete, or raises more questions than it resolves. Help me make the FAQ genuinely complete.
description: Find the important missing questions and gaps in your existing FAQ.
useCase: Use this to complete an FAQ that has gaps. Surfacing the unaddressed concerns and unspoken questions that stall purchases, including the ones customers are afraid to ask, makes your FAQ genuinely useful, since the questions you fail to answer are often the exact ones costing you sales.

title: Adjust FAQ Answers to Your Voice
promptText: Here are my current FAQ answers for my [BUSINESS TYPE]: [PASTE YOUR FAQ ANSWERS]. They sound stiff, corporate, or off brand. Rewrite them to match my voice, which is [DESCRIBE YOUR VOICE OR PASTE A SAMPLE], while keeping every answer accurate and complete. Make them warmer and more human where appropriate, matching how I actually talk to customers, without losing clarity or professionalism. My audience is [TARGET AUDIENCE]. Show me the rewritten answers and note what changed so I can keep future FAQ answers consistent. Do not make them more formal than my real voice.
description: Rewrite stiff FAQ answers to match your authentic brand voice.
useCase: Use this when your FAQ reads as corporate or generic. Rewriting answers to match how you actually talk to customers keeps your FAQ consistent with your brand, since a stiff FAQ undercuts the personal feel that draws customers to a small business, especially at the moment they are deciding to trust you.
variables: DESCRIBE YOUR VOICE OR PASTE A SAMPLE

title: Optimize FAQs for Search
promptText: Help me optimize my FAQ for search so it can bring in traffic for my [BUSINESS TYPE]. My topic area is [YOUR TOPIC], my audience is [TARGET AUDIENCE], and here are my current or planned FAQ questions: [PASTE QUESTIONS]. Help me: phrase the questions the way people actually search for them, ensure the answers directly and completely address the query so they can win featured snippets, identify additional question based searches I could target with new FAQ entries, and structure the answers to be snippet friendly. Note which questions have the best search potential. Keep the answers genuinely helpful, not keyword stuffed. My tone is [TONE].
description: Optimize FAQ questions and answers to rank and win featured snippets.
useCase: Use this to turn your FAQ into a search traffic source. Phrasing questions as people search and answering them completely can win featured snippets and answer boxes, capturing the growing volume of question based searches and bringing new visitors to your site through content you needed anyway.

title: Write FAQs for a Specific Product
promptText: Write FAQs for my specific product [PRODUCT NAME], which is [WHAT IT IS] for [TARGET AUDIENCE]. Key details are [KEY DETAILS, E.G. FEATURES, SPECS, USE, PRICE, OR DELIVERY]. Cover the questions buyers have about this specific product: what it does and does not do, who it suits, how to use it, what is included, practical details like delivery or access, and common concerns. Write clear honest answers in my voice, which is [BRAND VOICE], that help buyers decide and reduce returns from mismatched expectations. Order them by what a buyer wonders first. Give me the product FAQ set.
description: Write product specific FAQs that help buyers decide and reduce mismatched returns.
useCase: Use this for a specific product page. Product FAQs that clarify what it does, who it suits, and the practical details help buyers choose correctly and reduce returns from mismatched expectations, since a buyer who fully understands a product before purchase is both more likely to buy and more likely to be satisfied.
variables: KEY DETAILS, E.G. FEATURES, SPECS, USE, PRICE, OR DELIVERY

title: Write FAQs for a Service
promptText: Write FAQs for my service [SERVICE NAME], which helps [TARGET AUDIENCE] with [WHAT IT DOES]. Key details are [HOW IT WORKS, TIMELINE, PRICING, AND PROCESS]. Cover the questions prospects have about a service like mine: how the process works, what is included and excluded, timelines, pricing and payment, what I need from them, what happens if things change, and how we work together. Write clear honest answers in my voice, which is [BRAND VOICE], that set expectations and build confidence to hire me. Setting clear expectations upfront also prevents scope disputes later. Give me the service FAQ set, ordered by what a prospect asks first.
description: Write service FAQs that set expectations and build confidence to hire you.
useCase: Use this for a service business FAQ. Answering how the process works, what is included, and what you need from clients builds the confidence to hire you and sets clear expectations that prevent scope disputes later, since much service friction comes from mismatched assumptions the FAQ can resolve upfront.
variables: HOW IT WORKS, TIMELINE, PRICING, AND PROCESS

title: Audit and Improve Your FAQ
promptText: Audit my current FAQ for my [BUSINESS TYPE]: [PASTE YOUR FAQ]. My audience is [TARGET AUDIENCE] and my offer is [YOUR OFFER]. Assess it honestly: which answers are unclear, incomplete, or raise more questions than they resolve, which are too long or too short, whether the questions are phrased the way customers actually ask, whether the order makes sense, and what important questions are missing. Then give me the prioritized improvements and rewrite the weakest answers. Be direct about what is not working. The goal is an FAQ that genuinely reduces confusion and supports sales, not one that just exists.
description: Audit an existing FAQ and get prioritized improvements for the weak parts.
useCase: Use this to improve an FAQ that exists but underperforms. An honest audit of unclear answers, poor ordering, and missing questions turns a box ticking FAQ into one that genuinely reduces confusion and supports sales, since a mediocre FAQ can create as many questions as it answers.

title: Organize Your FAQ into Sections
promptText: Help me organize my FAQ into clear sections for my [BUSINESS TYPE]. Here are my FAQ questions in no particular order: [PASTE YOUR QUESTIONS]. My audience is [TARGET AUDIENCE]. Group them into logical categories that make sense to a customer, such as getting started, pricing and payment, how it works, and support, order the sections and the questions within them by how customers would look for them, and suggest clear section headings. If the FAQ is long, suggest how to make it navigable. The goal is a customer can quickly find their question rather than scrolling a wall of text. Give me the organized structure.
description: Organize a jumble of FAQ questions into clear, navigable sections.
useCase: Use this when your FAQ has grown into a hard to scan wall of questions. Grouping them into logical sections customers can navigate lets people find their answer fast, which is the entire point of an FAQ, since a disorganized FAQ frustrates rather than helps and sends people to your inbox anyway.

title: Make a Long FAQ Easy to Navigate
promptText: My FAQ for my [BUSINESS TYPE] has grown long and is hard to navigate. Here it is: [PASTE YOUR FAQ]. Help me make it easy to use: suggest how to structure it with sections and a way for people to jump to what they need, which questions are most common and should be featured or placed first, whether any questions should move to other pages, and how to keep it scannable. My audience is [TARGET AUDIENCE]. Give me a plan to make finding an answer quick and painless, even with many questions. Keep the guidance practical for a simple website.
description: Make a long FAQ scannable and easy to navigate to the right answer fast.
useCase: Use this when your FAQ has too many questions to scan easily. Structuring it so people can jump to what they need and featuring the most common questions keeps a large FAQ usable, since the value of an FAQ collapses if customers cannot quickly find their specific answer among dozens of others.

title: Build FAQs to Reduce Your Support Load
promptText: Help me build FAQs specifically to reduce the repetitive questions eating my time in my [BUSINESS TYPE]. The questions I answer over and over are [THE REPETITIVE QUESTIONS]. My audience is [TARGET AUDIENCE]. For each, write a clear complete FAQ answer in my voice, which is [BRAND VOICE], that fully resolves it so people stop needing to ask me directly. Also suggest where to place or link these answers so customers actually find them before contacting me, such as on key pages or in confirmation emails. The goal is to answer once, publicly, and reclaim my time. Give me the answers and the placement plan.
description: Build and place FAQs to eliminate the repetitive questions eating your time.
useCase: Use this to reclaim time lost to answering the same questions repeatedly. Writing complete public answers to your most repetitive questions and placing them where customers find them lets you answer once instead of endlessly, freeing a solo operator's time while still giving customers fast help.

title: Decide Where to Place Your FAQs
promptText: Help me decide where to place FAQs across my [BUSINESS TYPE] so customers find answers at the right moment. My key pages and touchpoints are [YOUR PAGES AND TOUCHPOINTS, E.G. HOMEPAGE, SALES PAGE, CHECKOUT, EMAILS]. My main FAQ topics are [YOUR FAQ TOPICS]. Help me map which questions belong where: which FAQs to put on the sales page to close a sale, which at checkout to remove last minute doubt, which in onboarding emails, and which on a dedicated FAQ page. Explain how placing the right answer at the right moment beats burying everything on one page. Give me a placement plan.
description: Map which FAQs belong on which pages so answers appear at the right moment.
useCase: Use this to place FAQs where they do the most good rather than burying them all on one page. Putting the right answer at the decision moment, like objection FAQs on the sales page and reassurance at checkout, removes doubt exactly when it would otherwise cost you the sale, which a separate FAQ page never reaches.
variables: YOUR PAGES AND TOUCHPOINTS, E.G. HOMEPAGE, SALES PAGE, CHECKOUT, EMAILS

title: Answer a Difficult or Sensitive FAQ
promptText: Help me answer a difficult or sensitive question customers ask about my [BUSINESS TYPE]: [THE DIFFICULT QUESTION]. This is awkward because [WHY IT IS HARD, E.G. IT TOUCHES ON PRICE, A LIMITATION, OR A COMMON CRITICISM], and the honest reality is [THE TRUTH OR CONTEXT]. Write an FAQ answer that is honest and transparent without being defensive or damaging, addresses the concern directly rather than dodging it, and turns a potentially negative question into a trust building answer where possible. Avoid corporate evasion, since a straight honest answer builds more trust than a slippery one. My voice is [BRAND VOICE]. Give me the answer.
description: Answer a difficult or sensitive FAQ honestly in a way that builds trust.
useCase: Use this for the awkward questions about price, limitations, or criticisms that you would rather avoid. Answering them honestly and directly builds more trust than dodging, since customers notice evasion, and a straight answer to a hard question often reassures more than a comfortable answer to an easy one.
variables: WHY IT IS HARD, E.G. IT TOUCHES ON PRICE, A LIMITATION, OR A COMMON CRITICISM

title: Write FAQs in a Conversational Style
promptText: Rewrite my FAQ for my [BUSINESS TYPE] in a warm conversational style rather than stiff corporate Q and A. Here are my questions and current answers: [PASTE YOUR FAQ]. My audience is [TARGET AUDIENCE], and my brand is [BRAND PERSONALITY, E.G. FRIENDLY, PLAYFUL, OR APPROACHABLE]. Rewrite so the questions sound like a real person asking and the answers sound like me genuinely helping, warm and human while still clear and complete. Keep all the accurate information intact. Show me the conversational version and note how the shift changes the feel. Do not sacrifice clarity for personality. Give me the rewritten FAQ.
description: Rewrite a stiff FAQ into a warm, conversational style that fits your brand.
useCase: Use this when your FAQ feels cold next to your friendly brand. A conversational FAQ where questions sound like real people and answers sound like you genuinely helping strengthens the personal connection customers came for, since the FAQ is often read at a decisive moment where warmth reassures and stiffness distances.
variables: BRAND PERSONALITY, E.G. FRIENDLY, PLAYFUL, OR APPROACHABLE

title: Anticipate Questions You Are Not Getting Asked
promptText: Help me anticipate the questions my customers have but are not asking me directly, for my [BUSINESS TYPE] serving [TARGET AUDIENCE]. My offer is [YOUR OFFER]. Think from the customer's perspective and surface: the concerns they wonder about privately but never voice, the questions they are too embarrassed or unsure to ask, the doubts that silently stop them from buying, and the things they wish they knew but do not think to ask. For each, give me the question and why it matters. These unspoken questions, answered proactively in my FAQ, could remove hidden barriers to buying. Give me the list.
description: Surface the unspoken questions customers wonder but never ask you directly.
useCase: Use this to address the silent doubts that never reach your inbox. Surfacing the questions customers wonder privately but are too unsure or embarrassed to ask, then answering them proactively, removes hidden barriers to buying, since the most damaging concerns are often the ones a customer never voices before quietly leaving.

title: Update and Refresh an Outdated FAQ
promptText: Help me update my outdated FAQ for my [BUSINESS TYPE]. Here it is: [PASTE YOUR FAQ]. What has changed in my business since I wrote it is [WHAT CHANGED, E.G. NEW OFFERS, PRICING, PROCESS, OR POLICIES]. Help me: identify answers that are now inaccurate or outdated, update them to reflect current reality, spot questions that no longer apply and should be removed, and add new questions that arise from the changes. My audience is [TARGET AUDIENCE]. Flag anything where an outdated answer could actively mislead or frustrate a customer. Give me the refreshed FAQ and a summary of what changed.
description: Update an outdated FAQ so it reflects your current offers, pricing, and process.
useCase: Use this when your business has changed but your FAQ has not. An outdated FAQ that lists old pricing, offers, or policies actively misleads and frustrates customers, so refreshing it to match current reality prevents the confusion and broken trust that come from answers contradicting how your business now works.
variables: WHAT CHANGED, E.G. NEW OFFERS, PRICING, PROCESS, OR POLICIES

title: Build FAQs from Support Messages
promptText: Here are recent support messages, emails, or DMs from my customers for my [BUSINESS TYPE]: [PASTE MESSAGES OR SUMMARIES]. My audience is [TARGET AUDIENCE]. Mine them to build an FAQ: identify the recurring questions and issues, group similar ones, phrase each as a clear FAQ question, and write a helpful answer in my voice, which is [BRAND VOICE]. Prioritize the questions that come up most or cause the most confusion. Also flag any recurring issue that points to a deeper problem I should fix rather than just answer. Turn my support history into a preventive FAQ. Give me the FAQ set and the flagged issues.
description: Turn recurring support messages into a preventive FAQ that stops repeat questions.
useCase: Use this to build an FAQ grounded in the real questions flooding your support. Mining actual support messages surfaces exactly what confuses customers, and answering those proactively prevents the repeat questions while flagging recurring issues that signal a deeper problem worth fixing rather than just explaining again.

title: Write FAQs That Guide Toward Purchase
promptText: Help me write FAQs that gently guide interested prospects toward buying my [YOUR OFFER], for [TARGET AUDIENCE]. Beyond just informing, I want each answer to resolve the concern and nudge the reader forward. For common pre purchase questions, write answers in my voice, which is [BRAND VOICE], that fully answer the question honestly, then where natural include a soft next step such as pointing to the offer, suggesting the right option for them, or reassuring them it is safe to proceed. Keep it helpful first, never pushy. The FAQ should feel like a helpful guide that happens to move people toward a decision. Give me the purchase guiding FAQ set.
description: Write FAQs that resolve concerns and gently guide prospects toward a purchase.
useCase: Use this to make your FAQ actively support sales, not just inform. Answers that fully resolve a concern then include a soft next step guide interested prospects toward a decision, turning the FAQ from a passive reference into a helpful guide that moves people forward at the moment they are weighing whether to buy.

title: Generate a Quick FAQ Answer Fast
promptText: I need a quick FAQ answer for my [BUSINESS TYPE]. The question is [THE QUESTION], and the key facts are [YOUR ANSWER OR CONTEXT]. Give me a clear, concise, helpful answer ready to use, written in a [TONE] tone for [TARGET AUDIENCE]. Keep it complete but brief, address the real concern, and make it ready to paste into my FAQ with minimal editing. Just give me a solid ready to use answer.
description: Generate a clear, ready to use FAQ answer quickly with minimal effort.
useCase: Use this when you just need one good FAQ answer fast. A quick ready to use answer lets you add to or update your FAQ as new questions arise without a big writing effort, keeping your FAQ current and complete over time rather than letting gaps accumulate because updating it feels like a chore.

title: Write FAQs for a New Offer or Launch
promptText: Help me write FAQs for my new offer [YOUR OFFER] that I am launching to [TARGET AUDIENCE]. Since it is new, prospects have no reviews or track record to rely on, so the FAQ must build confidence. Cover: what exactly the offer is and how it works, who it is right for and who it is not, what results or experience to expect, how it compares to alternatives, the practical details, and the risk or guarantee. Write reassuring honest answers in my voice, which is [BRAND VOICE], that reduce the extra hesitation people feel about something new and unproven. Give me the launch FAQ set, ordered by what a cautious prospect asks first.
description: Write launch FAQs that build confidence in a new, unproven offer.
useCase: Use this when launching a new offer with no track record yet. FAQs that clearly explain what it is, who it fits, and what to expect build the confidence that reviews and history would normally provide, reducing the extra hesitation people feel about something new and unproven, which is often the biggest barrier at launch.

title: Respond to a Customer Complaint
promptText: Write a response to this customer complaint for my [BUSINESS TYPE]: [PASTE THE COMPLAINT]. The issue is [THE ISSUE], and my side or context is [YOUR CONTEXT]. Write a reply that acknowledges their frustration genuinely, takes appropriate responsibility without over apologizing or getting defensive, addresses the specific problem, and offers a clear path to resolution. Keep it warm, professional, and human. The customer should feel heard and confident I will make it right. My brand voice is [BRAND VOICE].
