Give me one version that leans warmer and one that is more matter of fact.
description: Write a warm, professional response that makes a complaining customer feel heard.
useCase: Use this when a customer raises a complaint directly. A response that acknowledges frustration and offers a clear resolution turns a moment of tension into a chance to build loyalty, since how you handle a complaint often matters more to the relationship than the problem itself.

title: De Escalate an Angry Customer
promptText: Write a reply to de escalate this angry customer message for my [BUSINESS TYPE]: [PASTE THE MESSAGE]. They are upset about [THE ISSUE]. Write a response that lowers the temperature: lead with genuine empathy, stay completely calm and never match their tone, validate the emotion without necessarily agreeing with every claim, and move toward a concrete next step. The goal is to defuse the anger and show I am on their side in solving this. Do not be defensive or dismissive. My brand voice is [BRAND VOICE]. Give me a measured, human response that calms the situation.
description: Write a calm, empathetic reply that de escalates an angry customer.
useCase: Use this when a customer contacts you angry. Leading with empathy and staying calm defuses the emotion so you can actually solve the problem, since matching their frustration or getting defensive escalates a recoverable situation into a lost customer or a public complaint.

title: Respond to a Refund Request
promptText: Write a response to this refund request for my [BUSINESS TYPE]: [PASTE THE REQUEST]. The reason they want a refund is [THE REASON], my refund policy is [YOUR POLICY], and my decision is [APPROVE, DECLINE, OR OFFER AN ALTERNATIVE]. Write a reply that is gracious and professional regardless of the outcome, clearly communicates my decision and the reasoning, stays warm even if declining, and where possible preserves the relationship and leaves the door open. Avoid sounding rigid or bureaucratic. My brand voice is [BRAND VOICE]. Give me the response for my chosen decision.
description: Respond to a refund request graciously whether you approve, decline, or offer an alternative.
useCase: Use this when handling any refund request. A gracious response that clearly communicates your decision preserves goodwill regardless of the outcome, since how you handle a refund, even a declined one, shapes whether the customer leaves as a quiet detractor or a respectful former customer who might return.
variables: APPROVE, DECLINE, OR OFFER AN ALTERNATIVE

title: Write a Refund Approval Message
promptText: Write a message approving a refund for a customer of my [BUSINESS TYPE]. The refund is for [WHAT AND HOW MUCH], and the reason is [THE REASON]. Write a message that grants the refund graciously and without friction, confirms the details and timing they can expect, avoids making them feel bad for asking, and leaves the relationship warm so they might return or still speak well of me. Where genuine, keep a door open for the future without being pushy. My brand voice is [BRAND VOICE]. Give me a message that turns a refund into a positive final impression.
description: Write a gracious refund approval that leaves a positive final impression.
useCase: Use this when approving a refund. Granting it graciously without making the customer feel bad turns a potentially negative moment into a positive final impression, since a smoothly handled refund often earns more goodwill and future business than a grudging one that leaves the customer feeling like a problem.

title: Decline a Refund Gracefully
promptText: Write a message declining a refund request for my [BUSINESS TYPE], while keeping the relationship as intact as possible. The request is for [WHAT], the reason it does not qualify is [WHY IT DOES NOT QUALIFY UNDER YOUR POLICY], and any alternative I can offer is [ALTERNATIVE OR WRITE NONE]. Write a reply that declines clearly but kindly, explains the reasoning without hiding behind policy coldly, acknowledges their disappointment, and offers any alternative or goodwill gesture that might help. Stay firm but human, since a graceless decline can trigger a public complaint. My brand voice is [BRAND VOICE]. Give me the response.
description: Decline a refund clearly but kindly while protecting the relationship.
useCase: Use this when you must decline a refund. A clear but kind decline that explains the reasoning and offers an alternative holds your position while limiting the damage, since a cold policy driven refusal often provokes the angry public complaint that costs far more than the refund would have.
variables: WHY IT DOES NOT QUALIFY UNDER YOUR POLICY

title: Offer a Resolution to a Complaint
promptText: Help me craft a resolution to offer this complaining customer of my [BUSINESS TYPE]: [PASTE THE COMPLAINT]. The issue is [THE ISSUE], and what I am able to offer is [YOUR OPTIONS, E.G. A FIX, A REPLACEMENT, A PARTIAL REFUND, A CREDIT, OR AN APOLOGY]. Help me present a resolution that genuinely addresses their problem, feels fair or even generous to them, and is sustainable for my business. Write the message offering the resolution warmly and confidently, framing it as me wanting to make things right. Give me the resolution message plus a note on how to decide which option fits when I have several. My brand voice is [BRAND VOICE].
description: Craft and present a fair resolution that genuinely addresses a complaint.
useCase: Use this when a complaint needs a concrete resolution, not just words. Offering a fair fix framed as wanting to make things right resolves the problem and rebuilds trust, since a well handled resolution can create more loyalty than if the issue had never occurred, turning a complaint into a retention win.
variables: YOUR OPTIONS, E.G. A FIX, A REPLACEMENT, A PARTIAL REFUND, A CREDIT, OR AN APOLOGY

title: Write a Genuine Apology
promptText: Write a genuine apology to a customer of my [BUSINESS TYPE] for [WHAT WENT WRONG]. The impact on them was [THE IMPACT], and how I am making it right is [YOUR RESOLUTION]. Write an apology that takes real responsibility without excuses or a fake sounding sorry you feel that way, acknowledges the specific impact on them, explains briefly how I am fixing it and preventing a repeat, and feels sincere rather than corporate. Avoid over apologizing or groveling, which undermines confidence. My brand voice is [BRAND VOICE]. Give me an apology that rebuilds trust through genuine accountability.
description: Write a sincere apology that takes real responsibility and rebuilds trust.
useCase: Use this when you genuinely got something wrong. A real apology that owns the specific impact and shows how you are fixing it rebuilds trust, since customers forgive mistakes handled with genuine accountability but resent the hollow non apologies that dodge responsibility while pretending to accept it.

title: Plan a Service Recovery
promptText: Help me plan a full service recovery for a customer of my [BUSINESS TYPE] after a significant problem. What went wrong was [WHAT HAPPENED], the customer is [HOW THEY FEEL OR WHAT THEY WANT], and my constraints are [WHAT YOU CAN AND CANNOT DO]. Help me plan the recovery: the immediate response to stabilize the situation, the resolution that addresses their problem, any goodwill gesture that turns the experience around, and the follow up to confirm they are satisfied. The goal is not just to fix the issue but to recover the relationship. Give me the step by step recovery plan and the key messages. My brand voice is [BRAND VOICE].
description: Plan a full service recovery that fixes the problem and recovers the relationship.
useCase: Use this after a serious problem that risks losing a customer. A structured recovery that stabilizes, resolves, and follows up can turn an upset customer into a loyal one, since customers whose problems are recovered well often become more loyal than those who never had an issue, a pattern worth deliberately pursuing.
variables: HOW THEY FEEL OR WHAT THEY WANT
WHAT YOU CAN AND CANNOT DO

title: Set a Boundary with a Difficult Customer
promptText: Help me respond to a customer of my [BUSINESS TYPE] who is being unreasonable, abusive, or demanding something I cannot provide. The situation is [DESCRIBE THE SITUATION], and what they are demanding is [WHAT THEY WANT]. Write a response that stays professional and calm, holds a firm boundary about what I can and cannot do, does not reward abusive behavior or cave to unreasonable demands, and remains respectful while protecting myself and my business. Include how to disengage if they continue past a reasonable point. I want to be fair but not a doormat. My brand voice is [BRAND VOICE]. Give me the boundaried response.
description: Respond to an unreasonable or abusive customer with a firm, professional boundary.
useCase: Use this when a customer is being abusive or demanding the impossible. A calm firm boundary protects you and your business without escalating, since caving to unreasonable demands or matching hostility both make things worse, and knowing how to hold a professional line, and when to disengage, preserves your wellbeing and standards.

title: Follow Up After Resolving a Complaint
promptText: Write a follow up message to a customer of my [BUSINESS TYPE] after I resolved their complaint. The original issue was [THE ISSUE], and how I resolved it was [YOUR RESOLUTION]. Write a message that checks they are now genuinely satisfied, confirms the problem is fully behind us, thanks them for their patience and for giving me the chance to fix it, and reinforces that I value them. This should close the loop warmly and, if they are happy, leave them feeling better about my business than before. Do not reopen the issue unnecessarily. My brand voice is [BRAND VOICE]. Give me the follow up message.
description: Follow up after resolving a complaint to confirm satisfaction and close the loop warmly.
useCase: Use this after resolving a complaint. A follow up that confirms satisfaction and thanks them for the chance to fix it closes the loop and often leaves the customer feeling better than before the problem, since the thoughtful extra touch signals genuine care that most businesses skip once the immediate issue is handled.

title: Win Back a Refunded or Lost Customer
promptText: Help me win back a customer of my [BUSINESS TYPE] who requested a refund or left unhappy. Why they left was [THE REASON], and what has changed or what I can offer to bring them back is [WHAT CHANGED OR YOUR OFFER]. Write a genuine reconnection message that acknowledges their past experience honestly, shows what has improved or what I can do differently, and invites them back without pressure or desperation. Keep the focus on genuinely serving them better, not just recovering the sale. Only reach out if there is a real reason to. My brand voice is [BRAND VOICE]. Give me the win back message.
description: Write a genuine message to win back a refunded or unhappy former customer.
useCase: Use this to reconnect with a customer who left unhappy once things have genuinely improved. A sincere message acknowledging their experience and showing real change can recover a relationship that seemed lost, since some unhappy customers will return when they see genuine improvement and feel their earlier concern was actually heard.

title: Respond to a Chargeback or Payment Dispute
promptText: Help me respond to a customer of my [BUSINESS TYPE] who has filed or is threatening a chargeback or payment dispute. The situation is [DESCRIBE THE SITUATION], the customer's claim is [THEIR CLAIM], and the facts are [WHAT ACTUALLY HAPPENED]. Help me: write a calm professional message to the customer aiming to resolve it directly and avoid the chargeback if possible, and separately organize the factual account and evidence I would need if the dispute proceeds. Keep the customer message non confrontational since escalation rarely helps. Note this is general guidance, not legal or payment processor advice. My brand voice is [BRAND VOICE]. Give me both the customer message and the evidence outline.
description: Respond to a chargeback threat calmly while organizing your factual case.
useCase: Use this when facing a chargeback or payment dispute. A calm message aiming to resolve it directly often prevents the chargeback entirely, which is far better than fighting one, and organizing your factual account prepares you in case it proceeds, all while avoiding the escalation that hostility invites.

title: Respond to a Complaint About Your Genuine Mistake
promptText: Write a response to a customer of my [BUSINESS TYPE] complaining about something that was genuinely my fault. What I got wrong was [WHAT YOU DID WRONG], the impact was [THE IMPACT ON THEM], and how I am making it right is [YOUR RESOLUTION]. Write a reply that owns the mistake fully and quickly without excuses, apologizes sincerely, makes the resolution generous enough to match the error, and reassures them it will not happen again. Owning a real mistake gracefully builds more trust than deflecting. Avoid defensiveness or minimizing. My brand voice is [BRAND VOICE]. Give me the accountable response.
description: Respond to a complaint about your genuine mistake with full accountability.
useCase: Use this when a complaint is genuinely your fault. Owning the mistake fully and making a resolution that matches the error rebuilds trust faster than any defense, since customers respect a business that takes real accountability far more than one that minimizes or deflects, often becoming more loyal after a well handled failure.

title: Handle a Complaint That Is Not Your Fault
promptText: Write a response to a customer of my [BUSINESS TYPE] complaining about something that is not actually my fault, such as their own error, a misunderstanding, or a third party. The complaint is [PASTE OR DESCRIBE IT], and the real cause is [THE ACTUAL CAUSE]. Write a reply that stays empathetic and helpful without accepting blame that is not mine, gently clarifies what actually happened without making them feel stupid, and focuses on helping them move forward or resolve it. Avoid being defensive or making them wrong, since being right matters less than keeping the relationship. My brand voice is [BRAND VOICE]. Give me the response.
description: Handle a complaint that is not your fault with empathy and without accepting blame.
useCase: Use this when a complaint stems from the customer's error or a misunderstanding. Staying empathetic and helpful while gently clarifying what happened resolves it without making them feel stupid or accepting blame you do not own, since winning the argument matters far less than keeping the customer and avoiding a public grievance.

title: Offer a Partial Refund or Credit
promptText: Help me offer a partial refund or credit as a middle ground for a customer of my [BUSINESS TYPE]. The situation is [THE SITUATION], a full refund is not warranted because [WHY], but I want to acknowledge their concern with [PARTIAL REFUND OR CREDIT AMOUNT OR OFFER]. Write a message that frames the partial resolution as a fair and genuine gesture of goodwill, not a grudging compromise, explains it clearly, and aims to leave them satisfied rather than shortchanged. Position it as me valuing the relationship. My brand voice is [BRAND VOICE]. Give me the message offering the partial resolution.
description: Offer a partial refund or credit framed as a genuine goodwill gesture.
useCase: Use this when a full refund is not warranted but the customer deserves acknowledgment. A partial refund or credit framed as genuine goodwill, rather than a grudging compromise, resolves the tension fairly, keeping the customer satisfied and the relationship intact while protecting your business from giving away more than the situation truly calls for.
variables: PARTIAL REFUND OR CREDIT AMOUNT OR OFFER

title: Analyze Recurring Complaints for Root Causes
promptText: Help me analyze recurring complaints and refund reasons for my [BUSINESS TYPE] to fix the root causes. Here are the complaints and refund reasons I have been getting: [PASTE OR LIST THEM]. My offer is [YOUR OFFER] for [TARGET AUDIENCE]. Help me: identify the recurring themes and patterns, distinguish surface complaints from the underlying root problems, pinpoint what in my offer, process, messaging, or expectations is generating them, and recommend specific fixes prioritized by impact. The goal is to stop the complaints at the source rather than just handling each one. Give me the analysis and the fix list.
description: Analyze recurring complaints and refunds to find and fix their root causes.
useCase: Use this when the same complaints or refund reasons keep recurring. Finding the root cause, whether in your offer, process, or the expectations your messaging sets, lets you stop problems at the source, which is far more valuable than skillfully handling the same complaint over and over while it keeps costing you customers.

title: Prevent Complaints Before They Happen
promptText: Help me prevent common complaints before they happen in my [BUSINESS TYPE]. The complaints I most want to prevent are [YOUR COMMON COMPLAINTS]. My offer is [YOUR OFFER] and my process is [BRIEF DESCRIPTION OF YOUR PROCESS]. Help me: identify where in the customer journey each complaint originates, what expectation gaps or friction points cause them, and the proactive steps to prevent them, such as clearer expectations upfront, better onboarding, or process changes. Focus on the highest leverage prevention moves. The goal is fewer complaints reaching me at all. Give me the prevention plan mapped to each complaint. My audience is [TARGET AUDIENCE].
description: Build a plan to prevent common complaints before they reach you.
useCase: Use this to reduce complaints at the source rather than just handling them. Identifying the expectation gaps and friction points that cause complaints, then closing them proactively, means fewer problems reach you at all, freeing your time and improving the experience, since the best complaint handling is preventing the complaint from arising.

title: Write a Clear Refund Policy
promptText: Help me write a clear refund policy for my [BUSINESS TYPE] offering [YOUR OFFER] to [TARGET AUDIENCE]. What I am willing to offer is [YOUR REFUND TERMS, E.G. TIMEFRAME, CONDITIONS, EXCLUSIONS]. Write a policy that is clear and easy to understand, fair to both me and the customer, protects me from abuse while building buyer confidence, and is written in plain friendly language rather than cold legalese. Explain any conditions in a way that feels reasonable, not restrictive. Note this is general business guidance, not legal advice, and I should have anything binding reviewed. My brand voice is [BRAND VOICE]. Give me the refund policy.
description: Write a clear, fair refund policy that builds confidence and prevents abuse.
useCase: Use this to set a refund policy that works for both sides. A clear policy in plain language builds buyer confidence, since a fair visible refund policy actually increases sales by reducing risk, while well defined conditions protect you from abuse, replacing the ambiguity that causes disputes with terms everyone understands upfront.
variables: YOUR REFUND TERMS, E.G. TIMEFRAME, CONDITIONS, EXCLUSIONS

title: Set Expectations to Prevent Complaints
promptText: Help me write clear expectation setting communication for new customers of my [BUSINESS TYPE] to prevent complaints from mismatched expectations. My offer is [YOUR OFFER], and the areas where customers often misunderstand or expect more than I deliver are [WHERE MISMATCHES HAPPEN]. Write messaging, for onboarding or the point of purchase, that clearly and honestly sets expectations about what they will get, what to expect and when, what is and is not included, and how things work, in a warm confident way that reassures rather than lists caveats defensively. Setting honest expectations upfront prevents most complaints. My brand voice is [BRAND VOICE]. Give me the expectation setting message.
description: Write expectation setting communication that prevents complaints before they start.
useCase: Use this to prevent the complaints that come from mismatched expectations, one of the most common causes. Setting honest clear expectations at purchase or onboarding, about what customers get and when, heads off the disappointment that fuels complaints and refunds, since much dissatisfaction is really a gap between what was expected and what was delivered.

title: Build a Complaint Response Template Bank
promptText: Build me a bank of complaint response templates for my [BUSINESS TYPE] so I can respond quickly and consistently while staying personal. My brand voice is [BRAND VOICE] and my audience is [TARGET AUDIENCE]. Create adaptable templates for the common situations: a general complaint, an angry customer, a refund request I will approve, a refund request I will decline, a genuine mistake on my part, and an issue that is not my fault. Each should have a warm on brand structure with fill in the blank spots to personalize, not a canned script. Include notes on what to customize each time. Give me the template bank.
description: Build adaptable complaint and refund response templates that stay personal.
useCase: Use this once to handle complaints faster without sounding robotic. A bank of adaptable templates for each situation lets you respond quickly and consistently even when stressed or busy, which matters since complaints often arrive at inconvenient moments and a fast, calm, personalized response beats a slow or emotional one every time.

title: Get a Quick Complaint Response Fast
promptText: I need to respond to this customer complaint quickly for my [BUSINESS TYPE]: [PASTE THE COMPLAINT]. The issue is [THE ISSUE] and what I can offer is [YOUR RESOLUTION OR WRITE STILL DECIDING]. Give me a calm, professional, empathetic response ready to send with minimal editing, that acknowledges their concern, addresses the issue, and offers a path forward. Keep it warm and human, not corporate. My brand voice is [BRAND VOICE]. Just give me a solid ready to use response I can send now.
description: Get a calm, ready to send complaint response quickly when you need it fast.
useCase: Use this when a complaint needs a good response right away. A calm ready to send reply helps you respond promptly and professionally even under time pressure or emotion, since a fast composed response prevents a complaint from festering, and quick acknowledgment alone often defuses much of a customer's frustration.
variables: YOUR RESOLUTION OR WRITE STILL DECIDING

title: Respond to a Complaint Made Publicly
promptText: Help me respond to a complaint a customer made publicly about my [BUSINESS TYPE], such as on social media or in a comment: [PASTE THE PUBLIC COMPLAINT]. The issue is [THE ISSUE] and my context is [YOUR CONTEXT]. Write a public reply that responds with grace and composure knowing others are watching, acknowledges their concern genuinely, avoids arguing or airing details publicly, and invites them to continue privately to resolve it. The public response should reassure onlookers that I handle problems well. Give me the public reply plus a private message to send if they engage. My brand voice is [BRAND VOICE].
description: Respond to a public complaint with composure and move resolution offline.
useCase: Use this when a complaint is made publicly where others can see. A composed public reply that acknowledges the concern and moves resolution offline protects your reputation with the onlookers who matter most, since how you handle a public complaint signals to every potential customer watching how you would treat them too.

title: Decide When to Let a Customer Go
promptText: Help me decide whether to part ways with a difficult customer of my [BUSINESS TYPE] and how to do it professionally if so. The situation is [DESCRIBE THE SITUATION], and the pattern of behavior is [WHAT KEEPS HAPPENING]. Help me: honestly assess whether this customer is worth keeping given the cost to my time, energy, and wellbeing, distinguish a difficult but salvageable customer from one I should release, and if releasing them is right, write a professional respectful message to end the relationship cleanly, including any refund or transition. Firing a bad customer can be the healthiest business decision. My brand voice is [BRAND VOICE]. Give me the assessment and, if warranted, the message.
description: Decide whether to part ways with a difficult customer and do it professionally.
useCase: Use this when a customer costs more in stress and time than they are worth. Honestly assessing whether to keep or release them, and ending it professionally if warranted, protects your wellbeing and business, since keeping a genuinely toxic customer drains energy from good ones and firing a bad customer is sometimes the healthiest decision you can make.

title: Respond to an Emotionally Charged Complaint
promptText: Help me respond to a customer of my [BUSINESS TYPE] whose complaint is emotionally charged because the issue affected them deeply. The situation is [DESCRIBE THE SITUATION], and why it matters so much to them is [WHY IT IS EMOTIONAL FOR THEM]. Write a response that leads with real human empathy for what they are feeling, takes their emotion seriously rather than treating it as an overreaction, addresses both the practical issue and the emotional impact, and makes them feel genuinely cared for as a person. The emotional acknowledgment matters as much as the fix here. My brand voice is [BRAND VOICE]. Give me a deeply empathetic response.
description: Respond to an emotionally charged complaint with genuine human empathy.
useCase: Use this when a complaint carries real emotional weight because the issue mattered deeply to the customer. Leading with genuine empathy and taking their feelings seriously, alongside fixing the problem, makes them feel cared for as a person, since in emotionally charged moments the acknowledgment of their feelings often matters as much as the practical resolution.
variables: WHY IT IS EMOTIONAL FOR THEM
